cases.title: Toddler Pulling at His Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report ear tugging, fussiness, and fever for one day.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for one day of ear tugging on the right, irritability, and decreased appetite. He had clear rhinorrhea and a mild cough for the preceding five days that seemed to be improving until last night, when he developed a tactile fever and woke crying twice. He attends daycare. He is otherwise healthy, born full-term, growing along the 50th percentile, and his immunizations are up to date including pneumococcal conjugate vaccine. He takes no daily medications. He has no known drug allergies and has never had an ear infection before. He lives with both parents and an older sibling; his father smokes outside the home. There is no family history of immunodeficiency or recurrent ear disease. He is not bottle-fed and has not had any ear surgery. His mother gave one dose of weight-appropriate acetaminophen this morning with partial relief of fussiness, and she is asking whether he needs an antibiotic.
cases.exam: Alert but fussy, consolable in mother's lap, well hydrated with moist mucous membranes. Right tympanic membrane is bulging, erythematous, and opaque with loss of the normal light reflex and poor mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. The left tympanic membrane is pearly gray, mobile, and normal. No mastoid tenderness, swelling, or auricular protrusion. Oropharynx mildly erythematous without exudate; scant clear nasal discharge. Neck supple with small mobile anterior cervical nodes. Lungs clear, no increased work of breathing. No rash. Cardiovascular exam normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding most strongly supports a diagnosis of acute otitis media rather than otitis media with effusion?
options: Erythema of the tympanic membrane alone
A bulging tympanic membrane with impaired mobility
Clear rhinorrhea and a mildly erythematous oropharynx
Small mobile anterior cervical lymph nodes
stages.explanation: A bulging tympanic membrane with reduced mobility on pneumatic otoscopy is the single most specific sign of acute otitis media (AOM) and reflects middle-ear inflammation under pressure. Erythema alone is nonspecific because crying or fever can flush the drum, so guidelines require it be accompanied by bulging or otorrhea. Rhinorrhea and pharyngeal erythema indicate a concurrent viral URI, which often precedes AOM but does not establish it. Reactive cervical nodes are a nonspecific response to upper respiratory infection. Otitis media with effusion shows fluid without the bulging, marked erythema, or acute symptoms seen here, so the bulging drum distinguishes true AOM.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his age and presentation, which management approach is most appropriate?
options: Observation with analgesia and follow-up in 48–72 hours, antibiotics if no improvement
Immediate high-dose amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided twice daily
Oral azithromycin for five days
Topical ofloxacin otic drops alone
stages.explanation: This child is younger than 24 months would automatically favor antibiotics, but at 3 years with a clearly bulging drum, unilateral disease, and moderate symptoms, guidelines permit either observation or antibiotics; however, with documented fever of 38.4°C and a confidently bulging, erythematous membrane, high-dose amoxicillin is the recommended first-line agent and best answer. Observation is an option for nonsevere unilateral AOM in children 6–23 months or older with mild symptoms, but firm bulging plus fever justifies treatment here. Azithromycin is reserved for penicillin allergy and has higher pneumococcal resistance. Ofloxacin drops treat otitis externa or tympanostomy-tube otorrhea, not intact-membrane AOM.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His mother says he had a non-urticarial rash with amoxicillin as an infant. Which oral antibiotic is the most appropriate alternative?
options: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
Clindamycin
Cefdinir
Ciprofloxacin
stages.explanation: For a non-severe, non-IgE-mediated penicillin reaction such as a delayed maculopapular rash, a second- or third-generation cephalosporin like cefdinir is appropriate because cross-reactivity with modern cephalosporins is very low and they retain good activity against the major AOM pathogens. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole has poor activity against Streptococcus pneumoniae and group A streptococcus and is not recommended for AOM. Clindamycin lacks reliable coverage of Haemophilus influenzae and Moraxella catarrhalis and is reserved for resistant pneumococcus. Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone, avoided in routine pediatric AOM and inadequate against pneumococcus.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which counseling point about prevention and follow-up is most appropriate for this family?
options: He should receive prophylactic daily antibiotics through the winter
Eliminating household tobacco smoke exposure reduces his risk of recurrent ear infections
He needs an audiogram now because permanent hearing loss is expected
A repeat office visit is mandatory in all children once symptoms resolve
stages.explanation: Secondhand smoke is a well-established modifiable risk factor for recurrent AOM, so counseling the smoking father to eliminate the child's exposure is high-yield and appropriate. Prophylactic antibiotics are no longer recommended because of resistance concerns and marginal benefit. An urgent audiogram is unnecessary for a first uncomplicated AOM; transient conductive loss resolves as effusion clears, and testing is considered only with persistent effusion beyond three months or language concerns. Routine recheck of every resolved AOM is not required for older children who improve; follow-up is targeted to persistent symptoms, young infants, or recurrent disease.

cases.diagnosis: Acute otitis media (right), uncomplicated, following a viral upper respiratory infection
pearls: Diagnose AOM by a bulging tympanic membrane (or new otorrhea) plus acute symptoms—erythema alone is insufficient.
High-dose amoxicillin (80–90 mg/kg/day) is first-line; reserve observation for nonsevere unilateral disease with reliable follow-up.
For mild, delayed penicillin rashes use a cephalosporin such as cefdinir; avoid TMP-SMX and macrolides for empiric AOM.
Eliminating secondhand smoke and maintaining pneumococcal vaccination reduce recurrent AOM.

cases.title: Sore Throat and Fever in a Schoolgirl
cases.chiefComplaint: A 9-year-old girl presents with two days of sore throat and fever.
cases.history: A 9-year-old girl is brought in by her father for two days of progressively painful swallowing and subjective fever to 38.6°C measured at home. She has had decreased oral intake and missed school today. She denies cough, runny nose, hoarseness, or red eyes. A classmate was recently diagnosed with strep throat. She has no rash, abdominal pain, or vomiting, though she mentions her neck feels sore. Her past medical history is unremarkable; she has no prior episodes of rheumatic fever and her immunizations are current. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She lives with both parents and a younger brother and has no recent travel. There is no family history of rheumatic heart disease or autoimmune disease. Her father gave ibuprofen this morning with modest relief and is worried she needs an antibiotic because her throat 'looks really red.' On review of systems she has no joint pain, chest pain, or shortness of breath.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, in no distress, speaking in full sentences without drooling or muffled voice. Oropharynx shows erythematous tonsils with patchy white tonsillar exudate and no asymmetry or uvular deviation. Tender, enlarged anterior cervical lymph nodes bilaterally. No conjunctival injection, no nasal congestion, no cough during the visit. No scarlatiniform rash, no strawberry tongue. Lungs clear; heart regular without murmur. Abdomen soft, no hepatosplenomegaly. No joint swelling or tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Using the Centor criteria (with McIsaac age modification), which feature in this patient does NOT contribute a point toward group A streptococcal pharyngitis?
options: Absence of cough
Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy
Tonsillar exudate
Patchy white discharge on the tonsils
stages.explanation: The Centor/McIsaac criteria award points for fever >38°C, absence of cough, tender anterior cervical adenopathy, tonsillar swelling/exudate, and age 3–14 years. Those options each correspond to a distinct criterion. 'Patchy white discharge on the tonsils' is simply a restatement of the same tonsillar-exudate criterion already counted—it does not add a separate point, making it the correct choice. Recognizing that exudate is scored once prevents double-counting and over-estimating the probability of streptococcal infection. Her score (age 4–14, fever, no cough, tender nodes, exudate) is high, which appropriately prompts confirmatory testing rather than treating each finding as independent.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis before prescribing antibiotics?
options: Treat empirically with penicillin without testing given the high Centor score
Perform a rapid antigen detection test, with throat culture backup if negative
Order a monospot (heterophile antibody) test
Obtain an antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer
stages.explanation: In children, a rapid antigen detection test (RADT) should confirm group A strep before antibiotics, and because RADT sensitivity is imperfect, a negative result in a child is backed up by throat culture—the gold standard in this age group given their higher strep prevalence and rheumatic fever risk. Empiric treatment without testing leads to overprescribing, the very problem this case targets, since clinical features overlap with viral pharyngitis. A monospot screens for infectious mononucleosis, not strep, and is insensitive early and in young children. ASO titers rise over 1–3 weeks and reflect past infection, useful only retrospectively for rheumatic fever, not acute diagnosis.

stages.label: TEST RESULTS
stages.content:
Rapid antigen detection test (group A strep): POSITIVE
Throat culture: not required (RADT positive)
Rapid influenza A/B: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The rapid strep test is positive. Which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic for this child with no drug allergies?
options: Oral penicillin V (or amoxicillin) for 10 days
Azithromycin for 5 days
Levofloxacin for 7 days
Topical benzydamine and no antibiotics
stages.explanation: Penicillin V or amoxicillin for a full 10-day course is first-line for group A streptococcal pharyngitis; amoxicillin is often preferred in children for palatability and once- or twice-daily dosing, and the 10-day duration is required to eradicate the organism and prevent acute rheumatic fever. Azithromycin is reserved for true penicillin allergy and faces rising macrolide resistance. Levofloxacin is an unnecessarily broad fluoroquinolone, inappropriate in a child. No antibiotics would be correct only for confirmed viral pharyngitis; here the positive RADT establishes strep, where antibiotics shorten symptoms, reduce transmission, and most importantly prevent rheumatic fever.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Her father asks when she can return to school. What is the most appropriate guidance?
options: She may return once she has completed the full 10-day course
She may return after 24 hours of antibiotics if she is afebrile and feeling better
She should remain home until a negative repeat throat culture is documented
She may return immediately because strep is not contagious once treatment begins
stages.explanation: Children with strep pharyngitis are considered noncontagious after about 24 hours of appropriate antibiotics, so she may return to school once she has had a full day of therapy and is afebrile and improving—this is the standard public-health recommendation. Waiting the entire 10 days is unnecessary and keeps her out far longer than warranted. A negative 'test of cure' culture is not recommended for uncomplicated cases and would needlessly detect chronic carriers. Returning immediately is wrong because she remains contagious until roughly 24 hours of antibiotics have been given and fever has resolved.

pearls: Apply Centor/McIsaac criteria to decide who needs testing; treating on clinical impression alone drives antibiotic overuse.
In children, confirm a negative rapid antigen test with throat culture because of higher strep prevalence and rheumatic fever risk.
Penicillin or amoxicillin for 10 full days is first-line and prevents acute rheumatic fever.
Patients are noncontagious and may return to school after ~24 hours of antibiotics if afebrile.

cases.title: Cough and Fever in an Older Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: A 62-year-old man presents with four days of productive cough and fever.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents with four days of cough productive of rust-colored sputum, fevers to 38.7°C, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, and increasing fatigue. He felt a mild cold a week ago that worsened rather than resolved. He reports mild breathlessness climbing stairs but none at rest. His past medical history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and a 30-pack-year smoking history; he still smokes half a pack daily. He has no known lung disease, no recent hospitalization, and no immunosuppression. He had a pneumococcal vaccine several years ago and an influenza shot last fall. He has no drug allergies. He drinks alcohol socially and lives independently with his wife. There is no family history of lung cancer. He denies hemoptysis, weight loss, night sweats, sick contacts with tuberculosis, or recent travel. He has been taking acetaminophen for fever with minimal relief, and his appetite is reduced but he is drinking fluids and urinating normally.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, mildly ill-appearing but conversant in full sentences. Respiratory exam reveals dullness to percussion, bronchial breath sounds, and crackles over the right lower lung field with increased tactile fremitus and egophony in that region; the left lung is clear. No accessory muscle use. Heart regular without murmur, no jugular venous distention, no peripheral edema. Mucous membranes slightly dry but capillary refill brisk. Abdomen benign. No calf swelling or tenderness.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best supports a diagnosis of lobar community-acquired pneumonia in this patient?
options: Diffuse expiratory wheezing with prolonged expiration
Focal crackles, dullness to percussion, bronchial breath sounds, and egophony
Bilateral fine crackles with elevated jugular venous pressure and edema
A normal lung exam with isolated pleuritic pain
stages.explanation: Focal crackles, dullness to percussion, bronchial breath sounds, increased fremitus, and egophony together indicate lobar consolidation, the classic exam of community-acquired pneumonia, fitting his rust-colored sputum and fever. Diffuse wheezing with prolonged expiration suggests obstructive disease such as COPD or asthma exacerbation rather than consolidation. Bilateral crackles with elevated JVP and edema point to cardiogenic pulmonary edema/heart failure, which his normal JVP and absent edema argue against. A normal lung exam with only pleuritic pain would raise concern for pulmonary embolism, but his consolidative findings and productive febrile illness make pneumonia far more likely.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.6 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL
Na 138 mEq/L
BUN 16 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Chest X-ray: right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms, no effusion

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Using CURB-65, which assessment of this patient's disposition is most appropriate?
options: CURB-65 of 0–1, suitable for outpatient oral antibiotics
CURB-65 of 3, requiring ICU admission
CURB-65 cannot be calculated without an arterial blood gas
Admit regardless of score because all smokers with pneumonia require hospitalization
stages.explanation: CURB-65 assigns one point each for Confusion, BUN >19 mg/dL (>7 mmol/L), Respiratory rate ≥30, low Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60), and age ≥65. This man is alert, BUN is 16, RR is 22, BP is 128/78, and he is 62—scoring 0, indicating low mortality and suitability for outpatient management. A score of 3 is incorrect because none of the criteria are met. CURB-65 needs no arterial blood gas; it uses routine clinical data and BUN. Smoking alone is not an admission criterion; disposition is risk-stratified, and he can be safely treated at home with close follow-up.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has comorbid hypertension but no recent antibiotics and no MRSA/Pseudomonas risk factors. Which outpatient regimen is most appropriate?
options: Azithromycin monotherapy
Amoxicillin 1 g three times daily alone
Amoxicillin-clavulanate plus azithromycin (or respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy)
Oral vancomycin
stages.explanation: For outpatients with comorbidities such as hypertension (and a smoking history), current guidelines recommend combination therapy with a beta-lactam plus a macrolide—e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate plus azithromycin—or monotherapy with a respiratory fluoroquinolone like levofloxacin to cover Streptococcus pneumoniae, atypicals, and beta-lactamase-producing organisms. Azithromycin monotherapy is discouraged because of high pneumococcal macrolide resistance, reserved only for healthy patients without comorbidities in low-resistance areas. Amoxicillin alone lacks atypical and some H. influenzae coverage for a patient with comorbidities. Oral vancomycin is not absorbed systemically and treats C. difficile colitis, not pneumonia.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Beyond antibiotics, which intervention will most reduce his risk of future pneumonia and respiratory complications?
options: Daily prophylactic azithromycin
Smoking cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy
Routine follow-up chest CT in all patients after pneumonia
Lifelong inhaled corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Smoking is a major modifiable risk factor for pneumonia and impairs mucociliary clearance and immune defense, so cessation counseling with pharmacotherapy (such as varenicline or nicotine replacement) provides the greatest long-term benefit and is an essential part of his care. Prophylactic azithromycin is reserved for select chronic conditions like frequent COPD exacerbations and is not indicated here. Routine follow-up CT for everyone is not standard; repeat imaging is considered selectively, such as in older smokers with non-resolving infiltrates to exclude malignancy, but not universally. Lifelong inhaled corticosteroids treat asthma/COPD and have no role in uncomplicated CAP.

cases.diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia (right lower lobe), low severity
pearls: Consolidative signs—dullness, bronchial breath sounds, egophony, increased fremitus—indicate lobar pneumonia.
CURB-65 (Confusion, BUN >19, RR ≥30, low BP, age ≥65) guides outpatient versus inpatient disposition; 0–1 is usually outpatient.
Outpatients with comorbidities need a beta-lactam plus macrolide or a respiratory fluoroquinolone, not azithromycin alone.
Smoking cessation is a key intervention to prevent recurrent pneumonia; consider follow-up imaging in older smokers to exclude obstructing malignancy.

cases.title: Burning with Urination
cases.chiefComplaint: A 27-year-old woman presents with two days of painful, frequent urination.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman presents with two days of burning on urination, urinary frequency, and a sensation of urgency. She describes lower suprapubic discomfort but no flank pain. She denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, or vaginal discharge or pruritus. She is sexually active with one partner and uses combined oral contraceptives; her last menstrual period was two weeks ago and she has no possibility of pregnancy concerns clinically, but pregnancy will be considered. She has had one prior uncomplicated urinary tract infection two years ago that resolved with antibiotics. She has no diabetes, no kidney stones, no recent instrumentation or catheterization, and no immunosuppression. She takes no chronic medications other than her contraceptive pill and has a documented allergy to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (rash). She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. There is no relevant family history. She has been drinking cranberry juice without improvement and wants to feel better quickly.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-appearing, afebrile. Abdomen soft with mild suprapubic tenderness to deep palpation and no rebound or guarding. No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. No flank mass. No vaginal discharge appreciated externally; pelvic exam deferred given classic lower-tract symptoms and absence of vaginal complaints. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. No rash, no lower-extremity edema.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best classifies this as acute uncomplicated cystitis rather than a complicated UTI or pyelonephritis?
options: Suprapubic tenderness on examination
Dysuria, frequency, and urgency without fever, flank pain, or CVA tenderness in a healthy nonpregnant woman
Her history of a prior urinary tract infection
Use of combined oral contraceptives
stages.explanation: Acute uncomplicated cystitis is defined by lower urinary tract symptoms—dysuria, frequency, urgency—in a healthy, nonpregnant, non-immunocompromised woman with a structurally and functionally normal urinary tract and no signs of upper-tract or systemic infection. The absence of fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness rules out pyelonephritis, and lack of complicating factors keeps it uncomplicated. Suprapubic tenderness supports cystitis but does not distinguish complicated from uncomplicated disease. A single prior UTI does not make an infection complicated. Oral contraceptives are not a complicating factor; complicating factors include pregnancy, diabetes, obstruction, catheters, immunosuppression, or male sex.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach in this classic presentation?
options: Obtain urine culture and sensitivities and withhold treatment until results return
Diagnose clinically (with or without a urine dipstick) and treat empirically without culture
Order renal ultrasound to evaluate the urinary tract
Perform CT urography before any antibiotics
stages.explanation: In a healthy nonpregnant woman with classic cystitis symptoms, the diagnosis can be made clinically, often supported by a dipstick showing leukocyte esterase and/or nitrites, and empiric antibiotics started without a culture because the causative organisms and susceptibilities are predictable. Waiting for culture before treating needlessly delays relief; cultures are reserved for recurrent, complicated, or treatment-failure cases. Renal ultrasound and CT urography are imaging studies for suspected obstruction, stones, or complicated/recurrent infection, not for a first-line uncomplicated cystitis, and they expose her to cost and radiation without benefit.

stages.label: URINALYSIS
stages.content:
Urine dipstick: leukocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive
Microscopy: 25 WBC/hpf, few bacteria, no casts
Urine hCG: negative
Urine culture: not sent (uncomplicated case)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Considering her sulfa allergy, which empiric first-line antibiotic is most appropriate?
options: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 3 days
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days
Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 7 days
stages.explanation: Nitrofurantoin for 5 days is a recommended first-line agent for acute uncomplicated cystitis with excellent efficacy, low collateral resistance, and—importantly—no cross-reactivity with her sulfonamide allergy. TMP-SMX is contraindicated given her documented sulfa rash and is otherwise a first-line option only where local resistance is below ~20%. Fluoroquinolones like ciprofloxacin are effective but reserved for more serious infections because of resistance and serious adverse effects (tendinopathy, neuropathy, QT effects); they are not first-line for simple cystitis. Amoxicillin has high E. coli resistance and poor efficacy, so it is not recommended for empiric cystitis.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which counseling point is most appropriate at discharge?
options: Return promptly if she develops fever, flank pain, nausea, or vomiting
She should take prophylactic antibiotics indefinitely after this episode
A test-of-cure urine culture is required for all women after treatment
She must stop her oral contraceptives because they caused the infection
stages.explanation: She should be counseled that new fever, flank pain, nausea, or vomiting suggests progression to pyelonephritis and warrants prompt reevaluation—the key safety-netting instruction for outpatient cystitis. Indefinite prophylaxis is not indicated for a second lifetime UTI; prophylaxis is considered only for frequent recurrences (e.g., ≥3 per year). Routine test-of-cure cultures are unnecessary when symptoms resolve in uncomplicated cystitis. Oral contraceptives do not cause UTIs and should not be stopped; nitrofurantoin does not reduce their efficacy, so no contraceptive changes are needed.

pearls: Classic cystitis in a healthy nonpregnant woman can be diagnosed clinically and treated empirically without culture.
Nitrofurantoin, fosfomycin, and (where resistance is low) TMP-SMX are first-line; reserve fluoroquinolones for more serious infections.
Choose nitrofurantoin when there is a sulfa allergy—there is no cross-reactivity.
Counsel patients to return for fever or flank pain, which signal possible pyelonephritis.

cases.title: High Numbers at a Routine Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: A 54-year-old man is seen for elevated blood pressure readings.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man presents for follow-up after an automated pharmacy kiosk and a prior nurse visit both recorded elevated blood pressures around 150/95 mmHg. He feels well and is asymptomatic, denying headaches, chest pain, palpitations, visual changes, or shortness of breath. He has no known kidney disease and no prior cardiac events. His past medical history is notable only for borderline cholesterol. He takes no prescription medications but uses occasional ibuprofen for knee pain and a daily multivitamin. He has no drug allergies. He drinks two to three beers most evenings, eats a high-sodium diet with frequent takeout, and is sedentary at a desk job. He smoked in his twenties but quit twenty years ago. His father had hypertension and a stroke at 70, and his mother has type 2 diabetes. He does not snore loudly or report daytime sleepiness. He is concerned about starting 'pills for life' and asks whether the readings might just be stress.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, overweight (BMI 31 kg/m²), in no distress. Repeat blood pressure after five minutes of quiet sitting with proper cuff size in the contralateral arm is 150/94 mmHg. No carotid bruits. Heart regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no S3 or murmurs, point of maximal impulse non-displaced. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft without bruits or masses. No peripheral edema. Fundoscopic exam without hemorrhages or papilledema. Distal pulses intact and symmetric.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on guideline thresholds, how should his blood pressure be classified and confirmed?
options: Diagnose stage 2 hypertension immediately and start two drugs today
Confirm with repeated office readings and/or out-of-office monitoring before labeling hypertension, as his readings are in the stage 2 range
Reassure him this is normal blood pressure and recheck in one year
This is a hypertensive emergency requiring the emergency department
stages.explanation: His readings (~150/95 mmHg) fall in the stage 2 range (≥140/90), but a diagnosis of hypertension should be confirmed with multiple properly measured readings, ideally including out-of-office monitoring (home or ambulatory) to exclude white-coat effect—so this is the answer. Immediately committing to two drugs skips confirmation and overlooks that lifestyle plus a single agent may be reasonable. Calling this normal is wrong because readings clearly exceed 130/80. A hypertensive emergency requires severe elevation (often >180/120) with acute target-organ damage, which he lacks—he is asymptomatic with a benign exam and normal fundi.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
Home BP average over 7 days: 148/93 mmHg
Basic metabolic panel: Na 140, K 4.1, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
Fasting glucose 98 mg/dL, A1c 5.6%
Lipid panel: LDL 138 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no proteinuria
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no LVH

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His home readings confirm sustained hypertension. Beyond pharmacologic therapy, which lifestyle change offers the greatest blood-pressure-lowering benefit for him?
options: Switching from beer to red wine
Adopting the DASH diet with sodium restriction, weight loss, reduced alcohol, and regular exercise
Taking ibuprofen daily for its cardiovascular benefits
Adding a daily multivitamin with potassium
stages.explanation: The DASH dietary pattern combined with sodium reduction, weight loss, limiting alcohol, and aerobic exercise produces clinically meaningful blood-pressure reductions and addresses all of his modifiable risks, so this is the answer and foundational alongside medication. Substituting red wine does not lower blood pressure; reducing total alcohol does, and he currently exceeds recommended limits. Daily ibuprofen actually raises blood pressure and blunts antihypertensives, so NSAIDs should be minimized. A potassium-containing multivitamin is not a substitute for dietary potassium from the DASH diet and can be hazardous if renal function declines or he later takes ACE inhibitors.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no compelling indications (no diabetes, CKD, or heart failure). Which is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive choice?
options: A thiazide-type diuretic such as chlorthalidone (or another first-line agent: ACE inhibitor/ARB or dihydropyridine CCB)
An alpha-blocker such as doxazosin
A beta-blocker such as atenolol
Hydralazine plus isosorbide dinitrate
stages.explanation: For primary (essential) hypertension without compelling indications, first-line agents are a thiazide-type diuretic (chlorthalidone or hydrochlorothiazide), an ACE inhibitor or ARB, or a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker; chlorthalidone is a well-supported thiazide-type choice, matching this case's angle, so this is the answer. Alpha-blockers are not first-line and increased heart-failure events in trials. Beta-blockers are reserved for compelling indications like coronary disease or heart failure, not initial monotherapy for uncomplicated hypertension. Hydralazine/isosorbide dinitrate is used in specific heart-failure populations, not as initial therapy for essential hypertension.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After initiating a thiazide and lifestyle changes, what is the most appropriate monitoring and follow-up plan?
options: No follow-up needed unless he develops symptoms
Recheck blood pressure in about one month and obtain basic metabolic panel to assess potassium and renal function
Repeat an ECG weekly
Increase to three medications immediately to reach goal faster
stages.explanation: After starting therapy, reassessing blood pressure in roughly 2–4 weeks (about a month) and checking electrolytes and renal function detects thiazide-induced hypokalemia or rising creatinine and confirms response. No follow-up is unsafe because hypertension is asymptomatic yet progressive and medications need titration and monitoring. Weekly ECGs are unnecessary in stable, asymptomatic hypertension. Jumping straight to triple therapy is inappropriate before assessing the response to a single agent plus lifestyle change; escalation is guided by follow-up readings toward a goal generally below 130/80 mmHg.

pearls: Confirm hypertension with repeated, properly measured readings and out-of-office monitoring to exclude white-coat effect.
Lifestyle change—DASH diet, sodium restriction, weight loss, alcohol moderation, exercise—is foundational and lowers BP substantially.
First-line agents without compelling indications are thiazide-type diuretics, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, or dihydropyridine CCBs.
After starting a thiazide, recheck BP and a metabolic panel in ~1 month to monitor potassium and renal function.

cases.title: An Unexpected Lab Result
cases.chiefComplaint: A 49-year-old woman returns to discuss an abnormal blood sugar result.
cases.history: A 49-year-old woman presents to review labs drawn at an annual physical, where a hemoglobin A1c came back at 8.1%. She feels generally well but on questioning admits to mild fatigue, occasional nighttime urination, and increased thirst over the past few months that she attributed to summer heat. She has not had blurry vision, numbness, foot ulcers, or recurrent infections. Her past medical history includes obesity and a pregnancy complicated by gestational diabetes 15 years ago. She takes no medications other than a daily vitamin D supplement and has no drug allergies. She works long hours, eats out frequently, and is sedentary. She does not smoke and drinks wine on weekends. Her mother has type 2 diabetes and her father had a myocardial infarction at 60. She is anxious about needing insulin and is motivated to avoid it. Her weight has crept up about 15 pounds over two years.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, obese (BMI 33 kg/m²) woman in no distress. Acanthosis nigricans noted at the posterior neck. Cardiovascular exam normal without murmurs; lungs clear. Abdomen soft, obese, nontender, no organomegaly. Distal pulses intact. Monofilament testing of both feet shows intact protective sensation; no ulcers, deformities, or skin breakdown. Fundoscopic exam without retinopathy. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her A1c is 8.1% and she has classic symptoms. What is the most appropriate way to establish the diagnosis of diabetes?
options: A single A1c of 8.1% with classic hyperglycemic symptoms is sufficient for diagnosis
Two separate abnormal tests are always required even with symptoms
An oral glucose tolerance test is mandatory before any diagnosis
Fasting C-peptide and autoantibodies are required first
stages.explanation: Diabetes can be diagnosed with a single unequivocally elevated value when the patient has classic symptoms of hyperglycemia—polyuria, polydipsia—as this woman does; her A1c of 8.1% (≥6.5%) plus symptoms confirms diabetes, so this is the answer. Requiring two abnormal tests applies only to asymptomatic patients, where confirmation with a repeat test is needed. A glucose tolerance test is one diagnostic option but is not mandatory; A1c, fasting glucose, or random glucose with symptoms all suffice. Autoantibodies and C-peptide help distinguish type 1 from type 2 in ambiguous cases but are not required here, where the picture—obesity, family history, gestational diabetes, acanthosis—is classic type 2.

stages.label: BASELINE LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin A1c: 8.1%
Fasting glucose: 168 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio: 12 mg/g (normal)
LDL 142 mg/dL, HDL 44 mg/dL, triglycerides 190 mg/dL
ALT 30 U/L

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has no contraindications and normal renal function. Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy?
options: Basal insulin glargine at bedtime
Metformin titrated up with meals
A sulfonylurea such as glipizide
Empagliflozin as monotherapy first-line for everyone
stages.explanation: Metformin remains the preferred first-line pharmacotherapy for most patients with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes; it lowers A1c effectively, is weight-neutral to weight-favorable, does not cause hypoglycemia, and is inexpensive—ideal for this woman who fears insulin. Basal insulin is reserved for very high A1c (often >10%), marked symptoms, or catabolic features, none of which she has at 8.1%. Sulfonylureas cause weight gain and hypoglycemia and are not preferred initial agents. SGLT2 inhibitors like empagliflozin are excellent add-ons or first-line when there is established cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease, but metformin is the standard initial choice when those compelling indications are absent.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Alongside metformin, which lifestyle counseling target offers the greatest impact on her glycemic control?
options: Eliminating all carbohydrates permanently from her diet
A structured plan for 5–10% weight loss through diet and 150 minutes/week of moderate activity
Switching wine to hard liquor on weekends
Checking fasting fingerstick glucose ten times daily
stages.explanation: Modest weight loss of 5–10% combined with about 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity substantially improves insulin sensitivity, glycemia, and cardiovascular risk, and is the cornerstone of type 2 diabetes management, so this is the answer. Eliminating all carbohydrates is neither necessary nor sustainable; balanced carbohydrate-controlled eating is recommended. Switching alcohol type confers no benefit; reducing overall alcohol is more useful. Very frequent fingerstick testing is unnecessary for a patient not on insulin or sulfonylureas and adds burden without improving outcomes; structured self-monitoring is tailored to the regimen.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which additional measure is most appropriate as part of comprehensive diabetes care at this visit?
options: Begin a high-intensity statin and arrange retinal screening and annual foot exams
Defer all cardiovascular risk reduction until the A1c normalizes
Start daily aspirin for primary prevention in everyone with diabetes
Order a coronary angiogram to screen for asymptomatic disease
stages.explanation: Comprehensive diabetes care includes cardiovascular risk reduction and complication screening: a 49-year-old with diabetes and LDL 142 warrants a statin (moderate-to-high intensity given her age and risk), plus a baseline dilated retinal exam and annual comprehensive foot exams—so this is the answer. Deferring risk reduction until the A1c normalizes wastes the highest-yield interventions; statins act independently of glucose. Routine aspirin for primary prevention is no longer recommended for all diabetics because bleeding can outweigh benefit; it is individualized by risk. A screening coronary angiogram is invasive and not indicated in an asymptomatic patient.

pearls: A single A1c ≥6.5% with classic hyperglycemic symptoms confirms diabetes; asymptomatic patients need two abnormal tests.
Metformin is first-line for most new type 2 diabetes; insulin is reserved for very high A1c or catabolic symptoms.
Target 5–10% weight loss and ~150 min/week of activity—lifestyle change is the foundation of management.
Comprehensive care adds statin therapy and screening for retinopathy, nephropathy, and neuropathy.

cases.title: Sneezing Every Spring
cases.chiefComplaint: A 22-year-old man presents with weeks of sneezing, nasal congestion, and itchy eyes.
cases.history: A 22-year-old college student presents with three weeks of bouts of sneezing, watery rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itchy, watery eyes that worsen outdoors and in the mornings. He notes an itchy palate and postnasal drip but no facial pain, purulent discharge, or fever. He says this happens 'every spring' and resolves by summer. He has a history of mild eczema as a child and seasonal symptoms since his teens. He takes no daily medications, has used over-the-counter oral diphenhydramine occasionally but stopped because it made him drowsy before exams. He has no drug allergies. He does not smoke but lives in a dorm with a roommate's cat on weekends. His mother has asthma and his father has hay fever. He denies wheezing, chest tightness, or shortness of breath, though he occasionally coughs at night during pollen season. He wants something that works without making him sleepy.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young man, breathing comfortably through a congested nose. Nasal mucosa is pale, boggy, and edematous with clear watery secretions; bilateral inferior turbinate swelling. Transverse nasal crease present. Conjunctivae mildly injected and watery without purulent discharge; infraorbital darkening ('allergic shiners'). Oropharynx with cobblestoning of the posterior wall and clear postnasal drainage; no tonsillar exudate. Tympanic membranes normal. Lungs clear to auscultation without wheezes. No sinus tenderness to palpation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which constellation of findings best distinguishes allergic rhinitis from acute bacterial sinusitis or a viral upper respiratory infection?
options: Purulent nasal discharge with facial pain and fever
Itchy eyes and palate, sneezing, clear rhinorrhea, pale boggy turbinates, and a seasonal pattern
Sudden high fever with myalgias and sore throat
Unilateral facial pain with maxillary tooth pain lasting over 10 days
stages.explanation: Allergic rhinitis is characterized by itching (eyes, nose, palate), paroxysmal sneezing, clear watery rhinorrhea, nasal congestion with pale boggy edematous turbinates, and a recurrent seasonal pattern with associated atopy—exactly this patient's picture—so this is the answer. Purulent discharge with facial pain and fever and unilateral facial/tooth pain persisting beyond 10 days describe bacterial sinusitis. Abrupt high fever with myalgias and sore throat suggests influenza or a viral URI. The hallmark distinguishing feature here is pruritus plus the seasonal, atopic context, which are absent in infectious causes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He wants effective relief without sedation. Which is the most appropriate first-line therapy for his persistent, bothersome symptoms?
options: Oral first-generation antihistamine (diphenhydramine) scheduled daily
An intranasal corticosteroid such as fluticasone used daily
Oral decongestant (pseudoephedrine) indefinitely
A short course of oral prednisone
stages.explanation: Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective single agent for moderate-to-persistent allergic rhinitis, reducing congestion, sneezing, rhinorrhea, and ocular symptoms with minimal systemic effect and no sedation, making fluticasone the best first-line choice. First-generation antihistamines like diphenhydramine cause exactly the drowsiness he wants to avoid; if an oral antihistamine is used, a second-generation nonsedating agent is preferred. Chronic oral decongestants raise blood pressure and cause rebound congestion and insomnia, so they are not for long-term use. Oral prednisone is reserved for severe refractory cases because of systemic steroid toxicity and is inappropriate as initial therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After two weeks of correctly used intranasal fluticasone he has improved but still has breakthrough sneezing and itchy eyes. What is the best next step in stepwise therapy?
options: Stop the nasal steroid and switch entirely to oral decongestants
Add a second-generation oral or intranasal antihistamine (and consider an antihistamine eye drop)
Begin allergen immunotherapy as the immediate next step
Start a leukotriene antagonist as monotherapy in place of all other agents
stages.explanation: When an intranasal corticosteroid provides partial relief, the next step is to add a second-generation (nonsedating) antihistamine—oral or intranasal—and an antihistamine/mast-cell-stabilizer eye drop for the ocular itching, building combination therapy on top of the steroid. Stopping the steroid for decongestants abandons the most effective agent and risks rebound. Allergen immunotherapy is effective but reserved for patients with inadequate response to optimized pharmacotherapy or who prefer disease modification; it is not the immediate next step after only partial improvement. A leukotriene antagonist as sole therapy is less effective than intranasal steroids and would be a step down, not up.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which additional counseling point is most appropriate for this patient?
options: Allergen avoidance measures, such as limiting outdoor exposure on high-pollen days and managing cat exposure, complement medication
He should take antibiotics at the first sign of symptoms each season
Nasal steroids work instantly, so he can use them only on symptomatic days
He must avoid all exercise during pollen season
stages.explanation: Allergen avoidance—monitoring pollen counts, keeping windows closed, showering after outdoor exposure, and reducing contact with the roommate's cat—reduces the allergic burden and complements pharmacotherapy, so this is the answer. Antibiotics have no role in allergic rhinitis, which is not infectious, and reinforce inappropriate antibiotic use. Intranasal steroids take days to reach full effect and are most effective with regular daily use during the season rather than as-needed dosing. Avoiding all exercise is unnecessary and counterproductive; he can stay active while timing outdoor activity to lower-pollen periods and pretreating with his medications.

pearls: Itching, sneezing, clear rhinorrhea, pale boggy turbinates, and a seasonal/atopic pattern point to allergic rhinitis over infection.
Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective first-line therapy and are non-sedating.
Step up by adding a second-generation antihistamine and antihistamine eye drops; reserve immunotherapy for refractory cases.
Allergen avoidance complements medication; antibiotics and chronic oral decongestants have no role.

cases.title: Burning Chest After Meals
cases.chiefComplaint: A 45-year-old man presents with several months of burning chest discomfort after eating.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents with three months of retrosternal burning that occurs after large or fatty meals and when lying down at night, sometimes with a sour taste in his mouth and occasional regurgitation. Symptoms are worse after late dinners and alcohol and are partially relieved by over-the-counter antacids. He denies difficulty or pain with swallowing, food sticking, vomiting, black or bloody stools, or unintentional weight loss. He has no exertional chest pain, dyspnea, or diaphoresis, and symptoms are clearly tied to meals and position rather than activity. His past medical history is unremarkable; he takes only occasional antacids and no other medications, with no drug allergies. He has a BMI of 30, drinks coffee daily and alcohol several nights weekly, eats late, and quit smoking last year. He works as an accountant under deadline stress. Family history is notable for a father with peptic ulcer disease. He is worried because a coworker his age was recently diagnosed with esophageal cancer.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, overweight man in no acute distress. Oropharynx normal without erythema. Neck supple with no lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly. Cardiovascular exam normal: regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no reproducible chest-wall tenderness. Lungs clear bilaterally. Abdomen soft with mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, no rebound, guarding, masses, or hepatosplenomegaly. No melena on rectal exam. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before attributing his symptoms to reflux, which alarm feature, if present, would most warrant prompt endoscopy?
options: Symptom relief with antacids
Progressive dysphagia or unintentional weight loss
Symptoms worse when lying flat
A sour taste after fatty meals
stages.explanation: Alarm features that mandate prompt endoscopy include dysphagia, odynophagia, unintentional weight loss, GI bleeding or anemia, recurrent vomiting, or a mass—because they may indicate malignancy, stricture, or complicated disease. Progressive dysphagia and weight loss are classic red flags, so their presence would warrant endoscopy. Relief with antacids, positional worsening, and a sour taste after fatty meals are typical, reassuring features of uncomplicated GERD rather than alarm signs. This patient fortunately has none of the alarm features, which is what permits an empiric treatment approach instead of upfront endoscopy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given classic symptoms and no alarm features, what is the most appropriate initial management strategy?
options: Empiric trial of a once-daily proton pump inhibitor plus lifestyle modification
Upper endoscopy for all patients with heartburn
24-hour esophageal pH monitoring before any treatment
Barium swallow as the first diagnostic test
stages.explanation: In a patient with classic GERD symptoms and no alarm features, an empiric 8-week trial of a once-daily proton pump inhibitor together with lifestyle modification is both diagnostic and therapeutic—response supports the diagnosis—so this is the answer. Endoscopy for everyone is unnecessary and not cost-effective in the absence of alarm features or treatment failure. Ambulatory pH monitoring is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, refractory symptoms, or pre-surgical evaluation, not as an initial test. A barium swallow has limited sensitivity for reflux and is not the first-line study; it is mainly used to assess structural or motility abnormalities.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which lifestyle recommendations are most appropriate to recommend alongside the PPI trial?
options: Eat large meals late at night to buffer acid before sleep
Weight loss, avoiding meals within 3 hours of lying down, elevating the head of the bed, and reducing alcohol and trigger foods
Lie down immediately after meals to aid digestion
Increase coffee and carbonated beverage intake
stages.explanation: Evidence-supported lifestyle measures for GERD include weight loss (he is overweight), avoiding food within about three hours of recumbency, elevating the head of the bed, limiting alcohol, and cutting back trigger foods such as fatty meals and late-night eating—directly targeting his pattern, so this is the answer. Eating large late meals and lying down right after eating both worsen reflux by increasing gastric volume and promoting acid exposure when supine. Increasing coffee and carbonated drinks aggravates reflux in many patients and is the opposite of recommended guidance.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: He returns after 8 weeks with complete symptom resolution on the PPI. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue the PPI indefinitely at the same dose without reassessment
Attempt to step down or discontinue the PPI, using the lowest effective dose or on-demand therapy
Refer for fundoplication now that symptoms have resolved
Switch to lifelong twice-daily PPI to prevent any recurrence
stages.explanation: After successful treatment of uncomplicated GERD, guidelines recommend attempting to step down to the lowest effective dose or on-demand/intermittent therapy, balancing symptom control against long-term PPI exposure—so this is the answer. Continuing indefinitely without reassessment and escalating to lifelong twice-daily dosing expose him unnecessarily to potential long-term risks when his disease may be controlled with less. Anti-reflux surgery is reserved for patients with refractory symptoms, large hiatal hernias, or those who cannot tolerate or prefer to avoid medications, not for someone whose symptoms resolved completely on a PPI.

pearls: Screen every heartburn patient for alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding, anemia, vomiting) that mandate endoscopy.
Classic GERD without alarm features is managed with an empiric PPI trial plus lifestyle change—endoscopy is not required upfront.
Lifestyle measures: weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoiding late meals, and limiting alcohol and trigger foods.
After symptom control, step down to the lowest effective PPI dose or on-demand therapy.

cases.title: Aching Back After Yard Work
cases.chiefComplaint: A 38-year-old man presents with three days of low back pain after lifting.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents with three days of low back pain that began after a weekend of lifting heavy bags of mulch and twisting while gardening. The pain is a dull ache across the lower lumbar region, worse with bending and prolonged sitting and improved with movement and lying down. He denies pain radiating below the knee, leg weakness, numbness, or tingling. He has no bowel or bladder incontinence, no saddle anesthesia, no fever, and no recent unexplained weight loss. He has never had cancer and uses no injection drugs. His past medical history is unremarkable; he takes only occasional acetaminophen and has no drug allergies. He works as a teacher, exercises irregularly, and is otherwise healthy. He has no history of significant trauma, osteoporosis, or chronic steroid use. He does not smoke. There is no family history of inflammatory back disease. He is worried he 'slipped a disc' and asks whether he needs an MRI to be safe.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, moving stiffly but in no acute distress. Paraspinal lumbar muscle tenderness and palpable spasm bilaterally without midline spinous-process tenderness. Range of motion limited by pain on flexion. Straight-leg raise negative bilaterally. Strength 5/5 in hip flexors, knee extensors, ankle dorsiflexors, and great-toe extensors. Sensation intact in all dermatomes; patellar and Achilles reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Normal anal sphincter tone. Gait normal; able to heel- and toe-walk. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which assessment is most important in the initial evaluation of acute low back pain?
options: Measuring the exact severity on a 0–10 pain scale
Screening for red-flag features such as neurologic deficits, cauda equina signs, infection, fracture, or malignancy
Determining whether the pain is left- or right-sided
Ordering routine lumbar radiographs in all patients
stages.explanation: The priority in acute low back pain is screening for red flags—progressive neurologic deficit, cauda equina syndrome (saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction), signs of infection (fever, IV drug use), fracture (significant trauma, osteoporosis, steroids), or malignancy (history of cancer, weight loss, night pain)—because these change management urgently, so this is the answer. A pain-scale number guides analgesia but does not triage serious pathology. Laterality is minor and rarely alters management. Routine radiographs for everyone are explicitly discouraged in uncomplicated acute back pain because they add cost and radiation without improving outcomes. This patient has no red flags.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given his history and exam, which is the most appropriate statement about imaging?
options: No imaging is needed; acute nonspecific mechanical back pain without red flags does not require it
Obtain an MRI now to exclude a herniated disc
Order plain lumbar films to reassure the patient
Get a CT scan because it is faster than MRI
stages.explanation: For acute, nonspecific mechanical low back pain without red flags, guidelines recommend against routine imaging because most cases resolve within weeks, imaging findings are often incidental, and early imaging does not improve outcomes while increasing cost and unnecessary interventions—so this is the answer. MRI now is reserved for red flags, suspected cauda equina, progressive deficits, or persistent symptoms despite conservative care. Plain films for reassurance expose him to radiation without benefit and can paradoxically worsen anxiety by revealing incidental degenerative changes. A CT scan carries even higher radiation and is not indicated for uncomplicated mechanical back pain.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for his acute mechanical low back pain?
options: Strict bed rest for one to two weeks
Encourage staying active as tolerated, with NSAIDs and reassurance about a favorable prognosis
Prescribe a course of oral opioids as first-line therapy
Refer immediately to a spine surgeon
stages.explanation: First-line management is to remain active as tolerated, avoid bed rest, use NSAIDs (and possibly short-term muscle relaxants) for symptom relief, and provide reassurance that most acute mechanical back pain resolves within a few weeks—so this is the answer. Strict bed rest actually delays recovery and is no longer recommended. Opioids as first-line carry dependence and adverse-effect risks and are reserved for severe pain unresponsive to other measures, used briefly if at all. Immediate surgical referral is unwarranted without red flags, neurologic deficits, or failure of conservative therapy; this is self-limited muscular strain.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which counseling point is most appropriate when he asks how to know if something is seriously wrong?
options: He should return urgently for new leg weakness, numbness in the groin, or loss of bladder or bowel control
He should request an MRI if pain persists beyond 48 hours
Any back pain lasting more than a day signals a herniated disc
He should remain in bed until completely pain-free
stages.explanation: Appropriate safety-netting is to instruct him to seek urgent care for new or progressive leg weakness, saddle (groin/perineal) numbness, or bowel/bladder dysfunction—features of cauda equina syndrome or significant neurologic compromise—so this is the answer. Demanding an MRI after merely 48 hours contradicts evidence-based imaging guidance for uncomplicated pain. Equating any back pain beyond a day with a herniated disc is inaccurate fear-mongering; most pain is muscular and self-limited. Staying in bed until pain-free is harmful advice that prolongs recovery and deconditioning; gradual return to activity is recommended.

pearls: Screen every acute back pain for red flags: neurologic deficit, cauda equina, infection, fracture, malignancy.
Do not image acute nonspecific mechanical back pain without red flags—it does not improve outcomes.
Manage with continued activity, NSAIDs, and reassurance; avoid bed rest and first-line opioids.
Teach return precautions: new leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, or bowel/bladder dysfunction.

cases.title: A Cough That Won't Quit
cases.chiefComplaint: A 33-year-old woman presents with a persistent cough following a cold.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents with a cough that started about ten days ago along with a head cold that has otherwise resolved. The cough is now her main complaint—initially dry, now occasionally productive of small amounts of clear-to-whitish sputum, sometimes with chest soreness from coughing. She had low-grade subjective fevers in the first few days that have since resolved, and she feels otherwise back to baseline aside from the nagging cough that disrupts her sleep. She denies high fever, drenching night sweats, hemoptysis, weight loss, or shortness of breath at rest. She has no history of asthma, COPD, GERD, or heart disease and takes no daily medications; she has no drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, works as a nurse, and is up to date on vaccines. She has no sick contacts with tuberculosis and no recent travel. She is frustrated and asks specifically for 'a Z-Pak' because antibiotics 'always fix it.' On review of systems she notes mild postnasal drip but no wheezing.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, conversant without respiratory distress and speaking in full sentences. No accessory muscle use. Oropharynx mildly erythematous with clear postnasal drainage; tympanic membranes normal. Neck supple, no lymphadenopathy. Lungs with normal breath sounds and no focal crackles, no dullness to percussion, no bronchial breath sounds; occasional scattered rhonchi that clear with coughing, no wheezes. Heart regular without murmur. No peripheral edema. No egophony or increased tactile fremitus.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most strongly argues against community-acquired pneumonia and supports acute bronchitis in this patient?
options: Productive cough of clear sputum
Normal vital signs with a clear lung exam—no focal crackles, dullness, or hypoxia
Chest soreness with coughing
Preceding upper respiratory symptoms
stages.explanation: Acute bronchitis is a clinical diagnosis supported by an absence of pneumonia signs: normal vital signs (no tachypnea, tachycardia, or fever beyond the early viral phase), normal oxygen saturation, and a lung exam lacking focal crackles, dullness, bronchial breath sounds, or egophony—so this is the answer and obviates the need for a chest X-ray. A productive clear-sputum cough occurs in bronchitis but also in pneumonia, so it is nondiscriminating. Chest soreness from coughing is musculoskeletal and common in bronchitis. Preceding URI symptoms fit bronchitis but can precede pneumonia too; the reassuring exam and vitals are what distinguish them.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has normal vitals and a clear lung exam. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order a chest X-ray to rule out pneumonia in all coughs lasting over a week
No chest imaging is needed; diagnose acute bronchitis clinically given the absence of pneumonia findings
Send a sputum culture and complete blood count routinely
Obtain a CT chest to evaluate the cough
stages.explanation: In a healthy adult with normal vital signs and an unremarkable lung exam, acute bronchitis is a clinical diagnosis and no chest imaging is required—so this is the answer. A chest X-ray is indicated only when pneumonia is suspected by abnormal vitals (tachycardia, tachypnea, fever, hypoxia) or focal exam findings, none of which she has; reflexively imaging every prolonged cough is low-yield. Routine sputum cultures and CBC do not change management of acute bronchitis. A chest CT is far too aggressive for an uncomplicated post-viral cough and adds cost and radiation without benefit.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is requesting azithromycin. What is the most appropriate management and counseling?
options: Prescribe azithromycin because she is a nurse and exposed to many germs
Explain that acute bronchitis is almost always viral; recommend supportive care and counsel against antibiotics
Prescribe amoxicillin-clavulanate to cover bacterial bronchitis
Start inhaled corticosteroids for all cases of bronchitis
stages.explanation: Acute bronchitis is overwhelmingly viral, and randomized evidence shows antibiotics provide negligible benefit while causing adverse effects and driving resistance; the correct approach is supportive care—fluids, rest, antitussives or analgesics as needed—and clear counseling that antibiotics are not helpful, so this is the answer. Prescribing azithromycin or amoxicillin-clavulanate for viral bronchitis exemplifies the inappropriate prescribing this case warns against, regardless of occupation. Inhaled corticosteroids for everyone are not indicated; bronchodilators may help only if there is wheezing or airflow obstruction, which she does not have.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which counseling point about the expected course and return precautions is most appropriate?
options: The cough may persist up to 2–3 weeks; return if she develops high fever, shortness of breath, or worsening symptoms
If the cough is not gone in 3 days, antibiotics are definitely needed
A lingering cough always indicates whooping cough requiring antibiotics
She should expect the cough to resolve within 24 hours of starting any treatment
stages.explanation: Patients should be told that the cough of acute bronchitis commonly lasts two to three weeks even without complications, and that they should return for high fever, dyspnea, hemoptysis, or clinical worsening that could signal pneumonia—so this is the answer and sets realistic expectations that prevent unnecessary antibiotic-seeking. A 3-day rule for antibiotics is arbitrary and wrong. Assuming pertussis in every lingering cough overgeneralizes; pertussis is considered with paroxysms, post-tussive emesis, or known exposure. Expecting resolution within 24 hours sets a false expectation that fuels demand for ineffective antibiotics.

pearls: Acute bronchitis is a clinical diagnosis; normal vitals and a clear lung exam make pneumonia unlikely and obviate imaging.
It is almost always viral—antibiotics offer minimal benefit and drive resistance and side effects.
Manage with supportive care and explicit counseling against antibiotics.
Set expectations: cough can last 2–3 weeks; advise return for fever, dyspnea, or worsening that suggests pneumonia.

cases.title: A Positive Screening Test Before a New Job
cases.chiefComplaint: I had a blood test for TB for my hospital job and they said it came back positive.
cases.history: A 32-year-old man presents to establish care after a pre-employment health screen for a hospital dietary position returned a positive interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA). He emigrated from the Philippines four years ago. He feels well, denies cough, hemoptysis, fevers, night sweats, or weight loss, and works full-time without limitation. He received BCG vaccination as an infant. He has never been treated for tuberculosis and recalls no known TB contacts, though tuberculosis is common in his home country. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no medications and reports no allergies. He drinks alcohol socially, has never smoked, and uses no illicit drugs. He is monogamous with his wife. Family history is noncontributory. A prior tuberculin skin test years ago was reportedly positive but never evaluated, which he attributed to his BCG vaccine. He has no history of diabetes, HIV risk factors, or immunosuppressive therapy, and has had no recent incarceration or homeless-shelter exposure.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally without crackles, wheezes, or bronchial breath sounds. Heart regular without murmurs. Abdomen soft and nontender with no hepatosplenomegaly. No skin lesions, no scrofula, and no clubbing. No oral thrush. He appears at his stated weight with no temporal wasting. Neurologic exam is grossly intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How should you interpret his positive IGRA given his BCG history?
options: The IGRA is likely a false positive from prior BCG vaccination and can be disregarded
The IGRA is not affected by BCG and indicates Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection requiring evaluation
A confirmatory tuberculin skin test is required before the IGRA can be trusted
The result reflects only remote exposure and needs no further action
stages.explanation: IGRAs (QuantiFERON, T-SPOT) measure interferon-gamma release to M. tuberculosis-specific antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10) that are absent from BCG and most non-tuberculous mycobacteria, so BCG does not cause false positives. A positive IGRA therefore indicates genuine TB infection and mandates evaluation. That option is the classic trap: this property is exactly why IGRA is preferred over skin testing in BCG-vaccinated patients. Another option is wrong because a skin test is less specific here and would not 'confirm' the IGRA; the two are not used to validate each other. A third option underestimates the finding; infection must be distinguished from active disease before any treatment decision.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most important next step before considering treatment?
options: Start isoniazid empirically while awaiting further data
Obtain a chest radiograph and assess for symptoms to exclude active tuberculosis
Collect three sputum samples for acid-fast bacilli smear and culture immediately
Refer directly to pulmonology for bronchoscopy
stages.explanation: The pivotal step before treating presumed latent TB is to rule out active disease, because treating active TB with a single drug breeds resistance. The first move is a symptom screen plus a chest radiograph. That option is dangerous: empiric monotherapy for unrecognized active TB is a major cause of acquired isoniazid resistance. Another option is premature in an asymptomatic patient with a normal exam and no imaging—sputum is pursued only if symptoms or the radiograph suggest active disease. A third option is excessive; bronchoscopy is not a screening tool and is reserved for selected cases when sputum is nondiagnostic but suspicion for active disease remains high.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Chest X-ray (PA and lateral): Clear lung fields. No upper-lobe infiltrate, cavitation, nodules, or pleural effusion. No hilar or mediastinal adenopathy. Normal cardiac silhouette.
HIV 4th-generation Ag/Ab: Negative
Hepatic panel: AST 22 U/L, ALT 25 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 70 U/L
HbA1c: 5.3%
CBC: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the workup, what is the diagnosis?
options: Active pulmonary tuberculosis
Prior BCG reaction without true infection
Latent tuberculosis infection
Nontuberculous mycobacterial colonization
stages.explanation: A positive IGRA with no symptoms, a normal exam, and a clear chest radiograph defines latent TB infection—viable but contained organisms with no clinical or radiographic disease—so this is the answer. That option is excluded because active TB requires symptoms, abnormal imaging, or microbiologic confirmation, none of which are present. Another option is wrong because the IGRA is not affected by BCG, so the positive result reflects true infection, not vaccine cross-reactivity. A third option is incorrect because IGRA antigens are specific to the M. tuberculosis complex and do not detect most nontuberculous mycobacteria, so colonization with NTM would not produce this result.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which treatment regimen is now preferred for latent TB infection in this patient?
options: Isoniazid daily for 9 months as the only acceptable regimen
Rifampin plus isoniazid plus pyrazinamide plus ethambutol for 6 months
Three months of weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine, or four months of daily rifampin
No treatment because BCG-vaccinated patients clear the infection on their own
stages.explanation: Current CDC/ATS guidance favors short-course rifamycin-based regimens for latent TB—3 months of weekly isoniazid-rifapentine (3HP) or 4 months of daily rifampin (4R)—because they improve completion rates and are less hepatotoxic. That option is outdated: 9 months of isoniazid is still effective but no longer the preferred first choice given poorer adherence and higher hepatotoxicity. Another option describes the four-drug regimen for active TB, which is inappropriate and excessive for latent infection. A third option is false; latent infection does not self-clear, and untreated patients carry roughly a 5–10% lifetime risk of reactivation, higher in the first two years after infection.

pearls: IGRAs use M. tuberculosis-specific antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10) and are not confounded by BCG, making them the preferred test in BCG-vaccinated patients.
Always exclude active TB with a symptom screen and chest radiograph before treating latent infection—single-drug therapy for unrecognized active disease drives resistance.
Preferred latent TB regimens are now short-course rifamycin-based: 3HP (weekly INH-rifapentine x12) or 4R (daily rifampin x4 months).
Untreated latent TB carries a roughly 5–10% lifetime reactivation risk, concentrated in the first 1–2 years after infection.

cases.title: An Unexpected Result on Routine Bloodwork
cases.chiefComplaint: My doctor said one of my routine blood tests came back positive and I need it checked out.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents for follow-up after a one-time hepatitis C antibody screen, recommended for his birth cohort, returned positive. He feels well and denies fatigue, jaundice, abdominal pain, easy bruising, or leg swelling. He has no prior knowledge of liver disease. Past medical history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily. He has no known drug allergies. He used intravenous drugs a few times in his twenties but not since, received a blood transfusion after a motor-vehicle accident in 1985, and has several tattoos. He drinks two beers most weekends and has never smoked. He is sexually active with his wife of 30 years. Family history includes coronary disease in his father. He takes no over-the-counter hepatotoxins and reports no recent travel. Review of systems is otherwise negative, with no pruritus, dark urine, or confusion.
cases.exam: Well-nourished man in no acute distress and oriented fully. Sclerae anicteric and conjunctivae without pallor. No spider angiomata, palmar erythema, gynecomastia, or Dupuytren contracture. Cardiopulmonary exam normal with clear lungs and a regular rhythm. Abdomen soft and nontender; the liver edge is not enlarged and is nontender, with no splenomegaly, no ascites or fluid wave, and no caput medusae. No peripheral edema and no asterixis on wrist extension. Skin shows healed tattoos without active lesions or jaundice. Neurologic exam is intact with no confusion or tremor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A positive hepatitis C antibody is reported. What does this result establish?
options: Confirmed current chronic hepatitis C infection requiring immediate treatment
Exposure to hepatitis C at some point; current infection is not yet confirmed
Immunity to hepatitis C from prior cleared infection, so no further testing is needed
A false positive that should simply be repeated in one year
stages.explanation: A reactive HCV antibody indicates past or present exposure but cannot distinguish active infection from a resolved or cleared one, because antibodies persist after spontaneous or treated clearance. Thus this is correct, and confirmation of viremia is required. That option overcalls the result; up to 15–25% of acutely infected people clear the virus spontaneously and remain antibody-positive without active infection. Another option is wrong because a positive antibody does not denote protective immunity—reinfection is possible—and further testing is mandatory, not optional. A third option is inappropriate; the next step is reflex HCV RNA testing now, not repeating the antibody after a delay.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the appropriate confirmatory test?
options: Hepatitis C genotype testing alone
Repeat hepatitis C antibody by a different assay
Quantitative hepatitis C virus RNA (PCR)
Liver biopsy to confirm infection
stages.explanation: After a reactive antibody, the confirmatory test for active infection is quantitative HCV RNA by PCR; detectable RNA confirms current viremia, while undetectable RNA indicates cleared infection. That option is therefore correct. Another option is premature—genotype may guide therapy decisions but does not confirm whether the patient is currently infected, and pangenotypic regimens have made routine genotyping less essential. A third option adds nothing because repeating the same class of test does not establish viremia. A fourth option is invasive and unnecessary for diagnosis; biopsy is no longer required to diagnose HCV and has been largely replaced by noninvasive fibrosis assessment for staging.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
HCV RNA (quantitative): 1,250,000 IU/mL (detected)
AST 58 U/L
ALT 64 U/L
Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL
Albumin 4.1 g/dL
Platelets 188 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
HIV Ag/Ab: Negative
Hepatitis B surface antigen: Negative; anti-HBs positive (immune)
FIB-4 index: 1.8

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis, and how would you characterize his liver status?
options: Resolved hepatitis C with no active infection
Chronic hepatitis C infection with low likelihood of advanced fibrosis
Acute hepatitis C with fulminant hepatic failure
Hepatitis B and C coinfection requiring dual therapy
stages.explanation: Detectable HCV RNA with mild transaminase elevation in a long-exposed patient confirms chronic hepatitis C, and a FIB-4 of 1.8 (below the 3.25 threshold) with normal albumin, INR, and platelets indicates a low likelihood of advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis—so this is the answer. That option is excluded by the positive RNA, which proves ongoing infection rather than clearance. Another option is wrong: there is no synthetic dysfunction (normal INR, bilirubin, albumin), no encephalopathy, and the picture is chronic, not fulminant. A third option is incorrect because the HBsAg is negative with positive anti-HBs, indicating immunity to hepatitis B, not active coinfection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Recommend interferon plus ribavirin and counsel about its many side effects
Tell him treatment is reserved for patients who already have cirrhosis
Refer for direct-acting antiviral therapy and counsel that cure is expected in the great majority
Observe without treatment and recheck RNA in two years
stages.explanation: All patients with chronic HCV should be offered direct-acting antiviral (DAA) therapy; pangenotypic oral regimens achieve sustained virologic response (cure) in over 95% of patients with a short, well-tolerated course, so referral and counseling about expected cure is correct. That option reflects outdated interferon-based therapy, which is no longer recommended given DAAs' superior efficacy and tolerability. Another option is wrong because treatment is recommended for essentially all infected patients regardless of fibrosis stage, not just those with cirrhosis—earlier treatment prevents progression and transmission. A third option is inappropriate because deferring therapy allows ongoing liver injury and transmission risk when a curative, safe option exists now.

pearls: A reactive HCV antibody only proves exposure; confirm active infection with quantitative HCV RNA, since 15–25% of people spontaneously clear the virus yet stay antibody-positive.
Noninvasive tools like FIB-4 and elastography have largely replaced liver biopsy for fibrosis staging; FIB-4 < 1.45 suggests minimal fibrosis and > 3.25 suggests advanced fibrosis.
Pangenotypic direct-acting antiviral regimens cure >95% of patients in 8–12 weeks; treatment is recommended for essentially all infected patients, not only those with cirrhosis.
Always check HBsAg before DAA therapy—reactivation of hepatitis B can occur during HCV treatment.

cases.title: Stiff, Aching Hands in the Morning
cases.chiefComplaint: My hands and wrists have been swollen and stiff for a couple of months, especially in the mornings.
cases.history: A 43-year-old woman presents with three months of progressive bilateral hand and wrist pain, swelling, and stiffness. Morning stiffness lasts about 90 minutes before her joints loosen up. She reports symmetric involvement of the knuckles and wrists with sparing of the very ends of her fingers. She has fatigue and a low-grade sense of being unwell but denies rash, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, dry eyes, or Raynaud phenomenon.
