Past medical history is notable only for hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She takes ibuprofen as needed with partial relief and has no known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, drinks rarely, and works as a teacher. Family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis and a maternal aunt with autoimmune thyroid disease. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, chest pain, dyspnea, and bowel or bladder changes. She has had no recent infections, tick exposures, or new sexual partners, and her menstrual cycles are regular.
cases.exam: Symmetric synovitis with soft-tissue swelling and tenderness of the bilateral metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints and wrists; distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints are spared. Positive MCP squeeze test bilaterally. Mild limitation of wrist flexion. No nodules palpable at the elbows today. No malar rash, no oral ulcers, no nail pitting, and no dactylitis. Lungs clear; no pleural or pericardial rub. No lower-extremity edema. Remaining joints, including spine and lower limbs, are nontender with full range of motion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly points toward an inflammatory rather than degenerative arthritis?
options: Pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest
Prominent involvement of the distal interphalangeal joints
Morning stiffness lasting more than an hour with symmetric small-joint synovitis
Crepitus and bony hypertrophy without soft-tissue swelling
stages.explanation: Prolonged morning stiffness (>60 minutes) plus symmetric soft-tissue synovitis of small joints is the hallmark of inflammatory arthritis such as RA. That option describes a mechanical/degenerative pattern—osteoarthritis pain typically worsens with use and eases with rest, opposite to the inflammatory pattern where rest causes gelling. Another option points to osteoarthritis (Heberden nodes) or psoriatic arthritis; RA characteristically spares the DIP joints. A third option—crepitus and bony hypertrophy without synovial swelling—describes osteoarthritis. The combination of boggy synovitis, symmetry, and stiffness lasting >1 hour reliably distinguishes an inflammatory arthropathy from osteoarthritis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory and imaging workup?
options: Serum uric acid and joint aspiration for crystals
Anti-CCP antibody, rheumatoid factor, ESR/CRP, CBC, and hand/wrist radiographs
ANA with reflex panel only
MRI of both hands with contrast as the first-line test
stages.explanation: For suspected RA, the high-yield initial workup is anti-CCP (highly specific), rheumatoid factor, inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), a CBC, and baseline hand/wrist films to assess for erosions—. That option targets gout, which is unlikely given the symmetric small-joint synovitis and absence of acute monoarticular attacks. Another option is too narrow; ANA is more useful when lupus is suspected and is nonspecific, so it should not be the sole test in a classic RA presentation. A third option is premature and costly—MRI is more sensitive for early erosions but is not first-line; plain radiographs plus serologies and acute-phase reactants are the appropriate starting point.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Anti-CCP antibody: 145 U (strongly positive; normal <20)
Rheumatoid factor: 88 IU/mL (high)
ESR: 48 mm/hr
CRP: 2.8 mg/dL
ANA: negative
Uric acid: 4.8 mg/dL
CBC: mild normocytic anemia, Hb 11.6 g/dL; platelets 410 ×10³/µL
Hand/wrist X-rays: periarticular osteopenia and early marginal erosion at a right MCP joint; no joint-space narrowing of DIPs

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Osteoarthritis of the hands
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Rheumatoid arthritis
Gout
stages.explanation: Symmetric MCP/PIP/wrist synovitis with DIP sparing, morning stiffness >1 hour, strongly positive anti-CCP and rheumatoid factor, elevated ESR/CRP, and early marginal erosions on imaging are classic for rheumatoid arthritis—that option. Another option is wrong because osteoarthritis preferentially affects DIPs and weight-bearing joints with bony (not boggy) enlargement and minimal inflammatory markers. A third option is unlikely with a negative ANA and no malar rash, ulcers, serositis, or cytopenias beyond mild anemia of inflammation. A fourth option is excluded by the symmetric polyarticular pattern, normal uric acid, and absence of acute crystal-driven monoarthritis; gout is typically asymmetric and episodic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best initial disease-modifying management?
options: Long-term NSAIDs alone and reassess in six months
Prompt referral to rheumatology and initiation of methotrexate as the anchor DMARD
Start a TNF inhibitor as monotherapy first-line
Daily oral prednisone indefinitely as the sole therapy
stages.explanation: Early RA warrants prompt rheumatology referral and early DMARD therapy, with methotrexate as the preferred anchor drug to prevent erosive joint damage—'treat-to-target' within a tight window improves outcomes, so this is the answer. That option is inadequate: NSAIDs control symptoms but do not modify disease, and delaying DMARDs allows irreversible erosion. Another option is not first-line; biologics like TNF inhibitors are added when conventional DMARDs fail, not started alone initially in most patients. A third option is wrong because chronic glucocorticoid monotherapy causes substantial toxicity and does not adequately halt progression; steroids are used only as a short-term bridge while DMARDs take effect.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: She is started on methotrexate with folic acid. What baseline and ongoing monitoring is essential?
options: Periodic renal ultrasound only
CBC and hepatic transaminases at baseline and at regular intervals, plus avoidance of pregnancy and alcohol
Annual chest CT regardless of symptoms
No monitoring is required because methotrexate is well tolerated
stages.explanation: Methotrexate requires monitoring for myelosuppression and hepatotoxicity, so CBC and AST/ALT (with albumin and creatinine) are checked at baseline and periodically; it is also teratogenic, mandating reliable contraception, and alcohol should be limited to reduce hepatic injury—so this is the answer. Folic acid supplementation reduces mucosal and hematologic side effects. That option is wrong because routine renal ultrasound is not part of methotrexate monitoring, though renal function is checked since the drug is renally cleared. Another option is incorrect; baseline chest imaging is reasonable given pneumonitis risk, but routine annual CT is not standard, and new respiratory symptoms drive imaging instead. A third option is dangerous—methotrexate has serious potential toxicities that demand scheduled laboratory surveillance.

pearls: Morning stiffness lasting >60 minutes plus symmetric small-joint synovitis (MCPs, PIPs, wrists) with DIP sparing is the classic RA pattern.
Anti-CCP is more specific than rheumatoid factor and predicts erosive disease; both, with elevated ESR/CRP, support the diagnosis.
Early DMARD initiation—methotrexate as the anchor—within the first months prevents irreversible joint erosion; NSAIDs and steroids only bridge symptoms.
RA spares the DIP joints, helping distinguish it from osteoarthritis and psoriatic arthritis.

cases.title: A Young Woman With a Facial Rash and Aching Joints
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been exhausted, my joints ache, and I keep getting a red rash across my face in the sun.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman presents with two months of fatigue, diffuse joint pain, and a recurrent red rash over her cheeks and nose that flares with sun exposure. The joint pain is symmetric, involves the hands and knees, and is accompanied by about 45 minutes of morning stiffness, but she notes no joint deformity. She reports occasional painless mouth sores and patchy hair thinning. She denies fever, chest pain, dyspnea, leg swelling, or dark urine, though she has noticed mild ankle puffiness in the evenings. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive pill and a daily multivitamin, with no drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and works in graphic design. Family history includes a sister with autoimmune thyroid disease and a grandmother with 'a blood disorder.' Review of systems is positive for Raynaud-like color changes in cold weather and negative for seizures, psychosis, recent infection, and pregnancy. Her menstrual cycles are regular.
cases.exam: Erythematous malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds. Two small painless palatal ulcers. Mild tenderness and soft swelling of several PIP joints and both wrists without deformity; knees nontender. No DIP involvement. Diffuse, nonscarring hair thinning at the frontal scalp. Lungs clear with no pleural rub; heart regular with no rub or murmur. Trace bilateral ankle edema. No discoid plaques, no digital ulcers, and no synovial nodules. Neurologic exam intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation best fits a multisystem autoimmune disease in this patient?
options: Isolated photosensitive rash with no systemic features
Malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, symmetric arthralgia, and fatigue
Asymmetric large-joint arthritis with dactylitis and nail pitting
DIP-predominant bony arthritis with Heberden nodes
stages.explanation: The combination of a photosensitive malar rash, painless oral ulcers, symmetric inflammatory arthralgia, fatigue, and nonscarring alopecia points to a multisystem connective-tissue disease—classically SLE—so this is the answer. That option understates the picture; she clearly has systemic features beyond skin. Another option describes psoriatic arthritis (dactylitis, nail pitting, asymmetric large-joint involvement), which does not fit her symmetric small-joint pattern or facial rash. A third option describes osteoarthritis, a noninflammatory, DIP-predominant disease seen in older patients—inconsistent with her age, systemic symptoms, and skin/mucosal findings. The multisystem mucocutaneous-plus-articular pattern is the key clue.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the best initial screening test, and how should subsequent testing proceed?
options: Order anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith first; ANA is unnecessary
Obtain an ANA as the entry screen; if positive, pursue specific antibodies and organ-screening labs
Skin biopsy of the malar rash as the first diagnostic step
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP as the primary screen
stages.explanation: ANA is the recommended entry criterion and screening test for SLE because it is highly sensitive (positive in ~95–99%); a positive ANA then prompts specific antibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith) and organ screening (CBC, creatinine, urinalysis, complements)—so this is the answer. That option is inefficient and inverted: anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are specific but insensitive, so ordering them first risks missing patients and is not the screening strategy. Another option is unnecessary as a first step; biopsy may support cutaneous lupus but is not the initial diagnostic move for systemic disease. A third option screens for rheumatoid arthritis, which does not explain her rash, photosensitivity, or oral ulcers.

stages.label: LABS & URINE
stages.content:
ANA: positive, titer 1:640, homogeneous pattern
Anti-dsDNA: 220 IU/mL (high)
Anti-Smith: positive
C3: 62 mg/dL (low); C4: 9 mg/dL (low)
CBC: WBC 3.2 ×10³/µL (leukopenia), Hb 10.8 g/dL, platelets 120 ×10³/µL
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 2+ protein, 10–15 RBCs/hpf, occasional cellular cast
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio: 0.9 g/g

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most specifically supports systemic lupus erythematosus and raises concern for renal involvement?
options: Positive ANA at 1:640 alone
Anti-Smith and anti-dsDNA antibodies with low complements, plus proteinuria and an active urine sediment
Leukopenia in isolation
Homogeneous ANA pattern by itself
stages.explanation: Anti-Smith is highly specific for SLE, and anti-dsDNA both supports the diagnosis and correlates with lupus nephritis; together with hypocomplementemia and an active sediment (proteinuria, hematuria, cellular casts), they specifically indicate SLE with renal involvement—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because ANA is sensitive but nonspecific; a positive ANA alone is common and not confirmatory. Another option is a supportive hematologic criterion but nonspecific in isolation. A third option is incorrect because the ANA staining pattern alone does not confirm SLE and does not address renal risk. The combination of specific antibodies, low complement, and an active urinalysis is what flags possible lupus nephritis requiring further evaluation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What are the most appropriate next management steps?
options: Start hydroxychloroquine, counsel on sun protection, and refer to rheumatology/nephrology for likely renal biopsy
Begin high-dose lifelong steroids alone with no further evaluation
Reassure and observe, rechecking labs in one year
Start methotrexate as monotherapy and discharge
stages.explanation: Hydroxychloroquine is foundational for nearly all SLE patients (reduces flares, organ damage, and mortality), sun protection limits cutaneous and systemic flares, and the active urinary sediment with proteinuria mandates urgent rheumatology/nephrology referral for a renal biopsy to classify lupus nephritis and guide immunosuppression—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because steroid monotherapy without diagnostic workup or hydroxychloroquine is substandard and risks unguided toxicity. Another option is unsafe given evidence of renal involvement that needs prompt evaluation. A third option is inappropriate as initial management of probable lupus nephritis—methotrexate is not the agent for renal lupus, and discharging without nephrology input would be negligent.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Before starting long-term hydroxychloroquine, what counseling and monitoring is most important?
options: No monitoring is needed because hydroxychloroquine has no significant toxicities
Baseline and periodic ophthalmologic exams to screen for retinal toxicity
Monthly renal ultrasounds to track the medication
Routine bone marrow biopsies every six months
stages.explanation: Hydroxychloroquine can cause dose- and duration-dependent retinopathy, so a baseline eye exam followed by periodic screening (typically annually after about five years, sooner with risk factors) is recommended—. That option is false; although generally well tolerated, retinal toxicity is a real, vision-threatening risk that requires surveillance. Another option is incorrect because renal ultrasound does not monitor hydroxychloroquine and is not part of its safety program; renal status in lupus is followed with urinalysis, creatinine, and proteinuria. A third option is unnecessary—hydroxychloroquine does not require marrow biopsies, and routine marrow sampling has no role in its monitoring. Counseling on adherence and the long-term protective benefits is also key.

pearls: ANA is the sensitive entry screen for SLE; anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are the specific confirmatory antibodies, and anti-Smith is the most specific.
Low C3/C4 and a rising anti-dsDNA correlate with disease activity, especially lupus nephritis.
An active urinary sediment (proteinuria, hematuria, cellular casts) signals possible lupus nephritis and warrants renal biopsy and prompt nephrology involvement.
Hydroxychloroquine plus rigorous photoprotection is the backbone of SLE therapy and lowers flares and mortality.

cases.title: Blurry Vision in One Eye With a Past Numb Spell
cases.chiefComplaint: The vision in my right eye has gone blurry and dim over a few days, and it hurts when I move it.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents with five days of progressive blurred and dim vision in the right eye accompanied by pain on eye movement and reduced color vibrancy. She describes colors as 'washed out.' Importantly, about a year ago she had a two-week episode of numbness and tingling in both legs that resolved spontaneously and was attributed to a 'pinched nerve.' She denies headache, weakness, vertigo, or bowel/bladder dysfunction currently, though she notes that a hot shower briefly worsens her vision. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive and has no drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks socially, and works as a nurse. Family history includes an aunt with an unspecified neurologic condition. Review of systems is negative for fever, rash, joint pain, recent infection, and trauma. She has had no diplopia, dysarthria, or gait disturbance, and her menstrual history is regular. She lives at a higher latitude with limited sun exposure.
cases.exam: Right eye visual acuity 20/80, left 20/20. Reduced red color saturation in the right eye. Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on the right with the swinging-flashlight test. Fundoscopy shows a normal-appearing optic disc (retrobulbar). Pain with right extraocular movements; extraocular motility otherwise full without nystagmus. Motor strength 5/5 throughout; sensation intact today; reflexes symmetric. Gait normal; finger-to-nose intact. No facial droop or dysarthria. Remaining cranial nerves normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What does the unilateral painful vision loss with a relative afferent pupillary defect most likely represent?
options: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Central retinal artery occlusion
Optic neuritis
Migraine with aura
stages.explanation: Subacute, unilateral vision loss with pain on eye movement, dyschromatopsia (reduced color saturation), and a relative afferent pupillary defect is the classic picture of optic neuritis. That option is wrong: angle-closure glaucoma causes a painful red eye with a mid-dilated fixed pupil, halos, nausea, and elevated intraocular pressure—not an RAPD with normal-appearing eye. Another option (central retinal artery occlusion) causes sudden, painless vision loss with a cherry-red spot and a pale retina, not pain with movement. A third option (migraine aura) produces transient, fully reversible positive visual phenomena lasting minutes, not days of fixed monocular loss with an RAPD.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given the prior leg-numbness episode, what is the best test to evaluate for an underlying demyelinating disorder?
options: CT head without contrast
MRI of the brain and spinal cord with and without contrast
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Electroencephalogram
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain and spine with and without gadolinium is the key test, identifying characteristic demyelinating lesions (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) and distinguishing enhancing (active) from nonenhancing (old) plaques—central to demonstrating dissemination in space and time. Thus this is correct. That option is inadequate because CT is insensitive for demyelinating white-matter lesions. Another option evaluates carotid atherosclerosis/embolic stroke risk, irrelevant to a young woman with a demyelinating presentation. A third option assesses seizure activity, not demyelination. The prior transient sensory episode plus current optic neuritis raises strong suspicion for multiple sclerosis, which MRI is best positioned to substantiate.

stages.label: MRI & CSF
stages.content:
MRI brain: multiple T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions in periventricular and juxtacortical white matter; one ovoid lesion shows gadolinium enhancement; several others do not enhance.
MRI cervical spine: short-segment demyelinating lesion at C4.
Visual evoked potentials: prolonged P100 latency on the right.
CSF: 2 oligoclonal bands present that are absent in serum; elevated IgG index; normal cell count and glucose.
Aquaporin-4 (NMO) IgG: negative; MOG IgG: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How do the findings establish multiple sclerosis under the McDonald criteria?
options: A single enhancing lesion alone is sufficient for diagnosis
Lesions in multiple characteristic locations plus simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions satisfy dissemination in space and time
Positive aquaporin-4 antibody confirms MS
Oligoclonal bands rule out MS
stages.explanation: MS requires dissemination in space (lesions in ≥2 characteristic CNS regions—here periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord) and dissemination in time (simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or a new lesion over time); CSF-specific oligoclonal bands can substitute for dissemination in time. Both are met, so this is the answer. That option is wrong because one enhancing lesion alone does not satisfy the spatial and temporal requirements. Another option is incorrect—aquaporin-4 IgG indicates neuromyelitis optica, an MS mimic, and here it is negative. A third option is false; CSF-restricted oligoclonal bands actually support MS and can fulfill the dissemination-in-time criterion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate management of the acute attack and the underlying disease?
options: Oral antibiotics for presumed optic nerve infection
High-dose IV methylprednisolone for the acute relapse and referral for a disease-modifying therapy
Immediate optic nerve surgical decompression
Lifelong oral prednisone as monotherapy to prevent relapses
stages.explanation: Acute MS relapses, including optic neuritis, are treated with high-dose IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone), which speed recovery; long-term relapse prevention requires a disease-modifying therapy started in coordination with neurology—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because optic neuritis here is demyelinating, not infectious, so antibiotics have no role. Another option is incorrect; there is no surgical target—the lesion is inflammatory/demyelinating, not compressive. A third option is inappropriate because chronic oral steroids do not modify MS disease course and carry major cumulative toxicity; steroids are for acute relapses only, while DMTs (e.g., interferons, glatiramer, oral or monoclonal agents) reduce relapse frequency.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which disease course does her presentation most likely represent?
options: Primary progressive multiple sclerosis
Relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
A single isolated event that will not recur
stages.explanation: A history of distinct neurologic episodes separated in time—prior leg numbness that resolved and now optic neuritis—with recovery between attacks defines relapsing-remitting MS, the most common form (about 85% at onset), so this is the answer. That option (primary progressive MS) features steady accumulation of disability from onset without discrete relapses and recoveries, which does not match her episodic pattern. Another option (ALS) is a motor neuron disease causing progressive weakness with upper and lower motor neuron signs and no sensory or optic involvement, inconsistent with her presentation. A third option is wrong because the radiographic dissemination in time and her prior clinical episode confirm a recurrent demyelinating disease, not an isolated, non-recurring event; this is why disease-modifying therapy is indicated.

pearls: Optic neuritis presents as subacute monocular vision loss with pain on eye movement, dyschromatopsia, and a relative afferent pupillary defect; the disc often looks normal (retrobulbar).

MS diagnosis hinges on dissemination in space and time; simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI can fulfill both, and CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for dissemination in time.
Check aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies to exclude NMO spectrum disorder and MOG-associated disease, which mimic MS but are treated differently.
Treat acute relapses with high-dose IV steroids; long-term control requires disease-modifying therapy, not chronic oral steroids.

cases.title: A Slowing Down and a Trembling Hand
cases.chiefComplaint: My right hand shakes when it's resting, and my wife says I've gotten slow and stiff.
cases.history: A 69-year-old man is brought in by his wife for about a year of a slowly worsening tremor in the right hand that is most noticeable when the hand rests in his lap and disappears when he reaches for objects. His wife reports he has become slower in his movements, his handwriting has shrunk, his face is less expressive, and his voice is softer. He shuffles slightly and has nearly fallen turning around. He denies vertigo, weakness, or sensory loss. He notes constipation, reduced sense of smell over the past few years, and acting out dreams during sleep. Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and benign prostatic hyperplasia on tamsulosin. He takes no antipsychotics or antiemetics, and reports no drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, drinks rarely, and never smoked. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, headache, urinary retention beyond baseline, and recent toxin exposure. He has had no cognitive complaints, and there is no early severe autonomic failure.
cases.exam: Hypomimic (masked) facies and hypophonic speech. Resting 4–6 Hz pill-rolling tremor of the right hand that decreases with voluntary movement. Cogwheel rigidity at the right wrist. Bradykinesia on finger-tapping with decrementing amplitude, right worse than left. Reduced right arm swing while walking; stooped posture and short shuffling steps with slowed turning. Pull test shows mild retropulsion. No cerebellar dysmetria, no nystagmus, no pyramidal weakness, and downgaze is intact. Sensation normal. No orthostatic drop on repeat measurement today.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which triad of findings is most characteristic of his presentation?
options: Intention tremor, ataxia, and nystagmus
Resting tremor, bradykinesia, and rigidity, asymmetric in onset
Postural tremor, hyperreflexia, and spasticity
Action tremor improved by alcohol with a family history
stages.explanation: The cardinal features of parkinsonism are bradykinesia plus a resting tremor and/or rigidity, classically asymmetric at onset—exactly this patient's picture—so this is the answer. That option describes a cerebellar syndrome (intention tremor, ataxia, nystagmus), inconsistent with his normal coordination and gaze. Another option suggests an upper-motor-neuron/spastic process, but he has no hyperreflexia or spasticity, and a postural tremor is not his pattern. A third option describes essential tremor—an action/postural tremor that improves with alcohol and is often familial—whereas his tremor is present at rest and improves with movement, the opposite of essential tremor.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is Parkinson disease best diagnosed in this setting?
options: Clinically, based on the motor exam plus supportive features
By a confirmatory serum biomarker
By a characteristic finding on routine MRI brain
Only after a brain biopsy demonstrating Lewy bodies
stages.explanation: Parkinson disease is a clinical diagnosis based on bradykinesia with resting tremor and/or rigidity, supported by features like asymmetry, hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation, and a response to levodopa—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because no validated routine serum biomarker confirms PD. Another option is incorrect; MRI is typically normal in idiopathic PD and is used mainly to exclude mimics (e.g., vascular parkinsonism, normal-pressure hydrocephalus), not to confirm the diagnosis. A third option is false in clinical practice—Lewy bodies are a pathologic hallmark seen at autopsy, but diagnosis during life is clinical, not biopsy-based.

stages.label: ANCILLARY DATA
stages.content:
MRI brain: age-appropriate atrophy; no hydrocephalus, no basal ganglia infarcts, no midbrain 'hummingbird' sign.
TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (normal)
CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel: within normal limits
Medication review: no dopamine-blocking agents (no metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, or antipsychotics)
Orthostatic vitals: no significant drop
MoCA cognitive screen: 27/30 (normal)

stages.label: DIFFERENTIAL
stages.question: Which feature would argue AGAINST idiopathic Parkinson disease and suggest an atypical parkinsonian syndrome?
options: Asymmetric resting tremor with good expected levodopa response
Early severe autonomic failure, early falls, or vertical gaze palsy
Hyposmia and REM sleep behavior disorder
Slowly progressive, unilateral-onset bradykinesia
stages.explanation: Red flags for atypical parkinsonism (e.g., multiple system atrophy, progressive supranuclear palsy) include early prominent autonomic failure, early recurrent falls, vertical (especially downward) gaze palsy, symmetric onset, and poor levodopa response—so this is the answer. That option is typical of idiopathic PD, which is asymmetric and levodopa-responsive. Another option describes premotor/nonmotor features (hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder) that actually support idiopathic PD. A third option—gradual, unilateral-onset bradykinesia—is the expected course of idiopathic PD, not a warning sign. Recognizing these red flags is essential because atypical syndromes carry different prognoses and respond poorly to dopaminergic therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has functional impairment from his symptoms. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic therapy?
options: Carbidopa-levodopa
A typical antipsychotic to suppress the tremor
High-dose anticholinergic as first-line in this age group
No treatment is available; recommend supportive care only
stages.explanation: Carbidopa-levodopa is the most effective symptomatic therapy for Parkinson disease and is the preferred initial agent for an older patient with functional impairment, providing robust motor benefit; a good response also supports the diagnosis—so this is the answer. That option is harmful: dopamine-blocking antipsychotics worsen parkinsonism and can cause drug-induced parkinsonism. Another option is inappropriate as first-line in a 69-year-old—anticholinergics mainly help tremor but cause confusion, urinary retention (he already has BPH on tamsulosin), and constipation, and are avoided in older adults. A third option is incorrect; effective dopaminergic therapy exists and substantially improves quality of life, though it is symptomatic rather than disease-modifying.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After several years on carbidopa-levodopa, he develops involuntary writhing movements when his medication peaks and stiffness returning before the next dose. What does this represent?
options: A new diagnosis of Huntington disease
Motor fluctuations and levodopa-induced dyskinesias from long-term therapy
Tardive dyskinesia from levodopa
Worsening cerebellar ataxia
stages.explanation: Years of levodopa therapy commonly produce motor fluctuations ('wearing-off' with return of rigidity before the next dose) and peak-dose dyskinesias (involuntary choreiform movements at maximal drug levels)—a well-recognized long-term complication managed by adjusting dosing intervals, adding adjuncts (COMT or MAO-B inhibitors, dopamine agonists, amantadine), or considering deep brain stimulation—so this is the answer. That option is wrong; Huntington disease is a genetic chorea with cognitive and psychiatric decline, not a consequence of levodopa in an established PD patient. Another option is incorrect because tardive dyskinesia results from chronic dopamine-blocking agents, the opposite mechanism of levodopa. A third option does not fit—he has no cerebellar signs, and the movements are dopaminergic motor complications, not ataxia.

pearls: Parkinson disease is a clinical diagnosis: bradykinesia plus resting tremor and/or rigidity, classically asymmetric, with supportive nonmotor clues (hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation).
Resting pill-rolling tremor that improves with movement distinguishes PD from the action/postural tremor of essential tremor (which improves with alcohol).
Red flags for atypical parkinsonism include early autonomic failure, early falls, vertical gaze palsy, symmetric onset, and poor levodopa response.
Carbidopa-levodopa is the most effective symptomatic therapy and is preferred initially in older patients with functional impairment.

cases.title: Tired, Achy Joints, and a Bronze Tint
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been worn out and my knuckles ache, and a blood test showed my iron level was high.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents with several months of fatigue, generalized arthralgias most prominent in the second and third knuckles of both hands, and decreased libido. A workup elsewhere showed an elevated ferritin, prompting referral. He denies alcohol excess (one drink weekly), illicit drug use, and blood transfusions. He has noticed his skin looks 'tan' even in winter. Past medical history includes new borderline glucose elevations and mild erectile dysfunction. He takes no iron supplements, no vitamins with iron, and has no drug allergies. He is of Northern European ancestry. Family history is notable for a brother with 'liver problems' and a father who died of heart failure of unclear cause in his fifties. Review of systems is positive for mild right-upper-quadrant fullness and reduced exercise tolerance, and negative for jaundice, abdominal pain, fevers, and weight loss. He works as an engineer and exercises occasionally. He has no chronic hepatitis risk factors and takes no hepatotoxic medications.
cases.exam: Diffuse bronze-gray skin hyperpigmentation. Mild tenderness and bony enlargement of the second and third MCP joints bilaterally. Liver edge palpable 2 cm below the costal margin, mildly firm and nontender; no splenomegaly, ascites, or stigmata of chronic liver disease such as spider angiomata or palmar erythema. Heart regular without murmur or gallop; no elevated JVP or peripheral edema today. Lungs clear. Testes mildly small. No asterixis. Neurologic exam intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most useful initial blood test to evaluate suspected iron overload?
options: Ferritin alone, repeated in three months
Fasting transferrin saturation (with serum iron and TIBC) along with ferritin
Serum lead level
Peripheral blood smear for basophilic stippling
stages.explanation: Transferrin saturation (serum iron divided by total iron-binding capacity) is the most sensitive early screening test for iron overload and, with ferritin, forms the standard initial panel; a fasting transferrin saturation >45% raises concern for hemochromatosis—so this is the answer. That option is incomplete because ferritin is an acute-phase reactant elevated by inflammation, alcohol, and other conditions, so it cannot stand alone. Another option targets lead toxicity, unrelated to his iron-overload picture. A third option evaluates lead poisoning or thalassemia clues, not primary iron overload. Combining transferrin saturation with ferritin best establishes whether true iron overload exists.

stages.label: IRON STUDIES & LABS
stages.content:
Fasting transferrin saturation: 78% (high)
Serum iron: 215 µg/dL; TIBC: 275 µg/dL
Ferritin: 1,250 ng/mL (markedly elevated)
AST 62 U/L, ALT 70 U/L; alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L; total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL
Fasting glucose: 122 mg/dL; HbA1c 6.3%
CBC: normal (no anemia)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the elevated transferrin saturation and ferritin, what confirmatory test best establishes hereditary hemochromatosis?
options: Liver biopsy as the required first confirmatory step
HFE gene testing for C282Y and H63D mutations
Bone marrow biopsy for iron stores
Repeat ferritin after a course of oral iron
stages.explanation: When transferrin saturation and ferritin are elevated, HFE genetic testing (C282Y homozygosity, or C282Y/H63D compound heterozygosity) confirms hereditary hemochromatosis noninvasively—so this is the answer. That option is outdated as a first step; liver biopsy is now reserved for assessing fibrosis when ferritin is very high (often >1,000 ng/mL) or liver enzymes are abnormal, not to make the genetic diagnosis. Another option is unnecessary and invasive—marrow iron is not the diagnostic standard for hemochromatosis. A third option is dangerous and illogical: giving iron to a patient with suspected iron overload would worsen the condition and provides no diagnostic value.

stages.label: GENETIC & STAGING DATA
stages.content:
HFE genotype: C282Y homozygous
Liver elastography (FibroScan): mild stiffness, no cirrhosis
Echocardiogram: normal chamber sizes and ejection fraction
Fasting glucose confirms early diabetes (HbA1c 6.3%)
Hepatitis B and C serologies: negative

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which end-organ complication of iron overload is LEAST likely to improve with iron-depletion therapy?
options: Fatigue and skin hyperpigmentation
Established cirrhosis and the destructive arthropathy
Elevated transaminases
Cardiac iron deposition detected early
stages.explanation: Once cirrhosis is established, iron removal does not reverse the fibrosis (and HCC risk persists), and the hemochromatosis arthropathy characteristically does not improve—and may progress—despite phlebotomy, so this is the answer. This is why early diagnosis and treatment, before fixed organ damage, matter so much. That option is wrong because constitutional symptoms like fatigue and skin bronzing often improve with iron depletion. Another option is incorrect; transaminase elevations from iron-related hepatocyte injury commonly normalize as iron is removed, provided cirrhosis has not developed. A third option is also reversible when caught early—early cardiac iron loading and associated dysfunction can improve with timely iron reduction, unlike advanced fibrotic or arthritic damage.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment to reduce his iron burden?
options: Oral iron chelation with deferasirox as first-line
Therapeutic phlebotomy
Strict avoidance of all dietary iron without other intervention
Splenectomy
stages.explanation: Therapeutic phlebotomy is the first-line treatment for hereditary hemochromatosis—regular blood removal depletes excess iron and, if started before cirrhosis or diabetes are fixed, prevents end-organ damage—so this is the answer. Maintenance phlebotomy then keeps ferritin in a low-normal range. That option is wrong because iron chelation is reserved for patients who cannot tolerate phlebotomy (e.g., anemia, poor venous access) and is more toxic and costly. Another option is insufficient as the sole intervention; dietary restriction has minimal impact on established overload, though avoiding vitamin C supplements and alcohol helps. A third option has no role—the spleen is not the problem in HFE hemochromatosis, and splenectomy does not remove iron.

stages.label: SCREENING & COUNSELING
stages.question: Beyond treating him, what additional steps are appropriate given the diagnosis?
options: No family implications, since hemochromatosis is not inherited
Counsel and offer screening of first-degree relatives, and monitor him for cirrhosis-related hepatocellular carcinoma if advanced fibrosis develops
Recommend a high-iron diet to maintain energy
Start lifelong prophylactic antibiotics
stages.explanation: HFE-related hemochromatosis is autosomal recessive, so first-degree relatives should be offered counseling and screening with iron studies and HFE testing to detect and treat overload early; patients who develop cirrhosis require ongoing hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because the condition is clearly heritable, and identifying affected relatives before organ damage is a major benefit. Another option is harmful; a high-iron diet worsens overload, and patients are counseled to avoid iron and vitamin C supplements and to limit alcohol. A third option is incorrect—there is a modestly increased susceptibility to certain iron-loving organisms (e.g., Vibrio, Yersinia), warranting caution, but routine prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated.

pearls: Fasting transferrin saturation is the most sensitive early screen for iron overload; combine it with ferritin, since ferritin is a nonspecific acute-phase reactant.
C282Y homozygosity (or C282Y/H63D compound heterozygosity) on HFE testing confirms hereditary hemochromatosis without biopsy.
Classic triad is the 'bronze diabetes' picture—skin hyperpigmentation, diabetes, and hepatomegaly—plus arthropathy (2nd/3rd MCPs), hypogonadism, and cardiomyopathy.
Therapeutic phlebotomy is first-line; early treatment prevents cirrhosis, cardiomyopathy, and diabetes—chelation is reserved for those who cannot tolerate phlebotomy.

cases.title: A Healthy Patient Asks About a Recommended Test
cases.chiefComplaint: I just turned 50 and my friend said I should get screened for colon cancer.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents for a routine visit and asks about cancer screening now that he has turned 50. He feels well, with no abdominal pain, change in bowel habits, rectal bleeding, melena, or unintentional weight loss. He has never had a colonoscopy or stool-based test. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; he has no known drug allergies. He has no personal history of colorectal polyps, colorectal cancer, or inflammatory bowel disease. There is no family history of colorectal cancer or advanced adenomas in any first-degree relative, and no known hereditary cancer syndrome such as Lynch syndrome or familial adenomatous polyposis. He is a former smoker (quit 10 years ago), drinks moderately, and exercises a few times weekly. Diet is average. Review of systems is otherwise negative. He is anxious about the bowel-prep involved in colonoscopy and asks whether there is a simpler option he could do at home instead.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no acute distress and at a healthy body habitus. Abdomen soft, nontender, and nondistended, with normal bowel sounds and no palpable masses or organomegaly. No palpable inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Digital rectal exam reveals normal sphincter tone, no palpable mass or fissure, and brown stool that is negative for occult blood on the exam glove. Conjunctivae are pink without pallor, and there is no scleral icterus. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with a regular rhythm and clear lungs. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: At what age should average-risk colorectal cancer screening begin under current U.S. guidelines?
options: Age 40
Age 45
Age 50
Age 60
stages.explanation: Current USPSTF and major society guidance recommends starting average-risk colorectal cancer screening at age 45, lowered from 50 in response to rising incidence in younger adults—so this is the answer. That option (40) is too early for average-risk patients and applies more to those with a family history of colorectal cancer (begin at 40 or 10 years before the relative's diagnosis). Another option (50) reflects the older recommendation that has since been revised downward. A third option (60) is far too late and would miss years of opportunity to detect and remove precancerous adenomas. This patient at 50 is in fact already overdue to begin screening.

stages.label: RISK ASSESSMENT
stages.question: How would you classify his colorectal cancer risk?
options: High risk due to family history
Average risk
High risk due to inflammatory bowel disease
High risk due to a known hereditary syndrome
stages.explanation: He has no first-degree relatives with colorectal cancer or advanced adenomas, no personal history of polyps or colorectal cancer, no inflammatory bowel disease, and no hereditary cancer syndrome—placing him at average risk, so this is the answer. That option is wrong because he reports no qualifying family history. Another option is incorrect; he has no IBD, which would otherwise raise risk and call for earlier, more frequent surveillance. A third option does not apply because there is no Lynch syndrome or familial adenomatous polyposis history. Correct risk stratification matters because high-risk patients need earlier initiation and shorter intervals, whereas average-risk patients can use the standard menu of screening options.

stages.label: SHARED DECISION
stages.question: He is reluctant about colonoscopy prep and asks about a home stool test. How should you counsel him?
options: Colonoscopy is the only acceptable screening method; refuse the stool option
Annual FIT is a reasonable alternative for average-risk patients, but a positive result requires a diagnostic colonoscopy
A single one-time FIT replaces all future screening
Stool testing is only for patients who already have symptoms
stages.explanation: For average-risk patients, several strategies are acceptable; the fecal immunochemical test (FIT) performed annually is a valid, less invasive option, but any positive stool test must be followed by a diagnostic colonoscopy to complete the screening—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because the best test is the one the patient will actually complete; multiple modalities are endorsed, and patient preference matters in shared decision-making. Another option is incorrect because FIT must be repeated annually to retain effectiveness—it is not a one-time test. A third option confuses screening with diagnostic evaluation; FIT is specifically a screening tool for asymptomatic, average-risk individuals, while symptomatic patients need direct colonoscopy.

stages.label: SCREENING OPTIONS SUMMARY
stages.content:
Colonoscopy: every 10 years if normal; allows biopsy/polypectomy in one session; requires bowel prep and sedation.
FIT (fecal immunochemical test): annually; noninvasive, home-based; positive result requires diagnostic colonoscopy.
FIT-DNA (multitarget stool DNA): every 1–3 years; higher sensitivity, more false positives; positive requires colonoscopy.
Flexible sigmoidoscopy: every 5 years (or every 10 with annual FIT).
CT colonography: every 5 years.

stages.label: PLAN
stages.question: He chooses annual FIT now. What is the appropriate follow-up plan?
options: If FIT is negative, repeat FIT annually; if FIT is positive, proceed to diagnostic colonoscopy
If FIT is negative, no further screening is ever needed
If FIT is positive, repeat the FIT in one year before any other action
Switch to fecal occult blood testing every five years regardless of results
stages.explanation: A FIT-based strategy requires annual testing when negative, and any positive FIT must be promptly evaluated with a diagnostic colonoscopy—this is correct and reflects the core principle that a positive noninvasive test mandates colonoscopy. That option is wrong because screening must continue at intervals (annually for FIT) through the recommended age range, not stop after one negative test. Another option is dangerous because delaying colonoscopy after a positive FIT allows a potential cancer or advanced adenoma to progress. A third option is incorrect; FIT is more sensitive and specific than older guaiac FOBT, is done annually rather than every five years, and switching arbitrarily is not guideline-based.

pearls: Average-risk colorectal cancer screening now begins at age 45 (lowered from 50) and generally continues through 75, with individualized decisions from 76–85.
The best screening test is the one the patient will complete; offer the menu (colonoscopy every 10 years, annual FIT, FIT-DNA every 1–3 years, etc.) via shared decision-making.
Any positive stool-based test (FIT or FIT-DNA) requires a timely diagnostic colonoscopy—not a repeat stool test.

A family history of colorectal cancer or advanced adenoma in a first-degree relative shifts a patient to high risk, warranting colonoscopy starting at 40 (or 10 years before the relative's diagnosis) at shorter intervals.

cases.title: An Abnormal Result From a Routine Women's Health Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: My Pap smear came back abnormal and I'm worried about what it means.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman returns to discuss results of routine cervical cancer screening performed at her well-woman visit. She is asymptomatic, with no abnormal vaginal bleeding, postcoital bleeding, discharge, or pelvic pain. Her prior Pap tests have all been normal, with her last one three years ago. She has never had an abnormal result before. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes a combined oral contraceptive and has no drug allergies. She has had three lifetime sexual partners, is currently in a monogamous relationship, and uses condoms inconsistently. She received only one HPV vaccine dose as a teenager and never completed the series. She is a never-smoker. She is not pregnant, with a negative urine pregnancy test today. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative. Her co-testing today included both cytology and high-risk HPV testing as part of her routine screening, and she is anxious to understand whether she has cancer.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Abdomen soft and nontender without masses or suprapubic tenderness. External genitalia are normal without lesions or condylomata. On speculum exam the cervix appears grossly normal with no visible lesions, ulceration, friability, or abnormal discharge, and no contact bleeding is elicited. Bimanual exam reveals a normal-sized, nontender, mobile uterus with no adnexal masses or tenderness and no cervical motion tenderness. No inguinal lymphadenopathy is appreciated. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: SCREENING RESULTS
stages.content:
Cervical cytology: ASC-US (atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance)
High-risk HPV testing: POSITIVE
HPV 16/18 genotyping: negative for HPV 16 and 18 (positive for other high-risk types)
Urine pregnancy test: negative

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: How should the ASC-US cytology with a positive high-risk HPV co-test be interpreted?
options: This is diagnostic of cervical cancer
Mildly abnormal cytology with a positive high-risk HPV test, indicating elevated risk that warrants further evaluation
A completely normal result requiring only routine rescreening in five years
An indication for immediate hysterectomy
stages.explanation: ASC-US is the mildest cytologic abnormality, but a concurrent positive high-risk HPV test raises the estimated risk of underlying precancer enough to warrant further evaluation rather than routine return—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because ASC-US is not cancer; cytology only screens and cannot diagnose malignancy, which requires histology. Another option is incorrect because a positive HPV co-test cannot be dismissed as normal—reflex management is triggered. A third option is grossly excessive: no tissue diagnosis exists yet, and hysterectomy is never an appropriate response to an abnormal screening cytology with positive HPV alone. The reflex to risk-based management is the central teaching point.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step for ASC-US with positive high-risk HPV?
options: Repeat the Pap smear in three years
Proceed to colposcopy
Perform an immediate excisional procedure (LEEP)
Begin topical chemotherapy to the cervix
stages.explanation: For ASC-US cytology with a positive high-risk HPV test, current ASCCP risk-based guidelines recommend colposcopy for direct visualization and biopsy of any abnormal areas—so this is the answer. That option is inappropriate because the positive HPV result elevates risk above the threshold for simple deferral; a three-year repeat would undermanage her. Another option is premature: an excisional procedure (LEEP) is a treatment performed only after a high-grade lesion (typically CIN 2/3) is confirmed histologically, not as the first step. A third option has no role; there is no topical chemotherapy used for an undiagnosed abnormal screen, and management is driven by colposcopic biopsy results.

stages.label: COLPOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Colposcopy: adequate exam, entire squamocolumnar junction visualized.
Acetowhite epithelium with fine punctation noted at the 6 o'clock position.
Colposcopically directed biopsy: CIN 1 (low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion).
Endocervical curettage: negative.
No features suggestive of invasive disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The biopsy shows CIN 1 after an ASC-US/HPV-positive screen. What is the recommended management?
options: Immediate LEEP or cone biopsy
Hysterectomy
Observation with surveillance co-testing in one year, given CIN 1's high spontaneous regression rate
Cryotherapy of the entire cervix today regardless of follow-up
stages.explanation: CIN 1 (low-grade) discovered after a minor-abnormality screen has a high rate of spontaneous regression, so guidelines favor observation with surveillance (repeat HPV-based co-testing, typically at one year) rather than immediate treatment—this is correct. That option is overtreatment: excisional procedures are reserved for high-grade lesions (CIN 2/3) or persistent CIN 1, and carry risks such as cervical insufficiency in future pregnancies. Another option is far too aggressive for a low-grade, likely-regressing lesion. A third option commits her to an ablative treatment without an evidence-based indication; ablation is considered only for persistent CIN 1 or specific scenarios, not reflexively at diagnosis. Conservative surveillance balances risk and avoids unnecessary cervical injury.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She completed only one HPV vaccine dose as a teenager. At 35, how should you counsel her about HPV vaccination?
options: She is too old to derive any benefit and should not be vaccinated
Shared decision-making applies for ages 27–45; vaccination may still offer some benefit, though it does not treat existing infection
She must restart screening from scratch only after completing vaccination
Vaccination will clear her current HPV infection and resolve the CIN 1
stages.explanation: HPV vaccination is routinely recommended through age 26, and for adults aged 27–45 it is offered through shared decision-making because benefit is smaller in those likely already exposed—so this is the answer, with the key caveat that the vaccine is prophylactic and does not treat or clear an existing infection or lesion. That option is overly absolute; some adults in this age range can still benefit from protection against types they have not yet acquired. Another option is wrong because vaccination status does not reset or gate the screening schedule—she continues guideline-based surveillance regardless. A third option is incorrect and a common misconception: the HPV vaccine prevents new infections but has no therapeutic effect on her current infection or CIN 1.

cases.diagnosis: Cervical cancer screening abnormality (ASC-US, HPV-positive) with CIN 1
pearls: Cytology and HPV co-testing screen for cervical precancer; results guide risk-based management under the 2019 ASCCP guidelines—management follows estimated risk, not the label alone.
ASC-US with a positive high-risk HPV test crosses the threshold for colposcopy, whereas ASC-US with a negative HPV test can often be followed with surveillance.
ASC-US is the mildest cytologic abnormality and is not cancer; only histology from colposcopic biopsy can diagnose precancer or malignancy.
CIN 1 frequently regresses spontaneously, so it is typically observed with surveillance; excisional treatment is reserved for CIN 2/3 or persistent low-grade disease.

cases.title: Collapse at the Finish of a Summer Race
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in by EMS after collapsing and becoming confused near the end of a marathon on a hot day.
cases.history: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by EMS after collapsing near mile 24 of a marathon during a heat advisory with ambient temperature 35°C and high humidity. Bystanders report he became confused and combative before collapsing. He is a recreational runner who reportedly drank only water and had not acclimatized to the heat. Per a friend, he has no significant past medical history, takes no regular medications, and has no known drug allergies. He uses no stimulants or supplements that the friend knows of, though the friend mentions he 'took something for energy' before the race. He does not use insulin. There is no known seizure history. He drinks alcohol occasionally and does not use illicit drugs regularly. Family history is unknown. On arrival he is disoriented and cannot give a coherent history. EMS measured a rectal temperature of 41.2°C in the field and began moving him out of the sun. He has not been cooled aggressively yet.
cases.exam: Agitated, confused young man, GCS 13, not following commands consistently. Skin is hot; he is diaphoretic. Mucous membranes dry. Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft. No focal neurologic deficits, but he is disoriented to time and place with intermittent agitation. No neck stiffness. No rash or petechiae. Pupils equal and reactive. No obvious head trauma. Capillary refill slightly delayed.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature distinguishes heat stroke from heat exhaustion in this patient?
options: A core temperature of 38.5°C with normal mentation
Core temperature above 40°C with central nervous system dysfunction (confusion, altered mental status)
Muscle cramps with normal cognition after exertion
Profuse sweating with a normal mental status
stages.explanation: Heat stroke is defined by a core temperature typically >40°C together with central nervous system dysfunction (confusion, agitation, seizures, or coma)—both present here—so this is the answer. That option describes heat exhaustion, where temperature is usually below 40°C and mentation is preserved. Another option describes heat cramps, a milder heat illness with intact cognition. A third option fits heat exhaustion as well; importantly, sweating may persist in exertional heat stroke (unlike the classic teaching of dry skin in nonexertional cases), so the presence of sweat does not exclude heat stroke. The combination of hyperthermia and encephalopathy is the defining feature and a medical emergency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed sepsis
Give an antipyretic such as acetaminophen
Begin rapid whole-body cooling, ideally with cold-water immersion
Obtain a CT head before any other treatment
stages.explanation: Immediate, aggressive cooling is the priority in heat stroke because the duration of hyperthermia drives mortality; cold-water immersion achieves the fastest cooling rate and should begin at once with a target of roughly 38.5–39°C—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because the fever is from environmental heat, not infection; antibiotics do not lower core temperature, though sepsis can be evaluated concurrently. Another option is ineffective: antipyretics work by lowering the hypothalamic set-point in infectious fever and do not help environmental hyperthermia, and they may add hepatotoxicity in an already-stressed liver. A third option wastes time—imaging should not delay cooling, the definitive lifesaving treatment in an unstable hyperthermic patient.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 148 mEq/L
K 5.4 mEq/L
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (elevated)
Creatine kinase 9,800 U/L (markedly elevated)
AST 320 U/L, ALT 280 U/L
INR 1.6; platelets 95 ×10³/µL
Lactate 4.8 mmol/L
Glucose 92 mg/dL
Urinalysis: dark, positive for blood on dipstick with few RBCs on microscopy (myoglobinuria)

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The labs reveal a markedly elevated CK with myoglobinuria and rising creatinine. What complication is developing, and how should it be addressed?
options: Rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury; treat with aggressive IV fluid resuscitation and monitoring
Diabetic ketoacidosis; start an insulin drip
Acute hemolytic anemia; transfuse packed red cells
Contrast-induced nephropathy; restrict all fluids
stages.explanation: A CK near 10,000 U/L with myoglobinuria (dipstick blood positive but few RBCs) and a rising creatinine indicates rhabdomyolysis causing acute kidney injury—a hallmark complication of heat stroke—best managed with aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation to maintain urine output and clear myoglobin, plus monitoring of potassium and renal function—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because glucose is normal and there is no ketoacidosis. Another option is incorrect; the dipstick positivity reflects myoglobin, not hemolysis, and there is no evidence of significant anemia or hemolytic markers. A third option is the opposite of correct care—fluid restriction would worsen pigment nephropathy; he needs generous fluids, not restriction.

stages.label: COOLING ENDPOINT
stages.question: During active cooling, at what point should aggressive cooling measures be stopped to avoid overshoot?
options: When the core temperature reaches approximately 38.5–39°C
Only after the temperature reaches 35°C
As soon as cooling begins, regardless of temperature
Cooling should continue uninterrupted until the patient is fully alert
stages.explanation: Aggressive cooling should be discontinued once the core temperature falls to roughly 38.5–39°C to prevent overshoot hypothermia, since continued immersion or cooling beyond this point risks driving the patient hypothermic—so this is the answer. That option is wrong because cooling to 35°C overshoots the target and induces iatrogenic hypothermia with its own complications such as arrhythmia and coagulopathy. Another option is incorrect; stopping at the onset of cooling would leave the patient dangerously hyperthermic, defeating the purpose. A third option conflates mental status with the thermal endpoint—encephalopathy may persist after the temperature normalizes, and using alertness rather than core temperature as the stop point would cause harmful overcooling. Core temperature, not mental status, guides when to stop.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After initial cooling and resuscitation, what is the appropriate disposition and ongoing concern?
options: Discharge home once he is alert, with no further monitoring
Admit to the ICU for monitoring of multi-organ injury, coagulopathy, and electrolytes
Transfer to outpatient follow-up the same day
Observe for two hours and discharge if temperature normalizes
stages.explanation: Heat stroke can cause multi-organ dysfunction—including AKI from rhabdomyolysis, hepatic injury, and DIC (here suggested by an INR of 1.6 and falling platelets)—that evolves over hours to days, so ICU admission for close monitoring of organ function, coagulation, and electrolytes is appropriate—this is correct. That option is unsafe because apparent neurologic recovery does not exclude ongoing renal, hepatic, or hematologic deterioration. Another option inappropriately defers care for a critically ill patient with laboratory evidence of multi-organ involvement. A third option underestimates the risk: normalization of temperature does not mean the systemic complications have resolved, and premature discharge could be fatal.

pearls: Heat stroke = core temperature typically >40°C plus CNS dysfunction; in exertional heat stroke the skin is often still sweaty, so moist skin does not rule it out.
Cool first, transport second—rapid cooling (cold-water immersion is fastest) to ~38.5–39°C is the priority; antipyretics do not work for environmental hyperthermia.
Watch for multi-organ complications: rhabdomyolysis with AKI, hepatic injury, and DIC—treat rhabdomyolysis with aggressive IV fluids, not fluid restriction.
Critically ill heat-stroke patients warrant ICU admission because organ injury and coagulopathy can progress for hours to days after the core temperature normalizes.

cases.title: An Itchy Ring on a Wrestler's Forearm
cases.chiefComplaint: An itchy, expanding circular rash on the forearm for about two weeks.
cases.history: A 15-year-old high school wrestler presents with an itchy patch on his right forearm that started as a small red spot two weeks ago and has slowly enlarged into a ring shape. The center has cleared somewhat while the border stays red and scaly. He notes mild itching, worse after practice. He has had no fever, joint pain, or systemic symptoms. He shares mats, towels, and headgear with teammates, and a teammate recently had a similar rash that the trainer called 'ringworm.' He showers after practice but does not always wash his gear. Past medical history is notable only for mild atopic dermatitis as a child. He takes no daily medications and has no drug allergies. He plays year-round club wrestling and works part-time at a pet store handling kittens. Family history is unremarkable. He denies new soaps, detergents, or topical products and has not applied anything to the lesion. He is otherwise healthy, up to date on vaccinations, and has had no prior skin infections requiring treatment.
cases.exam: Well-appearing adolescent in no distress. On the dorsal right forearm there is a single 4 cm annular, erythematous plaque with a raised, scaly, advancing border and relative central clearing. No vesicles, pustules, or honey-colored crusting. No fluctuance, warmth, or surrounding cellulitis. No similar lesions on the scalp, trunk, groin, or feet on full skin survey. No regional lymphadenopathy. Nails are normal without pitting or onycholysis. Remainder of the skin and general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this lesion most strongly suggests a dermatophyte (fungal) infection rather than another annular dermatosis?
options: Complete absence of any scale within the plaque
A raised, actively scaling, advancing border with central clearing
Symmetric distribution over both forearms
Honey-colored crusting at the lesion center
stages.explanation: An advancing scaly border with central clearing ('ring' morphology) is the classic appearance of tinea corporis, where the dermatophyte spreads centrifugally and scale concentrates at the active edge. Absence of any scale argues against tinea, which is typically a scaly process; lesions lacking scale suggest granuloma annulare or nummular eczema. Symmetric bilateral distribution is more typical of an inflammatory or allergic process than a contagious fungal infection acquired focally. Honey-colored crusting indicates impetigo (Staphylococcus/Streptococcus), not a dermatophyte. The contact-sport exposure and shared equipment further support tinea corporis gladiatorum.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis at the point of care?
options: Skin biopsy with hematoxylin and eosin staining
Bacterial culture of a swab from the lesion
KOH (potassium hydroxide) preparation of skin scrapings from the active border
Patch testing for contact allergens
stages.explanation: A KOH preparation of scrapings taken from the scaly advancing edge is the quick, inexpensive, in-office test of choice; it dissolves keratinocytes and reveals branching, septate hyphae confirming a dermatophyte. Skin biopsy is invasive and reserved for atypical or refractory cases. Bacterial culture would be appropriate for suspected impetigo or abscess, not a dry annular plaque. Patch testing evaluates allergic contact dermatitis and takes days; it would not identify a fungus and is not indicated when morphology and exposure point to tinea.

stages.label: POINT-OF-CARE TESTING
stages.content:
KOH prep (scrapings from active border): numerous branching, septate hyphae visible
Wood's lamp examination: no fluorescence
No bacterial culture obtained
Full skin survey: no scalp or nail involvement

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a single localized plaque of tinea corporis, what is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical antifungal such as terbinafine or clotrimazole applied to the lesion and a margin of normal skin
Oral terbinafine for 6 weeks
Topical high-potency corticosteroid (clobetasol) twice daily
Oral cephalexin for 10 days
stages.explanation: Localized tinea corporis responds well to a topical antifungal (an allylamine like terbinafine or an azole like clotrimazole) applied once to twice daily for 2-4 weeks, extending about 2 cm onto surrounding normal skin and continuing about a week after clearance. Oral antifungals are reserved for extensive disease, tinea capitis, onychomycosis, or treatment failure, not a single small plaque. Topical corticosteroids alone worsen dermatophyte infection (tinea incognito) by suppressing local immunity. Oral cephalexin treats bacterial skin infection, not a confirmed fungal lesion.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What counseling is most important to prevent spread and recurrence in this wrestler?
options: He may continue full-contact practice immediately with no restrictions
Permanently stop wrestling because the infection cannot be cured
Cover or treat the lesion and withhold from skin-to-skin competition until adequately treated, plus disinfect mats and avoid sharing gear
Prophylactically treat all household members with oral antifungals
stages.explanation: Tinea corporis gladiatorum spreads through skin contact and contaminated mats/equipment, so the athlete should withhold from contact competition until adequately treated (commonly about 72 hours of therapy with the lesion covered, per athletic guidelines), keep lesions covered, regularly disinfect mats, and avoid sharing towels and gear. Returning to full contact immediately risks transmitting infection to teammates. Permanent cessation is unnecessary; tinea is curable. Empiric oral antifungals for asymptomatic household members is not indicated; treat individuals only if they develop lesions.

pearls: Tinea corporis classically shows an annular plaque with a raised scaly advancing border and central clearing.
Confirm with a KOH prep from the active edge showing branching septate hyphae before committing to therapy.
Topical allylamine or azole antifungals cure localized disease; reserve oral agents for extensive or refractory cases or tinea capitis.
Topical steroids cause 'tinea incognito' and should be avoided; counsel wrestlers on mat hygiene and return-to-play rules.

cases.title: A Red, Watery Eye with the Sniffles
cases.chiefComplaint: Redness and watery discharge in both eyes for two days during a cold.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl is brought in by her mother for red, watery eyes that began two days ago. The redness started in the right eye and spread to the left the next day. The discharge is thin and watery rather than thick; her eyelashes are not glued shut in the morning, though there is mild crusting. She reports a gritty, 'sandy' sensation but denies significant pain or light sensitivity. For the past four days she has had a runny nose, mild sore throat, low-grade fevers, and a cough, and two classmates have had similar red eyes. Vision is unchanged and she has no eye trauma or contact lens use. Past medical history is unremarkable; she is fully immunized. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She lives with both parents and a younger sibling and attends first grade. There is no family history of eye disease. Her mother has been wiping her eyes with tissues and is worried the school will require treatment before she can return.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful child. Bilateral diffuse conjunctival injection with watery discharge and mild lid edema; eyelashes not matted. Cornea clear with no fluorescein uptake; no ciliary flush or hypopyon. Pupils equal, round, reactive; extraocular movements intact; visual acuity grossly normal. A small, tender preauricular lymph node is palpable on the right. Nasal mucosa is erythematous with clear rhinorrhea; oropharynx mildly injected without exudate. No periorbital warmth, proptosis, or pain with eye movement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings best supports a viral rather than bacterial cause of this conjunctivitis?
options: Thick purulent discharge with lashes glued shut all day
Watery discharge, a concurrent URI, and a palpable preauricular node
Severe eye pain with photophobia and decreased visual acuity
Unilateral involvement that never spreads to the other eye
stages.explanation: Viral conjunctivitis typically causes watery (serous) discharge, frequently accompanies an upper respiratory infection, spreads from one eye to the other, and is often associated with a tender preauricular lymph node, all present here. Thick copious purulent discharge with persistent matting suggests bacterial conjunctivitis. Severe pain, photophobia, and decreased acuity are red flags for keratitis, iritis, or angle-closure, not simple conjunctivitis, and warrant urgent evaluation. Conjunctivitis that remains strictly unilateral is less typical of the contagious viral form, which commonly becomes bilateral.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach in this otherwise healthy child?
options: Routine viral and bacterial conjunctival cultures for every case
Clinical diagnosis based on history and examination
Immediate slit-lamp examination and intraocular pressure measurement
CT of the orbits to exclude orbital cellulitis
stages.explanation: Acute conjunctivitis in an otherwise well child is a clinical diagnosis; the watery discharge, URI symptoms, and preauricular node point to a viral etiology without need for testing. Routine cultures are reserved for severe, recurrent, or treatment-resistant cases, neonatal conjunctivitis, or contact lens wearers. Slit-lamp examination and IOP measurement are warranted only when red flags (pain, photophobia, vision loss) suggest deeper ocular pathology. Orbital CT is indicated for suspected orbital cellulitis (proptosis, pain with eye movement, restricted motility, systemic toxicity), none of which are present.

stages.label: FOCUSED EYE EXAM
stages.content:
Fluorescein staining: no corneal uptake (no dendrites or abrasion)
Visual acuity: normal for age
Pupils: equal, round, reactive; no photophobia on exam
No proptosis, restricted eye movement, or periorbital edema
No cultures obtained

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this presumed viral conjunctivitis?
options: Topical fluoroquinolone drops four times daily for 7 days
Oral acyclovir for 10 days
Topical corticosteroid drops to reduce redness
Supportive care with cool compresses, artificial tears, and strict hygiene
stages.explanation: Viral conjunctivitis is self-limited and managed supportively with cool compresses, lubricating artificial tears, and meticulous hand and towel hygiene to limit spread; it typically resolves over 1-2 weeks. Topical antibiotics do not treat a virus and are reserved for bacterial conjunctivitis; overuse promotes resistance and allergy. Oral acyclovir is for herpetic eye disease (dendritic keratitis with branching fluorescein uptake), which is absent here. Topical corticosteroids can worsen undiagnosed herpetic infection and cause complications; they should not be prescribed empirically in primary care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The mother asks when her daughter can return to school. What is the best guidance?
options: She must stay home until all redness and discharge have completely resolved
She can return once she feels well enough, with good hand hygiene; antibiotics are not required for return
She needs a documented negative viral culture before returning
She should be excluded from school for a minimum of three weeks
stages.explanation: Most guidelines allow children with viral conjunctivitis to return to school when they feel well enough to participate, emphasizing hand washing and not sharing towels, since the illness is highly contagious but self-limited; mandatory antibiotic treatment before return is not evidence-based. Requiring complete resolution of all redness would keep her out unnecessarily for up to two weeks. A negative viral culture is neither routinely obtained nor required. A fixed three-week exclusion has no basis and is excessive. Counsel on hygiene and expected course.

pearls: Watery discharge, a concurrent URI, and a tender preauricular node point to viral conjunctivitis; thick purulent discharge with matting suggests bacterial.
Pain, photophobia, or decreased vision are red flags warranting urgent ophthalmologic evaluation, not simple conjunctivitis.
Acute conjunctivitis in a well child is a clinical diagnosis; reserve cultures for severe, recurrent, neonatal, or contact-lens cases.
Manage viral conjunctivitis supportively and counsel strict hand hygiene; avoid empiric topical steroids that can worsen herpetic disease.

cases.title: Tired and Pale with Heavy Periods
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of worsening fatigue and feeling run-down.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents with about four months of progressive fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and occasional lightheadedness when standing. She also reports difficulty concentrating at work and unusual cravings to chew ice. She has no chest pain, but notes mild exertional shortness of breath climbing stairs. On review of systems, she describes heavy menstrual periods for the past two years, soaking through a pad every two hours on her heaviest days and passing clots, lasting seven days. She has no melena, hematochezia, or unexplained weight loss. Past medical history is unremarkable; she has two children and no surgeries. She takes no regular medications other than occasional ibuprofen for cramps and has no allergies. She follows a varied omnivorous diet, drinks alcohol socially, and does not smoke. Family history includes a mother with 'low iron.' She has never been evaluated for her heavy bleeding. She works as an accountant and is otherwise healthy, with no recent illnesses or known bleeding disorders.
cases.exam: Alert woman appearing mildly fatigued. Conjunctival pallor noted; skin without jaundice or petechiae. Nail beds pale; mild koilonychia (spooning) of several fingernails. Mucous membranes moist with no glossitis. Cardiac exam reveals a soft I/VI systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border without gallops; lungs clear. Abdomen soft, nontender, no hepatosplenomegaly or masses. No lower-extremity edema. Neurologic exam grossly intact without sensory deficits. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory test to evaluate her fatigue?
options: Complete blood count with red cell indices
Peripheral blood smear only
Bone marrow biopsy
Serum vitamin B12 and folate levels
stages.explanation: A CBC with red cell indices is the appropriate first step; it confirms anemia and characterizes it by mean corpuscular volume (MCV), which guides further workup. Her symptoms, pallor, koilonychia, and pica strongly suggest iron deficiency, and microcytosis on the CBC would support this. A peripheral smear can be informative but is secondary to the CBC. Bone marrow biopsy is invasive and reserved for unexplained cytopenias or suspected marrow disease, not a straightforward presentation.
