IV loop diuretics are first-line for acute decompensated HF with volume overload; avoid IV fluids and negative inotropes like non-dihydropyridine CCBs in HFrEF.
Four-pillar GDMT for HFrEF (ARNI/ACEi-ARB, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2 inhibitor) reduces mortality; start/optimize once stabilized.
Common precipitants: dietary sodium indiscretion, diuretic nonadherence, ischemia, arrhythmia, and infection.

cases.title: Fever and One-Sided Back Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of right-sided flank pain with fever and painful urination.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents to the ED with two days of right flank pain, fevers, and shaking chills. She first noticed dysuria, urinary frequency, and urgency about four days ago, which she attributed to a bladder infection, but over the past two days the pain migrated to her right back and she developed temperatures to 39 C, nausea, and one episode of vomiting. She denies vaginal discharge, abnormal bleeding, or recent sexual activity changes; her last menstrual period was two weeks ago and a urine pregnancy test today is negative. She has had two prior uncomplicated cystitis episodes. No known structural urinary abnormalities or stones. PMH otherwise unremarkable; she takes only a daily multivitamin and is on no chronic medications. Allergies: none. She does not smoke, drinks socially, and works as a teacher. Family history noncontributory. She is able to tolerate sips of water but feels weak and flushed. She has not taken any antibiotics yet.
cases.exam: Flushed and mildly ill-appearing but alert and oriented. Marked right costovertebral angle tenderness to percussion; left CVA nontender. Abdomen soft with mild right-sided and suprapubic tenderness, no rebound or guarding, normoactive bowel sounds. No palpable masses. Cardiac tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs clear. No rash. Pelvic exam (performed) without cervical motion tenderness, adnexal mass, or discharge. Extremities warm and well perfused; capillary refill normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding best distinguishes her current presentation from simple lower-tract cystitis?
options: Dysuria and urinary frequency
Suprapubic tenderness
Fever, flank pain, and costovertebral angle tenderness
A negative pregnancy test
stages.explanation: Fever, flank pain, and costovertebral angle tenderness signal upper-tract infection (pyelonephritis) rather than simple cystitis. Dysuria and frequency are lower urinary tract symptoms common to both cystitis and pyelonephritis and do not distinguish them. Suprapubic tenderness reflects bladder involvement and can occur in cystitis alone. A negative pregnancy test is important for management and drug selection but does not differentiate upper from lower tract disease. The systemic signs (high fever, rigors, nausea/vomiting) plus CVA tenderness define acute pyelonephritis, which requires more aggressive treatment than uncomplicated cystitis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
Urinalysis: positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, >50 WBC/hpf, many bacteria, WBC casts present
Lactate 1.4 mmol/L
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Urine culture: pending
Blood cultures: drawn, pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is tolerating oral fluids, hemodynamically stable, and not pregnant. Which empiric antibiotic approach is most appropriate if she is to be managed as an outpatient?
options: Oral nitrofurantoin for 5 days
A fluoroquinolone (e.g., oral ciprofloxacin) or an initial IV/IM dose of a long-acting agent (e.g., ceftriaxone) followed by oral therapy
Oral fosfomycin single dose
Topical estrogen and increased fluids only
stages.explanation: For outpatient pyelonephritis, an oral fluoroquinolone such as ciprofloxacin is a guideline-endorsed first-line option where local resistance is low; alternatively, an initial parenteral long-acting agent (ceftriaxone) or an aminoglycoside dose can be given, followed by an oral agent guided by susceptibilities. Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin achieve poor renal parenchymal/tissue concentrations and are appropriate for cystitis only, not pyelonephritis. Topical estrogen with fluids does not treat an active upper-tract bacterial infection. Cultures were obtained to allow later narrowing of therapy, which is important given rising fluoroquinolone and beta-lactam resistance.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor would most strongly favor hospital admission with IV antibiotics rather than outpatient oral therapy?
options: Two prior episodes of uncomplicated cystitis
Persistent vomiting precluding oral intake, hemodynamic instability, or signs of sepsis/obstruction
A temperature of 39 C at presentation
Mild right CVA tenderness
stages.explanation: Admission for IV antibiotics is warranted when patients cannot tolerate oral intake (intractable vomiting), are hemodynamically unstable or septic, are pregnant, have obstruction/urolithiasis or another complicating factor, or fail to improve on outpatient therapy. Her prior cystitis episodes do not by themselves mandate admission. A fever of 39 C is expected in pyelonephritis and alone does not require admission if the patient is otherwise stable and tolerating fluids. Mild CVA tenderness is part of the diagnosis but not an admission criterion. Because she is stable, tolerating oral fluids, and not pregnant, outpatient management with close follow-up is reasonable; deterioration or persistent vomiting would change the disposition.

pearls: Fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness with pyuria/bacteriuria (and WBC casts) define pyelonephritis; dysuria/frequency overlap with cystitis.
Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin do NOT achieve adequate renal tissue levels and should not be used for pyelonephritis.
Outpatient options: oral fluoroquinolone, or an initial IV/IM long-acting dose (ceftriaxone) then oral, with culture-guided narrowing.
Admit for inability to tolerate oral intake, sepsis/instability, pregnancy, obstruction/stone, or failure to improve; obtain urine (and often blood) cultures.

cases.title: Spreading Red, Warm Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: An expanding area of redness, warmth, and pain on the lower leg.
cases.history: A 55-year-old warehouse worker presents to clinic with three days of progressively spreading redness, warmth, swelling, and tenderness over his right lower leg. He recalls scratching the area on a metal shelf about five days ago and noticed a small superficial abrasion. Since then the redness has expanded with an advancing, somewhat irregular border, and the area feels hot and tender. He has had subjective low-grade fever and chills today but no nausea, and he denies any drainage of pus, fluctuance, or a discrete abscess. He reports no severe pain out of proportion, no rapidly advancing margins over hours, no skin necrosis, blistering, or crepitus. PMH: type 2 diabetes (last A1c 7.8%) and obesity. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily. Allergies: none. He does not smoke; drinks occasional beer. No recent travel, freshwater exposure, or animal bites. Family history noncontributory. He is up to date on tetanus.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-appearing. Right lower leg with a confluent area of erythema (~10 x 8 cm) over the anterior shin, warm and tender to palpation, with mild edema and an advancing but well-demarcated margin; the border is marked with a skin marker. A small healing abrasion is present centrally. No fluctuance, no pointing abscess, no purulent drainage, no bullae, no skin necrosis, and no crepitus. Pain is proportional to exam findings. Dorsalis pedis pulse intact; no lymphangitic streaking; small tender inguinal node. No calf cord or asymmetric swelling suggesting DVT.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most reassures you that this is uncomplicated cellulitis rather than a necrotizing soft-tissue infection?
options: The presence of a small healing abrasion
Pain proportional to exam findings, no crepitus, no bullae, no skin necrosis, and absence of rapidly advancing margins
Low-grade fever of 37.8 C
Tender inguinal lymphadenopathy
stages.explanation: Pain proportionate to exam, with no crepitus, hemorrhagic bullae, skin necrosis, or rapidly progressing margins, argues against a necrotizing soft-tissue infection, which is a surgical emergency. The hallmark warning signs of necrotizing infection are pain out of proportion to findings, rapid progression over hours, systemic toxicity, crepitus, bullae, and dusky/necrotic skin. A healing abrasion is just the likely portal of entry. Low-grade fever is common in uncomplicated cellulitis and is nonspecific. Tender inguinal lymphadenopathy reflects regional immune response and supports infection but does not distinguish simple from necrotizing disease. His benign exam supports outpatient management of uncomplicated cellulitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: There is no abscess, fluctuance, or purulent drainage. Which empiric oral antibiotic best targets the most likely pathogen for nonpurulent cellulitis?
options: Cephalexin (targets beta-hemolytic streptococci and MSSA)
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole alone
Metronidazole
Oral vancomycin
stages.explanation: Nonpurulent cellulitis is most often caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci (and methicillin-sensitive S. aureus), so an agent such as cephalexin (or dicloxacillin) is first-line empiric therapy. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and doxycycline cover MRSA and are favored for PURULENT cellulitis/abscess, but TMP-SMX alone has weaker streptococcal coverage, making it suboptimal for nonpurulent disease. Metronidazole covers anaerobes and does not treat the relevant gram-positive cocci. Oral vancomycin is not systemically absorbed (used for C. difficile) and would not treat a skin infection. Marking the border and arranging follow-up to confirm improvement is essential.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
48-hour follow-up:
Erythema has not advanced beyond the marked border; some areas slightly improved
Temperature 37.0 °C, feels better, eating normally
No new fluctuance, drainage, or bullae; pain decreasing
Glucose log: morning readings 150-170 mg/dL

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which scenario would most appropriately prompt a change to MRSA-active coverage or escalation of care?
options: The marked border showing no further advancement at 48 hours
Development of a fluctuant, purulent abscess or systemic signs/failure to respond to beta-lactam therapy
Persistent mild residual erythema that is fading
A single low-grade temperature on day one
stages.explanation: Escalation to MRSA-active therapy (e.g., TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or clindamycin) is indicated when purulence/abscess develops, when there are systemic signs of toxicity, or when the patient fails to improve on adequate beta-lactam therapy; an abscess also requires incision and drainage, the key intervention. Lack of advancement beyond the marked border at 48 hours indicates a good response and reassures, not escalates. Fading residual erythema is expected during recovery and may even transiently look worse before improving, but improvement overall is reassuring. A single early low-grade fever is common and not a reason to change therapy if the patient is otherwise improving. His response at 48 hours suggests continuing the current regimen.

pearls: Nonpurulent cellulitis is usually streptococcal/MSSA: treat with cephalexin or dicloxacillin; purulent cellulitis/abscess needs MRSA coverage (TMP-SMX, doxycycline) plus incision and drainage.
Mark the erythema border and reassess; cellulitis may transiently look worse in the first 24-48 hours before improving.
Red flags for necrotizing infection: pain out of proportion, rapid progression, crepitus, hemorrhagic bullae, necrosis, systemic toxicity, surgical emergency.
Diabetes and obesity are risk factors; address the portal of entry (abrasion, tinea pedis, edema) and optimize glycemic control.

cases.title: Persistent Lower-Left Belly Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of steady left-lower-quadrant abdominal pain with low-grade fever.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents to clinic with three days of constant, aching left-lower-quadrant abdominal pain. The pain is moderate, has not migrated, and is associated with a low-grade fever, mild nausea, and a change in bowel habits with some loose stools alternating with constipation. She denies vomiting, hematochezia, melena, dysuria, or vaginal bleeding (she is postmenopausal). She has had occasional similar but milder episodes attributed to her bowels. Her diet is low in fiber. PMH: hypertension and prior screening colonoscopy three years ago showing diverticulosis but no polyps or malignancy. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily; she occasionally takes ibuprofen for knee pain. Allergies: none. She does not smoke and drinks wine on weekends. Family history: father with colon polyps. She is able to eat small amounts and is not severely distressed but the persistent pain prompted today's visit. She has had no recent antibiotics and no prior abdominal surgery.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest, mildly uncomfortable with movement. Abdomen soft, with localized tenderness in the left lower quadrant and mild voluntary guarding; no rebound tenderness, no rigidity, and no palpable mass. Bowel sounds present and normoactive. No CVA tenderness. No hernias. Rectal exam: no gross blood, no mass, normal tone. No peritoneal signs. Skin without jaundice. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. No lower-extremity edema.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given LLQ pain, low-grade fever, and a history of diverticulosis, what is the most likely diagnosis and the best confirmatory test?
options: Acute diverticulitis; confirm with CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast
Acute diverticulitis; confirm with urgent colonoscopy during the acute episode
Ovarian torsion; confirm with pelvic ultrasound
Irritable bowel syndrome; no imaging needed
stages.explanation: LLQ pain with low-grade fever and known diverticulosis strongly suggests acute (sigmoid) diverticulitis, and CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the confirmatory study of choice; it diagnoses diverticulitis, grades severity, and identifies complications (abscess, perforation, obstruction). Colonoscopy is contraindicated during acute diverticulitis because of perforation risk; it is performed 6-8 weeks after resolution to exclude malignancy. Ovarian torsion typically causes acute severe pain in a younger or ovulating patient and would not explain the bowel-habit change in a postmenopausal woman with diverticulosis. IBS does not cause fever or localized tenderness with guarding, so dismissing this as IBS without imaging would be unsafe.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 12.4 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL
CRP elevated
Urinalysis: unremarkable, negative pregnancy not applicable (postmenopausal)
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: sigmoid colon wall thickening with pericolic fat stranding and several diverticula; no abscess, free air, or obstruction

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms uncomplicated sigmoid diverticulitis (no abscess, perforation, or obstruction) and she tolerates oral intake. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Admit for IV antibiotics and bowel rest in all cases
Outpatient management with clear-liquid/low-residue diet, analgesia, and close follow-up, with selective (often individualized) antibiotic use
Urgent sigmoid colectomy
Begin a high-fiber diet immediately during the acute attack
stages.explanation: For mild, uncomplicated diverticulitis in an immunocompetent, clinically stable patient who tolerates oral intake, outpatient management is appropriate: dietary modification (clear liquids/low residue advancing as tolerated), analgesia, and close follow-up. Recent guidelines support selective rather than routine antibiotics in carefully chosen uncomplicated cases, though many patients with fever/leukocytosis or comorbidities are still treated with oral antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate, or ciprofloxacin plus metronidazole). Routine admission for IV antibiotics is unnecessary when she is stable and tolerating oral intake. Urgent colectomy is reserved for complications (perforation, obstruction, refractory abscess). A high-fiber diet helps prevent recurrence but is started after recovery, not during the acute attack, when bowel rest/low residue is preferred.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She recovers fully. Regarding follow-up after this first episode of uncomplicated diverticulitis confirmed on CT, which recommendation is most appropriate?
options: No further evaluation is ever needed
Colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after recovery if she has not had a recent quality colonoscopy, plus long-term high-fiber diet
Immediate repeat CT in one week regardless of symptoms
Prophylactic elective colectomy after a single uncomplicated episode
stages.explanation: After an episode of acute diverticulitis, interval colonoscopy roughly 6-8 weeks after recovery is recommended to exclude underlying malignancy, particularly if a high-quality colonoscopy has not been done recently; long-term prevention centers on a high-fiber diet. Saying no evaluation is needed risks missing an occult colorectal cancer that can mimic diverticulitis. Routine immediate repeat CT in an asymptomatic, recovering patient is unnecessary and adds radiation. Elective colectomy is not advised after a single uncomplicated episode; surgery is individualized and generally reserved for complicated disease or recurrent/refractory cases. Counseling on fiber, hydration, and limiting NSAID use is also appropriate.

pearls: LLQ pain, low-grade fever, and leukocytosis in a patient with diverticulosis = diverticulitis; confirm and stage with CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast.
Avoid colonoscopy during acute diverticulitis (perforation risk); perform it 6-8 weeks after recovery to exclude malignancy.
Uncomplicated, stable, tolerating PO: outpatient bowel rest/low-residue diet and analgesia; antibiotics are increasingly used selectively rather than universally.
Complications (abscess, perforation, obstruction, fistula) or clinical instability warrant admission, IV antibiotics, drainage, or surgery.

cases.title: Right Upper Belly Pain After Meals
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent right-upper-quadrant pain that comes on after fatty meals.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents to clinic with several weeks of recurrent right-upper-quadrant abdominal pain. Episodes typically begin 30-60 minutes after eating fatty or large meals, build to a steady, severe ache that sometimes radiates to the right shoulder blade, last 1-3 hours, and then resolve completely. She often feels nauseated during attacks and occasionally vomits. Between episodes she feels well. She denies fever, chills, jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, or persistent pain. The most recent episode last night lasted about two hours after a fried dinner and resolved before arrival. PMH: she is overweight and had two pregnancies; she takes a combined oral contraceptive. No prior abdominal surgery. Medications: oral contraceptive and occasional acetaminophen. Allergies: none. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history: mother had her gallbladder removed. She is concerned because attacks are becoming more frequent and limiting her diet. She is afebrile today and feels back to baseline.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable, in no distress between episodes. Abdomen soft, non-distended, with mild RUQ tenderness to deep palpation but no guarding or rebound. Negative Murphy sign today (no inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation). No palpable gallbladder or mass. No hepatomegaly. No scleral icterus; skin without jaundice. Bowel sounds normal. No CVA tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best supports uncomplicated biliary colic rather than acute cholecystitis?
options: Pain that is constant for more than 6 hours with fever and a positive Murphy sign
Self-limited postprandial RUQ pain lasting a few hours that fully resolves, with no fever and a negative Murphy sign
Jaundice with dark urine and pale stools
Right shoulder radiation alone
stages.explanation: Biliary colic is characterized by episodic, self-limited RUQ/epigastric pain (often postprandial, lasting up to a few hours) that resolves completely, without fever or sustained inflammation, and with a negative Murphy sign between attacks, exactly her presentation. Constant pain beyond ~4-6 hours with fever and a positive Murphy sign indicates acute cholecystitis (a stone impacted in the cystic duct causing inflammation). Jaundice with dark urine and pale stools suggests choledocholithiasis or biliary obstruction. Right shoulder radiation is a nonspecific feature of biliary pain (referred via the phrenic nerve) and occurs in both colic and cholecystitis, so it does not distinguish them.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Right upper quadrant abdominal ultrasound
Abdominal CT without contrast
MRCP
HIDA (cholescintigraphy) scan
stages.explanation: Transabdominal RUQ ultrasound is the first-line test: it is sensitive for gallstones, noninvasive, inexpensive, and radiation-free, and can assess for wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid if cholecystitis is suspected. CT is less sensitive for cholesterol stones (many are radiolucent) and adds radiation. MRCP is excellent for ductal stones/choledocholithiasis but is reserved for suspected common bile duct involvement, not first-line for simple biliary colic. A HIDA scan evaluates cystic duct patency/gallbladder function and is used when acute cholecystitis is suspected but ultrasound is equivocal, or for suspected biliary dyskinesia, not as the initial study here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CBC: normal WBC
Liver panel: AST/ALT normal, alkaline phosphatase normal, total bilirubin normal
Lipase: normal
RUQ ultrasound: multiple mobile gallstones; gallbladder wall normal thickness (3 mm); no pericholecystic fluid; common bile duct not dilated (4 mm); sonographic Murphy sign negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ultrasound confirms symptomatic cholelithiasis without cholecystitis or ductal dilation. What is the most appropriate management recommendation?
options: Reassure and observe only, since asymptomatic gallstones need no treatment
Refer for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cholelithiasis
Start ursodeoxycholic acid as definitive long-term therapy for all patients
Recommend urgent ERCP
stages.explanation: She has SYMPTOMATIC cholelithiasis (recurrent biliary colic with documented stones), so the definitive treatment is elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy, which prevents recurrent attacks and complications. Pure observation applies to ASYMPTOMATIC gallstones, which generally do not require intervention; she is clearly symptomatic and worsening. Ursodeoxycholic acid can slowly dissolve small cholesterol stones but is reserved for poor surgical candidates, has high recurrence rates, and is not first-line definitive therapy. Urgent ERCP is indicated for choledocholithiasis/biliary obstruction or cholangitis; her bilirubin and ducts are normal, so there is no current indication for ERCP. Dietary fat reduction can reduce attacks while awaiting surgery.

pearls: Biliary colic: episodic postprandial RUQ pain (often radiating to the right scapula) lasting a few hours, resolving fully, with no fever and a negative Murphy sign.
RUQ ultrasound is the first-line test for gallstones; HIDA is reserved for suspected cholecystitis with equivocal US or biliary dyskinesia.
Symptomatic cholelithiasis warrants elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy; asymptomatic gallstones are generally observed.
Sustained pain >4-6 hours with fever, leukocytosis, and a positive Murphy sign suggests acute cholecystitis; jaundice/elevated bilirubin suggests choledocholithiasis (consider MRCP/ERCP).

cases.title: Sudden Hot, Swollen Big Toe
cases.chiefComplaint: Excruciating overnight pain, redness, and swelling of the big toe joint.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents to clinic with the abrupt onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling at the base of his right big toe that woke him from sleep around 3 a.m. The joint is so tender that even the bedsheet touching it is unbearable. The pain peaked within hours and is the worst he has experienced. He recalls a similar but milder episode in the same toe a year ago that resolved over a week. He attended a wedding two days ago with heavy alcohol intake and a steak dinner. He denies trauma, fever, chills, urethral discharge, or rash. He has no prior diagnosed joint disease. PMH: hypertension and obesity; he was recently started on hydrochlorothiazide. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily and occasional acetaminophen. Allergies: none. He drinks several beers most weekends and eats a meat-rich diet. Family history: father had 'gout.' He works as an accountant. He has normal kidney function on prior labs and no history of peptic ulcer disease or anticoagulant use.
cases.exam: Well-appearing but uncomfortable, limping. Right first metatarsophalangeal joint is markedly swollen, erythematous, warm, and exquisitely tender with overlying shiny skin; even light touch elicits pain. Range of motion severely limited by pain. No other joints involved; knees, ankles, and wrists nontender with full range of motion. No tophi appreciated over ears, elbows, or other joints. No skin breaks, ulcers, or lymphangitic streaking. No fever or systemic toxicity. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which is the most definitive test to confirm the diagnosis and exclude the key alternative?
options: Serum uric acid level
Arthrocentesis with synovial fluid analysis for crystals, cell count, Gram stain, and culture
Plain radiograph of the foot
ESR and CRP
stages.explanation: Joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis is the gold standard: it demonstrates negatively birefringent, needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals in gout and, crucially, helps exclude septic arthritis via cell count, Gram stain, and culture, the can't-miss alternative. Serum uric acid can be normal or even low during an acute flare (urate is deposited in the joint), so a normal level does not exclude gout and a high level does not confirm it. Plain radiographs are often normal early and show classic erosions only in chronic gout. ESR and CRP are nonspecific inflammatory markers that cannot distinguish gout from infection. When aspiration is not feasible, a clinical diagnosis can be made in a classic podagra presentation.

stages.label: JOINT FLUID
stages.content:
Synovial fluid aspirate:
Appearance: cloudy yellow
WBC 28,000/µL (inflammatory range), predominantly neutrophils
Crystals: needle-shaped, negatively birefringent under polarized light
Gram stain: no organisms
Culture: pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Synovial fluid confirms monosodium urate crystals with no organisms. He has hypertension but no peptic ulcer disease, normal renal function, and no anticoagulants. What is the most appropriate first-line acute therapy?
options: Start allopurinol immediately at a high dose
An NSAID (e.g., indomethacin or naproxen) or colchicine; an oral glucocorticoid is an alternative
Oral antibiotics for presumed septic arthritis
Begin probenecid during the acute flare
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of an acute gout flare is an NSAID, colchicine, or a glucocorticoid; with his normal renal function and no ulcer or anticoagulation, an NSAID such as naproxen or indomethacin is reasonable, with colchicine or oral steroids as alternatives (steroids preferred in renal impairment or NSAID contraindications). Allopurinol should NOT be started during an acute flare because initiating urate-lowering therapy can prolong or worsen the attack; it is begun later. Antibiotics are unnecessary, the Gram stain is negative and crystals confirm gout. Probenecid is a uricosuric urate-lowering agent and, like allopurinol, is not started during an acute attack.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After the flare resolves, you consider long-term urate-lowering therapy. Which statement about timing and approach is correct?
options: Never start allopurinol; it causes more flares so urate-lowering therapy should be avoided
Start allopurinol after the acute flare settles, titrate to a uric acid target (typically <6 mg/dL), and co-prescribe flare prophylaxis (e.g., low-dose colchicine) during initiation
Stop his hydrochlorothiazide is unnecessary since diuretics do not affect urate
Start allopurinol at full target dose immediately with no prophylaxis
stages.explanation: Urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol is initiated after the acute flare resolves, started low and titrated up to a serum urate target (commonly <6 mg/dL, or <5 mg/dL with tophi), with concurrent anti-inflammatory prophylaxis (low-dose colchicine or NSAID) for several months because urate-lowering can paradoxically trigger flares early on. Avoiding urate-lowering therapy entirely is wrong for recurrent gout, tophi, or urate nephrolithiasis. Thiazide diuretics like hydrochlorothiazide RAISE serum urate and can precipitate gout, so reconsidering his antihypertensive (e.g., switching toward losartan, which is mildly uricosuric) is appropriate. Starting at full dose without titration or prophylaxis increases flare risk and adverse effects.

pearls: Acute gout: sudden, exquisitely painful monoarthritis, classically the first MTP (podagra); arthrocentesis showing negatively birefringent needle-shaped urate crystals confirms it and helps exclude septic arthritis.
Serum uric acid can be normal during an acute flare, do not rely on it to diagnose or exclude gout.
Treat acute flares with an NSAID, colchicine, or glucocorticoid; do NOT start or stop urate-lowering therapy during the flare.
Begin allopurinol after the flare, titrate to a urate target (<6 mg/dL) with colchicine/NSAID prophylaxis; thiazides and alcohol/purine-rich diets raise urate.

cases.title: Hands That Wake Her at Night
cases.chiefComplaint: Numbness and tingling in both hands that wake her from sleep.
cases.history: A 46-year-old administrative assistant presents with a 4-month history of nocturnal tingling and burning in both hands, worse on the right. She wakes several times nightly and shakes her hands to relieve the symptoms. Paresthesias involve the thumb, index, and middle fingers; she denies little-finger involvement. She drops objects more often and has trouble buttoning blouses and opening jars. Symptoms worsen while driving and holding a phone. She denies neck pain, arm radiation, weakness elsewhere, or bowel/bladder changes. PMH: hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, well controlled; type 2 diabetes diagnosed 1 year ago on metformin 1000 mg twice daily with last A1c 7.0%. She is gravida 2 para 2. No prior trauma or surgery. Allergies: none. Social: never smoker, rare alcohol, BMI 31. Heavy daily keyboard use. Family history: mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Review of systems negative for rash, dry eyes/mouth, fevers, or weight change. She has tried over-the-counter ibuprofen with minimal relief.
cases.exam: Alert and comfortable. Hands: no swelling, erythema, or deformity; full range of motion at wrists. Subtle thenar eminence flattening on the right; pinch strength mildly reduced on the right. Decreased light-touch and two-point discrimination over the palmar thumb, index, and middle fingers bilaterally, sparing the little finger and the thenar palm. Phalen maneuver reproduces paresthesias within 30 seconds bilaterally; Tinel sign positive at both wrists. Reverse Phalen positive. Neck range of motion full and painless; Spurling test negative. Biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No fasciculations.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly localizes this patient's symptoms to median nerve compression at the wrist rather than a C6 radiculopathy?
options: Symptoms worse at night and relieved by shaking the hands
Sparing of the little finger with sensory loss over the thumb, index, and middle fingers and a negative Spurling test
Bilateral involvement of the hands
Presence of thenar atrophy
stages.explanation: A median-nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger) that spares the little finger, combined with a negative Spurling test and intact neck movement, points to carpal tunnel syndrome rather than cervical radiculopathy. C6 radiculopathy typically causes neck pain radiating down the arm to the thumb, a reduced biceps/brachioradialis reflex, and a positive Spurling test. Nocturnal symptoms relieved by shaking (flick sign) are characteristic of CTS but also occur in other compressive neuropathies, so they localize less precisely. Bilateral involvement is common in CTS but does not distinguish it from systemic causes. Thenar atrophy indicates severity and chronicity but is a late sign, not a localizer between wrist and root.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis in this patient?
options: Order MRI of the cervical spine
Make the diagnosis clinically; reserve electrodiagnostic studies for atypical cases, suspected alternative diagnoses, or surgical planning
Obtain wrist radiographs
Check antinuclear antibody and rheumatoid factor
stages.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome is primarily a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic history and exam. Nerve conduction studies/EMG are not required for every patient; they are most useful when the presentation is atypical, another diagnosis is suspected, severity must be quantified, or surgery is being planned. Cervical MRI evaluates radiculopathy, which is already made unlikely by the exam. Wrist radiographs are reserved for trauma, deformity, or suspected arthritis and would not confirm CTS. ANA and RF screen for connective tissue disease and are not indicated without systemic features; her exam shows no synovitis. Given a classic presentation, empiric conservative management is appropriate while confirmatory testing is reserved for select situations.

stages.label: NERVE CONDUCTION STUDY
stages.content:
Because of early thenar atrophy and diabetes, electrodiagnostic testing was obtained.
Median distal motor latency (right): 5.8 ms (normal < 4.4)
Median sensory latency (right): 4.2 ms (prolonged)
Median-ulnar sensory latency difference: 1.1 ms (abnormal > 0.5)
Ulnar motor and sensory studies: normal
Needle EMG: mild chronic denervation in right abductor pollicis brevis
Impression: moderate bilateral median neuropathy at the wrist, worse on the right

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her symptoms are bothersome but she has only mild sensory loss and no fixed motor deficit. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Immediate surgical carpal tunnel release
Nocturnal neutral-position wrist splinting, with consideration of a corticosteroid injection if inadequate relief
Long-term oral corticosteroids
Oral gabapentin as monotherapy
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderate CTS without progressive motor deficit, first-line therapy is a neutral-position wrist splint worn at night (and during provocative activities), which reduces intracarpal pressure. A local corticosteroid injection provides additional short-to-intermediate-term relief and is a reasonable next step if splinting is insufficient. Surgical release is indicated for severe disease, denervation with motor weakness/atrophy, or failure of conservative measures, not as initial therapy in mild cases. Oral corticosteroids may offer brief benefit but carry systemic risk—especially problematic given her diabetes—and are not a durable strategy. Gabapentin has not shown meaningful benefit in CTS and is not recommended as primary treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After 10 weeks of diligent splinting and one steroid injection, she now reports worsening numbness, more thenar atrophy, and weakness opening jars. What is the best next step?
options: Refer for surgical carpal tunnel release
Repeat the corticosteroid injection every month indefinitely
Switch to daytime-only splinting
Begin physical therapy with ultrasound and discharge
stages.explanation: Progressive thenar atrophy and motor weakness despite adequate conservative therapy signal a failed trial and risk of permanent nerve damage, making surgical carpal tunnel release the appropriate referral. Open or endoscopic release reliably relieves symptoms and halts progression. Repeated frequent steroid injections offer diminishing returns and risk tendon and nerve injury, so monthly injections are inappropriate. Switching to daytime-only splinting reduces, rather than intensifies, treatment for worsening disease. Therapeutic ultrasound has limited evidence and would not address advancing motor loss; discharging her would allow irreversible deficits. Persistent or progressive motor findings are a key indication to escalate from conservative care to surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Carpal tunnel syndrome (median neuropathy at the wrist)
pearls: Classic CTS spares the little finger and the thenar palm (palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to the tunnel), helping distinguish it from C8/ulnar lesions.
Diagnosis is clinical; reserve nerve conduction studies for atypical cases, severity grading, or surgical planning.
First-line treatment for mild-to-moderate CTS is a nocturnal neutral wrist splint, with steroid injection as a second step.
Thenar atrophy or progressive motor weakness is an indication for surgical release to prevent permanent deficit.

cases.title: An Unexpected Result on the Glucose Test
cases.chiefComplaint: Abnormal screening glucose result during routine prenatal care.
cases.history: A 31-year-old woman, gravida 2 para 1, at 27 weeks gestation presents for follow-up after an abnormal 1-hour 50-g glucose challenge test of 168 mg/dL drawn at her last visit. She feels well and denies polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision, or vaginal symptoms. The pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated; fetal movement is normal. Her first child, born 3 years ago, weighed 4.3 kg (9 lb 8 oz) at term. PMH: obesity (pre-pregnancy BMI 33), no known diabetes. Meds: prenatal vitamin and ferrous sulfate. Allergies: none. Social: never smoker, no alcohol during pregnancy, works as a teacher. Family history: father and sister with type 2 diabetes. She has gained 9 kg this pregnancy. Diet is high in refined carbohydrates and sweetened beverages; she is sedentary. Review of systems is otherwise negative. Blood pressure has been normal throughout, and prior urine dipsticks showed no protein or glucose. She is anxious about what the abnormal test means for her baby.
cases.exam: Well-appearing gravid woman in no distress. Fundal height appropriate for 27 weeks. Fetal heart tones 148 bpm by Doppler. No edema beyond trace pretibial. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. No acanthosis nigricans noted at the neck. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric without clonus. Skin without rashes. Abdomen gravid, soft, nontender, with no contractions palpated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the abnormal 1-hour 50-g glucose challenge test, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Diagnose gestational diabetes and start insulin now
Repeat the 1-hour 50-g challenge in 4 weeks
Check hemoglobin A1c and treat if above 6.5%
Perform a diagnostic 3-hour 100-g oral glucose tolerance test
stages.explanation: In the common two-step screening approach, an abnormal 1-hour 50-g screen (threshold typically 130-140 mg/dL) is followed by a diagnostic 3-hour 100-g oral glucose tolerance test; two or more elevated values confirm gestational diabetes. A single abnormal screen does not establish the diagnosis, so starting insulin now is premature. Simply repeating the 1-hour screen delays diagnosis and is not the standard pathway. Hemoglobin A1c is not the diagnostic test for GDM—pregnancy alters red cell turnover, and the diagnosis rests on glucose tolerance testing—so an A1c cutoff of 6.5% does not apply here. The next correct step is the confirmatory 3-hour OGTT.

stages.label: 3-HOUR 100-g OGTT
stages.content:
Fasting glucose: 99 mg/dL (threshold ≥ 95)
1-hour: 192 mg/dL (threshold ≥ 180)
2-hour: 168 mg/dL (threshold ≥ 155)
3-hour: 130 mg/dL (threshold ≥ 140)
Three values meet or exceed Carpenter-Coustan thresholds
Urinalysis: no protein, no ketones
HbA1c (incidental): 5.6%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How should these results be interpreted?
options: Normal test; reassure and continue routine care
Gestational diabetes is confirmed because two or more values meet or exceed the thresholds
Pre-existing (overt) type 2 diabetes given the fasting value
Impaired glucose tolerance not requiring treatment in pregnancy
stages.explanation: On the 3-hour 100-g OGTT, two or more values at or above the Carpenter-Coustan thresholds confirm gestational diabetes; here three values qualify. The test is therefore not normal, and reassurance alone would be inappropriate. Overt (pre-existing) diabetes is suggested by very high early-pregnancy fasting glucose (≥126 mg/dL), random ≥200 mg/dL, or A1c ≥6.5%—her fasting of 99 mg/dL and A1c of 5.6% do not meet those criteria, so this is gestational, not overt, diabetes. "Impaired glucose tolerance not requiring treatment" understates the diagnosis; confirmed GDM warrants treatment to reduce macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, and neonatal hypoglycemia. The correct interpretation is confirmed gestational diabetes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management after confirming gestational diabetes?
options: Start basal-bolus insulin immediately
Begin a sulfonylurea (glyburide) as first-line therapy
Medical nutrition therapy, exercise, and self-monitoring of blood glucose, reassessing glycemic targets in 1-2 weeks
Recommend a strict ketogenic diet
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of gestational diabetes is lifestyle modification: medical nutrition therapy (controlled carbohydrate distribution), regular moderate exercise, and self-monitoring of blood glucose (fasting and 1- or 2-hour postprandial). Most women achieve targets with these measures alone. Pharmacotherapy is added only if glycemic goals are not met after a 1-2 week trial, so immediate insulin is not the initial step. Insulin—not a sulfonylurea—is the preferred pharmacologic agent when medication becomes necessary; glyburide and metformin cross the placenta and are considered alternatives, not first-line drugs. A ketogenic diet is inappropriate in pregnancy because of the risk of starvation ketosis and inadequate fetal substrate. The correct first step is structured lifestyle therapy with glucose monitoring.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After two weeks of diet and exercise, her glucose log shows fasting values of 105-115 mg/dL on most mornings despite good adherence, with normal postprandial values. What is the best next step?
options: Add bedtime (basal) insulin such as NPH or detemir
Add rapid-acting insulin only with each meal
Continue diet alone and recheck in another month
Switch entirely to glyburide before breakfast
stages.explanation: Persistently elevated fasting glucose (target typically <95 mg/dL) despite adequate dietary therapy is best treated by adding a basal insulin at bedtime (NPH or a long-acting analog), which targets overnight hepatic glucose output and the morning value. Mealtime rapid-acting insulin addresses postprandial spikes, which are well controlled here, so prandial-only dosing would not fix the fasting problem. Continuing diet alone for another month leaves fasting hyperglycemia untreated and increases fetal risk. Glyburide is a second-line oral alternative that crosses the placenta and is not preferred over insulin; switching entirely to it—particularly a morning dose—would not optimally address fasting hyperglycemia. Adding bedtime basal insulin is the targeted, guideline-concordant choice.

pearls: Two-step screening: abnormal 1-hour 50-g screen is confirmed with a 3-hour 100-g OGTT; two or more values at/above thresholds diagnose GDM.
Distinguish GDM from overt diabetes (fasting ≥126, random ≥200, or A1c ≥6.5%, especially early in pregnancy).
First-line therapy is medical nutrition therapy plus exercise and glucose self-monitoring; insulin is the preferred drug when medication is needed.
Elevated fasting glucose is targeted with bedtime basal insulin; postprandial elevations are targeted with mealtime rapid-acting insulin.

cases.title: High Blood Pressure Late in Pregnancy
cases.chiefComplaint: Elevated blood pressure found at a routine prenatal visit at 34 weeks.
cases.history: A 28-year-old woman, gravida 1 para 0, at 34 weeks gestation presents for a scheduled prenatal visit. She feels generally well but reports mild swelling of her hands and face over the past week and a dull frontal headache this morning that improved with rest. She denies visual changes, right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, nausea, or decreased fetal movement. Her blood pressures earlier in pregnancy were normal (around 110/70 mmHg). PMH: none; this is her first pregnancy. Meds: prenatal vitamin only. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: nonsmoker, no alcohol, works in retail. Family history: mother had high blood pressure in her one pregnancy. She has had no prior renal or autoimmune disease. Today's office blood pressure is elevated on two readings 15 minutes apart. A urine dipstick shows protein. She is anxious but in no acute distress and is asking whether she can simply go home and recheck her pressure later in the week.
cases.exam: Alert, comfortable, no acute distress. Fundal height appropriate for 34 weeks; fetal heart tones 144 bpm. Face and hands with mild nonpitting edema; 2+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally. Lungs clear to auscultation. Heart regular without murmur. Abdomen gravid, soft; no right upper quadrant or epigastric tenderness. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ at the patellae without clonus. No focal neurologic deficits. No scotomata on confrontation visual field testing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the visit findings, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Reassure her and have her recheck blood pressure at home in 1 week
Start an ACE inhibitor and discharge home
Confirm proteinuria and order preeclampsia laboratory workup (CBC, LFTs, creatinine, and a protein quantification) while assessing for severe features
Order a renal ultrasound and follow up in 2 weeks
stages.explanation: New-onset hypertension (≥140/90 on two readings) after 20 weeks with proteinuria raises concern for preeclampsia, which requires prompt laboratory evaluation: CBC (platelets, hemolysis), liver enzymes, creatinine, and quantified proteinuria (spot protein/creatinine ratio or 24-hour urine), plus a careful search for severe features. Sending her home to recheck in a week dangerously delays diagnosis of a condition that can progress rapidly. ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy because of fetal toxicity and would never be the discharge plan. A renal ultrasound does not diagnose or risk-stratify preeclampsia and would inappropriately delay care. The correct step is immediate confirmatory testing and severe-feature assessment.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Spot urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.6 (elevated; ≥ 0.3 abnormal)
Platelets: 188 ×10³/µL
AST: 28 U/L
ALT: 24 U/L
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
Uric acid: 6.5 mg/dL
Repeat BP after rest: 150/96 mmHg
Fetal nonstress test: reactive; estimated fetal weight 40th percentile

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How should this clinical picture be classified?
options: Gestational hypertension without proteinuria
Preeclampsia without severe features
Preeclampsia with severe features
Chronic hypertension
stages.explanation: She has new hypertension after 20 weeks plus proteinuria (protein/creatinine ratio ≥0.3), which defines preeclampsia. There are no severe features: her blood pressure is below the severe range (<160/110), platelets are normal, transaminases are normal, creatinine is normal, and she has no persistent headache, visual disturbance, pulmonary edema, or epigastric/RUQ pain. This is therefore preeclampsia without severe features. Gestational hypertension is excluded because proteinuria is present. Severe-feature preeclampsia would require sustained BP ≥160/110, thrombocytopenia, elevated liver enzymes, renal insufficiency, pulmonary edema, or neurologic/visual symptoms—none of which are met. Chronic hypertension is excluded by her documented normal early-pregnancy pressures.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which finding, if newly present, would reclassify her as having preeclampsia with severe features?
options: Lower-extremity edema to the mid-shins
A sustained blood pressure of 165/112 mmHg, a platelet count of 80 ×10³/µL, or new persistent visual scotomata
A urine protein/creatinine ratio of 0.6
A uric acid level of 6.5 mg/dL
stages.explanation: Severe features of preeclampsia include systolic ≥160 or diastolic ≥110 mmHg (sustained), platelets <100 ×10³/µL, AST/ALT roughly twice the upper limit of normal, serum creatinine >1.1 mg/dL (or doubling), pulmonary edema, and new cerebral or visual symptoms—so a BP of 165/112, platelets of 80, or new scotomata would all qualify. Notably, the degree of proteinuria is not itself a severe feature, so a ratio of 0.6 does not upgrade the diagnosis. Peripheral edema is common in normal pregnancy and is not a diagnostic criterion. An elevated uric acid is associated with preeclampsia but is not part of the severe-feature definition. Recognizing true severe features drives the urgency of delivery decisions.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For preeclampsia without severe features at 34 weeks with reassuring fetal testing, what is the most appropriate plan?
options: Immediate cesarean delivery today
Expectant management with close maternal-fetal surveillance and obstetric co-management, planning delivery around 37 weeks
Start magnesium sulfate and induce labor immediately
Discharge with no follow-up until the routine 36-week visit
stages.explanation: For preeclampsia without severe features and reassuring fetal status before 37 weeks, expectant management with close surveillance—serial blood pressures, labs, symptom checks, and fetal monitoring—is appropriate, with planned delivery at about 37 weeks. As a family physician, prompt obstetric referral/co-management is essential. Immediate cesarean is not indicated without severe features, worsening status, or fetal compromise and would impose unnecessary prematurity. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and immediate delivery are reserved for severe features or term gestation, not this scenario. Discharging her without close follow-up is unsafe because preeclampsia can progress quickly to severe disease. The guideline-concordant plan is monitored expectant management toward delivery near 37 weeks.

pearls: Preeclampsia = new hypertension (≥140/90) after 20 weeks plus proteinuria (or, without proteinuria, end-organ dysfunction).
Degree of proteinuria is NOT a severe feature; severe features are BP ≥160/110, platelets <100k, transaminitis, Cr >1.1, pulmonary edema, or cerebral/visual symptoms.
ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated in pregnancy; labetalol, nifedipine, and methyldopa are preferred antihypertensives.
Deliver at 37 weeks for preeclampsia without severe features; severe features generally warrant delivery at/after 34 weeks plus magnesium for seizure prophylaxis.

cases.title: Stiff Shoulders Every Morning
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of aching and stiffness in both shoulders and hips, worst in the morning.
cases.history: A 73-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip-girdle aching and stiffness. Mornings are worst: she needs over an hour to loosen up and struggles to lift her arms to comb her hair or rise from a low chair. Symptoms came on relatively abruptly. She reports fatigue, low-grade malaise, and a 2-kg weight loss but denies true muscle weakness when tested at her best. She has no headaches, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, or visual changes. No rash, dry eyes/mouth, Raynaud phenomenon, or recent infection. PMH: hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; osteopenia. She recently started no new medications, specifically no statin. Allergies: none. Social: never smoker, occasional wine, lives independently. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems otherwise negative for fevers, cough, dysuria, or bowel changes. She is frustrated that ibuprofen has barely helped and worries she is developing arthritis like her late mother. Functional decline is affecting her ability to dress and care for herself.
cases.exam: Well-appearing but moves stiffly. Active shoulder abduction limited by pain to about 90 degrees bilaterally; passive range fuller. Mild tenderness over the trapezius and proximal arms; hip-girdle discomfort with motion. No joint swelling, warmth, or synovitis in the hands, wrists, or knees. True proximal muscle strength is 5/5 when pain is overcome; no fasciculations or atrophy. Temporal arteries nontender with normal pulses; no scalp tenderness. No rash. Cranial nerves intact; visual acuity and fields normal. No lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes polymyalgia rheumatica from an inflammatory myopathy such as polymyositis in this patient?
options: The presence of morning stiffness
Bilateral shoulder involvement
Older age at onset
Pain-limited but fundamentally preserved muscle strength with normal muscle enzymes expected, rather than true proximal weakness
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica causes pain and stiffness that limit movement, but true muscle strength is preserved when the pain is overcome, and muscle enzymes (CK, aldolase) are normal. Polymyositis, by contrast, produces genuine, painless proximal weakness with elevated CK. Morning stiffness occurs in many inflammatory conditions and does not separate the two. Bilateral shoulder involvement is typical of PMR but is not specific. Older age is consistent with PMR (almost always >50) yet does not by itself exclude a myopathy. The key discriminator is the pattern of strength and enzyme levels: pain-limited but intact strength with normal CK favors PMR over an inflammatory myopathy.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ESR: 78 mm/hr (elevated)
C-reactive protein: 5.2 mg/dL (elevated)
Creatine kinase: 90 U/L (normal)
Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia)
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP: negative
Comprehensive metabolic panel: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How do these results support the diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica?
options: The elevated creatine kinase confirms muscle inflammation
Markedly elevated inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) with a normal CK and negative RF/anti-CCP fit PMR and argue against myositis and rheumatoid arthritis
The normal TSH confirms PMR
The mild anemia is diagnostic of PMR
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica characteristically shows markedly elevated ESR and CRP with a normal CK; the normal CK argues against an inflammatory myopathy, and negative rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP make seropositive rheumatoid arthritis unlikely. Her CK is normal, so it cannot confirm muscle inflammation. A normal TSH usefully excludes hypothyroid myopathy as a mimic but does not itself confirm PMR. The mild normocytic anemia reflects chronic inflammation and supports an inflammatory process but is nonspecific and not diagnostic. Taken together, the high inflammatory markers with normal CK and negative RA serologies in an older patient with girdle stiffness fit PMR well.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Low-dose oral prednisone, typically 15-20 mg daily, with a planned gradual taper
High-dose prednisone (e.g., 60 mg daily) plus aspirin
Methotrexate as first-line monotherapy
Continue NSAIDs alone and reassess in 3 months
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica responds dramatically to low-dose glucocorticoids; initial prednisone of about 15-20 mg daily typically produces marked improvement within days, followed by a slow taper over many months guided by symptoms and inflammatory markers. High-dose prednisone (40-60 mg) is reserved for suspected giant cell arteritis, which this patient lacks (no headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, or visual symptoms). Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing agent considered for relapsing disease or to limit steroid exposure, not first-line monotherapy. NSAIDs alone are inadequate and have already failed her. The rapid, near-complete response to low-dose steroids is both therapeutic and diagnostically supportive of PMR.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two months into prednisone, she develops a new throbbing temporal headache, scalp tenderness when brushing her hair, and transient blurring of vision in one eye. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Reassure her that this is a steroid side effect and continue the current taper
Promptly increase to high-dose corticosteroids for suspected giant cell arteritis and arrange urgent evaluation including temporal artery biopsy, without delaying treatment
Order an outpatient MRI of the brain and follow up in 1 week
Stop the prednisone entirely and observe
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica is closely associated with giant cell arteritis; new temporal headache, scalp tenderness, and transient monocular visual symptoms are alarm features for GCA, which threatens permanent blindness. The correct action is to start high-dose corticosteroids immediately and arrange urgent evaluation, including temporal artery biopsy—treatment must not wait for the biopsy, which remains informative for up to about 1-2 weeks after steroids begin. Attributing visual symptoms to a steroid side effect risks irreversible vision loss. An outpatient MRI with a week's delay is far too slow for a sight-threatening emergency. Stopping prednisone would worsen the inflammation and is exactly the wrong move. Immediate high-dose steroids are the priority.

pearls: PMR features bilateral shoulder/hip-girdle pain and prolonged morning stiffness in patients >50 with high ESR/CRP but normal CK and preserved true strength.
Dramatic response to low-dose prednisone (15-20 mg/day) is characteristic and supports the diagnosis.
Always screen for giant cell arteritis: headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and visual changes warrant high-dose steroids immediately.
Do not delay GCA treatment for biopsy—temporal artery biopsy stays informative for 1-2 weeks after steroids begin.

cases.title: A Face That Suddenly Drooped
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden drooping of the right side of the face since waking this morning.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents with right-sided facial droop noticed when he woke this morning, about 8 hours ago. He has trouble closing his right eye and food collects in his right cheek; his speech sounds slightly slurred because of the lip weakness. He reports decreased taste on the front of his tongue and a sense that sounds are uncomfortably loud in the right ear. He had a mild upper respiratory illness last week. He denies arm or leg weakness, numbness elsewhere, headache, vertigo, double vision, difficulty swallowing, or limb incoordination. No recent tick bites, rash, or travel to Lyme-endemic areas, and no ear pain or vesicles. PMH: none. Meds: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, social alcohol, works in sales. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems otherwise negative for fever, neck stiffness, or recent trauma. He is frightened that he is having a stroke and wants to know whether he needs to go to the emergency department or can be managed in clinic.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented, fluent speech aside from labial articulation. Right facial weakness involving the forehead (cannot wrinkle the right brow), with incomplete right eye closure and a positive Bell phenomenon. Flattened right nasolabial fold and drooping mouth corner. Remaining cranial nerves intact: extraocular movements full, pupils equal and reactive, facial sensation intact, palate elevates symmetrically, tongue midline. Motor strength 5/5 in all four limbs; reflexes 2+ symmetric; no pronator drift; gait and finger-to-nose normal. No ear canal vesicles; tympanic membranes normal. No nuchal rigidity. No limb sensory deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which exam finding most strongly indicates a peripheral (lower motor neuron) facial palsy rather than a central (stroke) cause?
options: The slurred speech from lip weakness
The flattened nasolabial fold
Involvement of the forehead with inability to wrinkle the brow on the affected side
The acute onset of symptoms
stages.explanation: The forehead receives bilateral cortical innervation, so a central (upper motor neuron) lesion such as a stroke spares the forehead, allowing brow wrinkling on the weak side. A peripheral seventh-nerve lesion (Bell palsy) paralyzes the entire hemiface, including the forehead, so inability to wrinkle the brow points to a peripheral cause. Slurred speech from labial weakness occurs with facial weakness of any origin and does not localize. A flattened nasolabial fold reflects lower-face weakness, which occurs in both central and peripheral lesions. Acute onset is common to both stroke and Bell palsy and is not discriminating. Forehead involvement is the classic feature separating peripheral from central facial weakness.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the isolated peripheral facial palsy with normal remaining neurologic exam, what is the most appropriate diagnostic approach?
options: Obtain an urgent CT angiogram of the head and neck before any treatment
Diagnose Bell palsy clinically; routine neuroimaging and extensive lab testing are not required without atypical or red-flag features
Order an MRI of the brain for every patient with facial droop
Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis
stages.explanation: Bell palsy is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion in a patient with acute, isolated, unilateral peripheral facial weakness and an otherwise normal neurologic exam. Routine neuroimaging and broad lab panels are not needed in classic cases. Urgent CT angiography is appropriate for suspected acute stroke, but the forehead involvement and intact remaining exam make a central cause unlikely. Reflexively obtaining brain MRI for every facial droop is low-yield in classic presentations and is reserved for atypical features—gradual onset, bilateral or recurrent palsy, additional cranial nerve deficits, limb findings, or no recovery by 3 months. A lumbar puncture is unnecessary without meningitic signs. The correct approach is clinical diagnosis without reflexive imaging.

stages.label: BEDSIDE ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
House-Brackmann grade: IV (moderately severe dysfunction)
Eye closure: incomplete on the right, with visible sclera on attempted closure
Corneal reflex: intact; cornea clear without staining or abrasion on fluorescein exam
Otoscopy: no vesicles in the ear canal or auricle (no evidence of Ramsay Hunt)
Lyme serology (sent given seasonal exposure question): pending, low pretest probability
Fingerstick glucose: 96 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He presents about 8 hours after symptom onset. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Antiviral therapy alone (e.g., valacyclovir)
Oral corticosteroids started promptly, plus meticulous eye protection for the eye that cannot fully close
Aspirin and admission for stroke workup
No treatment; reassure that all cases resolve spontaneously
stages.explanation: Early oral corticosteroids (ideally within 72 hours of onset) improve the likelihood of complete recovery in Bell palsy and are the mainstay of treatment. Equally important is eye protection—lubricating drops, ointment at night, and taping or a moisture chamber—because incomplete eye closure risks exposure keratopathy and corneal injury. Antivirals alone provide little benefit; they may be added to steroids in severe (near-complete) palsy but should not replace corticosteroids. Aspirin and a stroke admission are not indicated given the clear peripheral pattern and normal exam. Although many cases improve spontaneously, withholding steroids forfeits a treatment that increases full recovery, so "no treatment" is not the best choice. Steroids plus eye care is optimal.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which feature, if present, should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis and further workup rather than a confident diagnosis of Bell palsy?
options: Decreased taste on the anterior tongue and hyperacusis
Incomplete eye closure on the affected side
A preceding viral upper respiratory illness
Slowly progressive weakness over several weeks, bilateral facial involvement, or additional cranial nerve deficits
stages.explanation: Red flags that argue against simple Bell palsy include weakness that progresses gradually over weeks (suggesting a mass), bilateral facial palsy (Lyme disease, sarcoidosis, Guillain-Barre), recurrent palsy, or involvement of other cranial nerves or limbs (brainstem or cerebellopontine-angle lesion). Any of these warrants imaging and a broader evaluation. Decreased anterior-tongue taste and hyperacusis actually localize the lesion to the facial nerve proximal to the chorda tympani and stapedial branches and are consistent with Bell palsy, not against it. Incomplete eye closure is expected with peripheral facial weakness and simply mandates eye protection. A preceding viral illness is commonly associated with Bell palsy. Progressive, bilateral, or multi-nerve findings are the true warning signs.

cases.diagnosis: Bell palsy (idiopathic peripheral facial nerve palsy)
pearls: Forehead sparing = central lesion (stroke); forehead involvement = peripheral seventh-nerve palsy (Bell palsy).
Bell palsy is a clinical diagnosis; imaging is reserved for atypical features (gradual, bilateral, recurrent, other cranial nerves, or no recovery by 3 months).
Start oral corticosteroids within 72 hours; add antivirals only for severe palsy, and never skip eye protection.
Hyperacusis and altered anterior-tongue taste localize the lesion along the facial nerve and are consistent with Bell palsy.

cases.title: Belly Pain That Moved to the Right
cases.chiefComplaint: Abdominal pain that started around the navel and shifted to the lower right side.
cases.history: A 17-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with 18 hours of abdominal pain. It began as a vague ache around his umbilicus and over several hours migrated to the right lower quadrant, where it is now sharp and constant. He has nausea, vomited twice, and has no appetite—he refused his favorite dinner. Walking and the car ride over jostled and worsened the pain. He has had no diarrhea; his last bowel movement was yesterday and normal. No dysuria, hematuria, or testicular pain. No prior similar episodes. PMH: none. Meds: none. Allergies: none. Surgical history: none. Social: high school student, denies alcohol, tobacco, drugs, or sexual activity. Family history: noncontributory. Immunizations up to date. Review of systems notable for subjective fever and chills today; otherwise negative. His mother reports he has been lying still and reluctant to move on the stretcher. He rates the pain 7/10 and says it hurts most when the bed is bumped.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, lying still, reluctant to move. Abdomen soft elsewhere but with focal tenderness and guarding at McBurney point in the right lower quadrant. Positive Rovsing sign (RLQ pain on left-sided palpation), positive psoas sign, and pain with heel strike. Voluntary guarding without diffuse rigidity; no rebound across the whole abdomen but localized rebound at RLQ. Bowel sounds present but reduced. No flank tenderness. Genital exam: testes nontender, no masses, no hernia. No skin rash. Lungs clear; heart tachycardic but regular.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which element of the history and exam is most specific for acute appendicitis?
options: Subjective fever and chills
Two episodes of vomiting
Migration of pain from the periumbilical region to the right lower quadrant with anorexia
A heart rate of 104 bpm
stages.explanation: Classic appendicitis begins with poorly localized periumbilical visceral pain (referred to the T10 dermatome) that migrates over hours to the right lower quadrant as the inflamed appendix irritates the parietal peritoneum; this migration plus anorexia is among the most specific clinical features and is heavily weighted in scoring systems. Fever and chills support inflammation but are nonspecific and occur in many intra-abdominal and systemic illnesses. Vomiting is common but nonspecific, occurring in gastroenteritis, obstruction, and many other conditions. Tachycardia reflects pain, fever, or dehydration and is not specific. Pain migration with anorexia is the hallmark that most strongly points to appendicitis among the listed findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Alvarado score (migration, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, RLQ tenderness, rebound, fever, leukocytosis, left shift), how should you proceed before labs return?
options: Discharge with outpatient follow-up given his stable vital signs
Calculate the Alvarado score and obtain labs and imaging, since his clinical features already suggest moderate-to-high risk
Give a large oral fluid challenge and reassess in 12 hours
Start oral antibiotics at home without imaging
stages.explanation: His presentation already accrues multiple Alvarado points—migration, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, RLQ tenderness, rebound, and fever—placing him at moderate-to-high risk, so the appropriate path is to obtain labs (CBC with differential) and imaging rather than discharge. Sending him home with stable vitals ignores a likely surgical condition and risks perforation. An oral fluid challenge with a 12-hour delay is inappropriate when appendicitis is suspected, as delay raises perforation risk. Empiric home oral antibiotics without imaging or surgical evaluation is not standard initial care for suspected appendicitis. Risk stratification with labs and imaging is the correct next step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.8 ×10³/µL with 82% neutrophils (left shift)
CRP 6.0 mg/dL (elevated)
Urinalysis: trace ketones, no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, no blood
Beta-hCG: not applicable (male)
Ultrasound RLQ: noncompressible, blind-ending tubular structure measuring 9 mm in diameter with surrounding fat stranding; no fecalith visualized
Alvarado score: 9

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ultrasound shows a 9-mm noncompressible appendix with fat stranding and an Alvarado score of 9. What is the best next step?
options: Obtain a CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast to confirm before any intervention
Admit for observation and serial exams without surgical consultation
Discharge on oral antibiotics with surgery clinic follow-up in 1 week
Consult surgery for appendectomy, begin IV fluids, analgesia, and preoperative antibiotics
stages.explanation: With a high Alvarado score and diagnostic ultrasound (noncompressible appendix >6 mm with periappendiceal inflammation), the diagnosis is established and the definitive treatment is appendectomy; the correct step is surgical consultation along with IV fluids, analgesia, and preoperative antibiotics. Ultrasound is the preferred first imaging study in children and adolescents to avoid radiation, and because it is already diagnostic, a confirmatory CT is unnecessary and would delay care—CT is reserved for equivocal ultrasounds. Observation without surgical involvement risks progression to perforation. Discharging him on outpatient oral antibiotics with delayed follow-up is unsafe for confirmed acute appendicitis. Prompt surgical management with perioperative antibiotics is standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If surgery were delayed and he developed diffuse abdominal rigidity, worsening fever, and hypotension, what complication would you most suspect?
options: Resolving appendicitis
Appendiceal perforation with peritonitis
Simple gastroenteritis
A urinary tract infection
stages.explanation: Progression from localized RLQ findings to diffuse rigidity, high fever, and hypotension indicates appendiceal perforation with generalized peritonitis and developing sepsis—the feared complication of delayed appendicitis, especially common when surgery is postponed. Resolving appendicitis would produce improving, not worsening, signs and would not cause hypotension. Gastroenteritis typically causes diffuse crampy pain with diarrhea and does not produce peritoneal rigidity or septic hypotension. A urinary tract infection causes dysuria and pyuria, not diffuse peritonitis; his urinalysis was bland. Diffuse rigidity with hemodynamic instability after suspected appendicitis signals perforation and peritonitis requiring emergent resuscitation and operative intervention.

pearls: Pain migrating from periumbilical to RLQ with anorexia is among the most specific features of appendicitis.
The Alvarado score aggregates symptoms, signs, and labs to stratify risk and guide imaging and surgical referral.
Ultrasound is first-line imaging in children/adolescents and pregnancy; reserve CT for equivocal cases to limit radiation.
Definitive treatment is appendectomy with perioperative antibiotics; delay risks perforation and peritonitis.

cases.title: Thirsty, Vomiting, and Breathing Hard
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of excessive urination, thirst, vomiting, and rapid heavy breathing.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department with 2 days of polyuria, intense thirst, nausea, and several episodes of vomiting, now with deep rapid breathing and lethargy. Over the past 2 weeks she lost about 4 kg unintentionally and felt increasingly fatigued. She had a flu-like illness a week ago. She has no known diabetes but recalls a cousin with "juvenile diabetes." She denies abdominal trauma, chest pain, dysuria, or focal infection symptoms beyond the recent viral illness. PMH: none. Meds: none; no insulin. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, nonsmoker, college student; last menstrual period 2 weeks ago. Family history: type 1 diabetes in a cousin; autoimmune thyroid disease in mother. Review of systems notable for blurred vision and dry mouth. On arrival she is sleepy but rousable, answering questions slowly. Her breath has a fruity odor noted by the triage nurse, and her respirations are deep and labored. She is too nauseated to drink and has not urinated in several hours despite earlier polyuria.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, lethargic but rousable. Mucous membranes dry; skin tenting and delayed capillary refill indicate volume depletion. Breathing deep and rhythmic (Kussmaul pattern) with a fruity (acetone) odor. Abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness, no rebound or guarding. Cardiopulmonary: tachycardic, regular; lungs clear. Neurologic: oriented to person and place, slow but appropriate responses, no focal deficits, reflexes symmetric. No neck stiffness or photophobia. No rash or cellulitis. No focal source of infection identified on initial survey.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first intervention while initial labs are being drawn?
options: Begin aggressive isotonic IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline) and obtain point-of-care glucose, electrolytes, venous blood gas, and ketones
Immediate IV bolus of regular insulin
Administer IV sodium bicarbonate
Give oral rehydration solution and observe
stages.explanation: Diabetic ketoacidosis causes profound osmotic-diuresis–related volume depletion, so the first priority is aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation with normal saline, alongside rapid labs (glucose, electrolytes including potassium, venous blood gas, ketones, and renal function). Fluids alone begin to lower glucose and improve perfusion. Insulin should not be given as the very first step—particularly before the potassium is known—because insulin drives potassium intracellularly and can precipitate dangerous hypokalemia; it is also less effective without volume repletion. Bicarbonate is not routinely indicated and is reserved for severe acidemia (pH <6.9). Oral rehydration is inadequate in a vomiting, hypotensive, obtunded patient who cannot reliably drink. Fluids first, then guided insulin and electrolyte management.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Glucose: 528 mg/dL
Venous pH: 7.12 (acidemic)
Bicarbonate (HCO3): 9 mEq/L (low)
Anion gap: 28 (elevated)
Sodium: 132 mEq/L (corrected ~136)
Potassium: 5.4 mEq/L
Serum beta-hydroxybutyrate: strongly positive
BUN: 32 mg/dL  Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL
Urinalysis: large ketones, glucosuria; no nitrites/leukocyte esterase
ECG: sinus tachycardia, no peaked T waves

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These results confirm DKA. The potassium is 5.4 mEq/L. How does this guide initial therapy?
options: Withhold all potassium and start insulin immediately
Start insulin now while monitoring potassium closely, because total-body potassium is depleted despite a normal-to-high serum level, and add potassium to fluids once it falls below ~5.0-5.3 mEq/L
Give a large IV potassium bolus before any insulin
Delay insulin until the potassium is above 6.0 mEq/L
stages.explanation: In DKA, acidosis and insulin deficiency shift potassium out of cells, so the serum value (here 5.4) overstates true stores—total-body potassium is markedly depleted. Insulin can be started now because the serum potassium is above the safety threshold (>3.3 mEq/L), but it must be accompanied by close monitoring, and potassium is added to fluids once the level drops to roughly 5.0-5.3 mEq/L to prevent hypokalemia as insulin drives K+ intracellularly. Withholding potassium entirely while giving insulin risks dangerous hypokalemia as treatment proceeds. A large potassium bolus is unsafe and unnecessary at this level. Waiting until potassium exceeds 6.0 needlessly delays insulin. Insulin with vigilant potassium repletion is the correct strategy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After fluids and an insulin infusion, her glucose falls to 210 mg/dL but her anion gap is still 16 and bicarbonate remains low. What is the best next step?
options: Stop the insulin infusion now that glucose is near normal
Switch immediately to subcutaneous long-acting insulin and discharge from the ED
Add dextrose-containing IV fluids and continue the insulin infusion until the anion gap closes and ketoacidosis resolves
Reduce fluids to avoid cerebral edema and recheck in 6 hours
stages.explanation: The endpoint of DKA treatment is resolution of ketoacidosis—closure of the anion gap and normalization of bicarbonate/pH—not merely lowering glucose. When glucose reaches about 200 mg/dL while the gap remains open, you add dextrose to the IV fluids and continue the insulin infusion so that insulin keeps suppressing ketogenesis without causing hypoglycemia.
