Anaphylaxis to contrast
stages.explanation: Beck's triad—hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and distended neck veins (elevated JVP)—in a patient with a known type A dissection indicates cardiac tamponade, here from proximal rupture/leak into the pericardium (the CTA already showed a small effusion). This is a leading cause of death in type A dissection and demands emergent surgery; blind large-volume pericardiocentesis can be hazardous and may accelerate bleeding, so the definitive answer is rapid surgical decompression/repair. Tension pneumothorax would show tracheal deviation and absent breath sounds. PE is less consistent with this established dissection and exam. Septic shock and contrast anaphylaxis do not fit the timeline or the muffled-sounds/elevated-JVP picture.

cases.diagnosis: Acute Stanford type A aortic dissection
pearls: Abrupt 'tearing' chest pain maximal at onset with an inter-arm BP differential, pulse deficit, or new aortic regurgitation murmur = dissection until proven otherwise.
Anti-impulse therapy: beta-blocker FIRST to lower heart rate/dP/dt, THEN a vasodilator—never a vasodilator alone (reflex tachycardia worsens shear).
CT angiography is the usual confirmatory test; type A (ascending) dissection is a surgical emergency, while uncomplicated type B is managed medically.
Watch for tamponade (Beck's triad), acute AR, and coronary involvement—lethal complications of proximal dissection.

cases.title: Fever, Stiff Neck, and Light Hurting His Eyes
cases.chiefComplaint: Twelve hours of high fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, and intolerance to light.
cases.history: A 20-year-old college student is brought to the ED by his roommate with 12 hours of rapidly worsening headache, high fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia. He felt 'fluish' yesterday and now is lethargic and confused at times. He lives in a crowded dormitory; a hallmate was recently hospitalized with a serious infection. Past medical history is unremarkable. Immunizations: he is unsure whether he received the meningococcal vaccine. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends, does not smoke, and denies IV drug use or recent travel. Family history noncontributory. He has had no recent head trauma, no known immunodeficiency, and no prior similar episodes. His roommate reports he became progressively harder to wake over the last few hours and vomited twice. He has not noticed a rash but has not undressed to check.
cases.exam: Ill, lethargic young man, oriented to person and place only, intermittently drowsy. Marked nuchal rigidity; positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs. Photophobia present. A few scattered non-blanching petechiae noted on the lower legs. Pupils equal and reactive, no papilledema on fundoscopy, no focal motor deficit, normal reflexes, no seizure activity observed. Heart tachycardic, regular. Lungs clear. No new murmur. Capillary refill ~2-3 seconds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has fever, nuchal rigidity, photophobia, and altered mentation. What is the correct sequence to avoid delaying treatment?
options: Perform lumbar puncture first; give antibiotics only after CSF results return

Draw blood cultures, give dexamethasone and empiric IV antibiotics promptly, then perform lumbar puncture (with CT head first only if focal deficits, papilledema, immunocompromise, or seizures are present)
Obtain an MRI brain before any treatment
Start oral antibiotics and arrange outpatient follow-up
Give antivirals alone and observe
stages.explanation: Bacterial meningitis is a time-critical emergency. The correct sequence: obtain blood cultures, then give dexamethasone WITH or just before the first dose of empiric IV antibiotics, and perform the LP—but do NOT delay antibiotics for the LP. A CT head before LP is required only if there are signs raising herniation risk (focal neurologic deficit, papilledema, new seizures, altered consciousness raising concern, or immunocompromise); if CT is needed, give antibiotics first. LP before any antibiotics dangerously delays treatment. MRI is too slow. Oral antibiotics/outpatient management is inadequate for suspected bacterial meningitis. Antivirals alone miss the bacterial cause suggested by petechiae and rapid toxicity.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For a previously healthy 20-year-old with suspected bacterial meningitis, which empiric IV antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?
options: Vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime)
Oral amoxicillin alone
Ceftriaxone alone with no vancomycin
Metronidazole plus gentamicin
Azithromycin monotherapy
stages.explanation: For adults 18-50, the most likely pathogens are Streptococcus pneumoniae and Neisseria meningitidis. Empiric therapy is vancomycin (to cover possible penicillin/cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus) PLUS a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime). Ceftriaxone alone omits coverage for resistant pneumococcus. Oral amoxicillin cannot achieve adequate CSF levels for fulminant meningitis. In patients over 50 or immunocompromised, ADD ampicillin to cover Listeria—but this young, immunocompetent patient does not require it empirically. Metronidazole/gentamicin and azithromycin do not provide appropriate CNS coverage for these pathogens. Dexamethasone is given alongside to reduce neurologic complications, especially in pneumococcal disease.

stages.label: CSF ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Lumbar puncture (after antibiotics started):
Opening pressure 32 cm H2O (elevated)
Appearance: cloudy
WBC 4,200/µL (90% neutrophils)
Glucose 28 mg/dL (serum glucose 110 mg/dL; CSF:serum ratio markedly low)
Protein 220 mg/dL (elevated)
Gram stain: gram-negative diplococci
Blood cultures: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein, and gram-negative diplococci. Which organism and consequence is most relevant?
options: Streptococcus pneumoniae; no public health measures needed
Listeria monocytogenes; treat with ceftriaxone alone
Neisseria meningitidis; close contacts require chemoprophylaxis and it can cause Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome
Herpes simplex virus; this is the typical CSF pattern
Cryptococcus neoformans; expect a lymphocyte-predominant picture
stages.explanation: Gram-negative diplococci with a neutrophilic, low-glucose, high-protein CSF in a dorm-dwelling young adult with petechiae indicate Neisseria meningitidis. Two key consequences: (1) close contacts (household, dormmates, anyone exposed to oral secretions) need antibiotic chemoprophylaxis (e.g., rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) and the case is reportable to public health; (2) meningococcemia can cause Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome—bilateral adrenal hemorrhage with shock and DIC. Pneumococcus is a gram-positive diplococcus and still warrants nothing public-health-wise like meningococcus. Listeria (gram-positive rods) needs ampicillin. HSV gives a lymphocytic CSF with normal glucose. Cryptococcus is lymphocyte-predominant in immunocompromised hosts.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the correct public health and prophylaxis action for his dormmates and close contacts?
options: No action is needed because he is now on antibiotics
Provide antibiotic chemoprophylaxis (e.g., rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) to close contacts and report the case to public health
Give all contacts a 10-day course of IV ceftriaxone
Only vaccinate contacts; no antibiotics are ever indicated
Quarantine the dormitory for 30 days without medication
stages.explanation: Meningococcal disease requires prompt chemoprophylaxis for close contacts—household members, roommates, intimate contacts, and anyone exposed to the patient's oral/respiratory secretions (including some healthcare providers who performed unprotected airway maneuvers). Effective single/short regimens include rifampin, a single dose of ciprofloxacin, or a single ceftriaxone injection. The case must be reported to public health for contact tracing and outbreak control; vaccination may be offered for serogroup control but does NOT replace prompt prophylaxis. Treating the index patient does not protect contacts. A full IV course for every contact is unnecessary and impractical. Vaccination alone is insufficient acutely, and blanket quarantine without prophylaxis is not the standard approach.

cases.diagnosis: Bacterial meningitis due to Neisseria meningitidis
pearls: Do not delay antibiotics for the LP: cultures, then dexamethasone with empiric antibiotics; CT before LP only with focal deficits, papilledema, seizures, or immunocompromise.
Empiric therapy for ages 18-50 is vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin; add ampicillin for Listeria if >50 or immunocompromised.
Bacterial CSF: neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein; gram-negative diplococci = meningococcus.
Meningococcal disease mandates chemoprophylaxis for close contacts, public health reporting, and vigilance for Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome.

cases.title: Back Pain That Won't Quit, Now with Fevers
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of worsening mid-back pain, now with fevers and night sweats.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man admitted for management of cellulitis reports 3 weeks of progressively worsening, constant mid-thoracic/lumbar back pain that is worse at night and at rest and is not relieved by repositioning. Over the past week he has had subjective fevers, chills, and night sweats. He has a history of injection drug use (heroin), most recently weeks ago, and was treated for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) bacteremia 2 months ago with what he thinks was an incomplete antibiotic course after leaving against medical advice. Past medical history: hepatitis C, poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.2%). Medications: none reliably. No known drug allergies. He smokes and drinks alcohol. Family history noncontributory. He denies recent trauma. He notes the pain is point-tender over the spine and has begun to limit his ability to walk comfortably. He reports no bowel or bladder incontinence and no saddle anesthesia at present, but does describe new mild tingling in the feet.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man lying still. Focal, reproducible point tenderness to percussion over the lower thoracic/upper lumbar spinous processes. Paraspinal muscle spasm. Lower-extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally; sensation grossly intact with subjective mild distal tingling; reflexes symmetric; no clonus. Normal rectal tone, no saddle anesthesia. Gait cautious and guarded due to pain. Healing injection track marks on both arms. No new heart murmur appreciated. Skin: resolving cellulitis on the left lower leg. No spinal deformity or overlying erythema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which features make this back pain a red-flag presentation requiring urgent workup rather than mechanical low back pain?
options: Pain only with movement that improves with rest
Fever, night sweats, IV drug use, recent MRSA bacteremia, focal spinal tenderness, and pain that is worse at rest/night
A single episode of acute pain after lifting, fully resolved
Bilateral leg pain relieved by sitting (neurogenic claudication)
Chronic stable pain present unchanged for ten years
stages.explanation: Mechanical back pain typically improves with rest and worsens with activity, lacks systemic features, and has no focal bony tenderness. This patient has multiple red flags for vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis: fever and night sweats (systemic infection), IV drug use and recent incompletely treated MRSA bacteremia (hematogenous seeding source), poorly controlled diabetes (immunocompromise), focal spinal point tenderness, and pain that is constant, worse at night and at rest. These features mandate urgent imaging and infection workup. Pain relieved by rest, an isolated resolved lifting injury, classic neurogenic claudication, and unchanged decade-long pain all argue AGAINST an acute serious cause and would not trigger emergent infectious evaluation.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate combination of initial diagnostic studies?
options: Plain lumbar X-ray alone, then discharge if normal
MRI of the spine with and without contrast, plus blood cultures, ESR, and CRP
Bone scan only, with no blood work
CT myelogram as the first-line test
Empiric antibiotics with no imaging or cultures
stages.explanation: MRI with and without gadolinium is the imaging gold standard for vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis and any associated epidural abscess—it has high sensitivity early and defines neural compression. It should be paired with at least two sets of blood cultures (frequently positive in hematogenous osteomyelitis, especially S. aureus) and inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP), which are sensitive and useful for tracking response. Plain X-rays are insensitive early (changes lag by weeks) and cannot exclude infection. A bone scan lacks anatomic detail and specificity. CT myelogram is reserved for patients who cannot undergo MRI. Giving empiric antibiotics before cultures (in a stable patient without sepsis) can compromise pathogen identification needed for prolonged targeted therapy.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI thoracolumbar spine with/without contrast:
T2 hyperintensity and contrast enhancement of the L1 and L2 vertebral bodies with disc-space involvement
Small ventral epidural phlegmon, no significant cord compression
ESR 88 mm/hr | CRP 14 mg/dL
WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL
Blood cultures (2 sets): gram-positive cocci in clusters, speciation pending
HbA1c 9.2%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Cultures grow gram-positive cocci in clusters and he has a history of MRSA. He is neurologically stable without cord compression. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start IV vancomycin (empiric MRSA coverage) pending speciation/sensitivities, obtain ID consultation, and plan a prolonged course (typically ~6 weeks) of targeted IV antibiotics
Give a single dose of oral cephalexin and discharge
Proceed directly to emergent spinal surgery for all cases
Start a 5-day course of azithromycin
Withhold antibiotics until a CT-guided biopsy is obtained, even though blood cultures are already positive
stages.explanation: Given gram-positive cocci in clusters and prior MRSA, empiric IV vancomycin is appropriate while awaiting speciation and susceptibilities, then narrow accordingly. Vertebral osteomyelitis requires prolonged therapy—commonly about 6 weeks of IV (or highly bioavailable) antibiotics—guided by infectious disease. A short oral course or azithromycin is grossly inadequate for bone infection. Surgery is NOT required for all cases; it is reserved for cord compression, neurologic deficits, spinal instability, large abscess, or failure of medical therapy—this patient is neurologically stable with only a small phlegmon. When blood cultures are already positive with a likely pathogen, a CT-guided biopsy is not mandatory before starting therapy; biopsy is pursued mainly when blood cultures are negative.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On hospital day 3 he develops new bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and saddle anesthesia. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Continue current antibiotics and reassess in the morning
Add oral gabapentin for neuropathic symptoms and observe
Obtain urgent repeat MRI and emergent neurosurgical consultation for possible epidural abscess decompression
Discharge with outpatient physical therapy
Increase the antibiotic dose only and place a urinary catheter without imaging
stages.explanation: New, progressive neurologic deficits—bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and saddle anesthesia—signal an expanding epidural abscess causing cord/cauda equina compression, a surgical emergency. The correct action is urgent repeat MRI and emergent neurosurgical (and continued ID) involvement for decompression/drainage, because delayed surgery risks permanent paralysis and incontinence. Waiting until morning, adding gabapentin, or discharging are dangerous delays. Simply increasing antibiotics and catheterizing without imaging fails to address the compressive lesion; antibiotics alone cannot relieve acute mechanical cord compression. Time-to-decompression is the key determinant of neurologic recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis with epidural abscess (Staphylococcus aureus)
pearls: Back pain with fever, IV drug use, recent bacteremia, or immunocompromise is a red flag for vertebral osteomyelitis—worse at night/rest with focal spinal tenderness.
MRI with contrast plus blood cultures and ESR/CRP is the diagnostic core; plain films are insensitive early.
Most cases are treated with ~6 weeks of targeted IV antibiotics (cover MRSA empirically when suspected); surgery is for cord compression, instability, or medical failure.
New motor weakness, urinary retention, or saddle anesthesia means epidural abscess with cord/cauda equina compression—emergent MRI and surgical decompression.

cases.title: Racing Heart, Fever, and Frantic Agitation
cases.chiefComplaint: Pounding rapid heartbeat, fever, tremor, and severe agitation for one day.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman is brought to the ED with 1 day of palpitations, fever, profuse sweating, tremor, and escalating agitation and confusion. For several months she has had weight loss despite a big appetite, heat intolerance, frequent loose stools, anxiety, and a fine tremor. She was diagnosed with Graves disease last year but stopped her methimazole 6 weeks ago because she 'felt fine' and disliked the side effects. Two days ago she had a tooth infection and developed a fever. Past medical history: Graves disease, anxiety. Medications: none currently (stopped methimazole). No known drug allergies. She does not smoke; drinks socially. Family history: mother with hypothyroidism. She has had no chest pain at rest before today and no known heart disease. Her partner reports she became increasingly restless, then confused and combative over the last few hours. She has had no recent travel and no recreational stimulant use.
cases.exam: Agitated, diaphoretic, restless woman, oriented only intermittently. Warm, moist skin. Diffuse, non-tender goiter with an audible bruit; bilateral exophthalmos and lid lag. Cardiovascular: irregularly irregular tachycardia consistent with atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, no murmur, hyperdynamic precordium. Lungs clear. Fine resting tremor of the outstretched hands; brisk reflexes. Abdomen soft, hyperactive bowel sounds. No focal neurologic deficit. Mild bibasilar crackles. No neck rigidity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her presentation suggests thyroid storm. Besides supportive care and identifying the precipitant, which physiologic targets define the initial drug sequence?
options: Replace thyroid hormone and rewarm the patient aggressively
Give a single dose of iodine first, then nothing else
Sedate heavily and avoid all cardiac medications
Block adrenergic effects, block new hormone synthesis, then block hormone release—plus treat fever and the precipitant
Start levothyroxine and IV calcium
stages.explanation: Thyroid storm management targets several steps in sequence: (1) block peripheral adrenergic effects with a beta-blocker (propranolol also reduces T4→T3 conversion); (2) block new hormone synthesis with a thionamide (PTU or methimazole; PTU additionally blocks peripheral conversion); (3) block release of preformed hormone with iodine—but ONLY after the thionamide is on board; plus (4) treat hyperthermia (acetaminophen and cooling, avoid aspirin which displaces T4 from binding proteins) and address the precipitant (here, the dental infection). Glucocorticoids (e.g., hydrocortisone) are also given to reduce peripheral conversion and treat relative adrenal insufficiency. Replacing thyroid hormone or giving levothyroxine is exactly wrong. Calcium and heavy sedation alone do not treat the storm.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Why must the thionamide be given BEFORE iodine in thyroid storm?
options: Iodine and thionamides cannot be given on the same day
Thionamides only work if iodine has already been given
Iodine causes anaphylaxis unless a thionamide is given first
Giving iodine first can transiently increase thyroid hormone synthesis/stores; the thionamide must first block organification so iodine then suppresses release without fueling more hormone production
There is no required order; they are interchangeable
stages.explanation: Iodine (e.g., Lugol's solution or SSKI) acutely inhibits the release of preformed thyroid hormone (Wolff-Chaikoff effect). But if iodine is given to a gland whose synthetic machinery is unblocked, the extra iodide substrate can transiently INCREASE hormone synthesis and replenish stores—worsening the storm (the Jod-Basedow phenomenon in susceptible glands). Therefore a thionamide (PTU or methimazole) must be administered FIRST to block organification/new synthesis; iodine is then added (classically ~1 hour later) to suppress release safely. The other options are incorrect: there is no anaphylaxis rule, the order is NOT interchangeable, thionamides do not require prior iodine, and same-day administration is exactly what is done—just in the correct sequence.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
TSH <0.01 µU/mL (suppressed)
Free T4 markedly elevated | Free T3 markedly elevated
WBC 13.0 ×10³/µL
Glucose 142 mg/dL | Calcium 9.4 mg/dL
LFTs mildly elevated
ECG: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, rate ~165 bpm
Burch-Wartofsky score: high, consistent with thyroid storm

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She is in atrial fibrillation with RVR and developing pulmonary edema with crackles. Which agent best addresses both her adrenergic surge and rate control while you treat the storm?
options: IV propranolol (or esmolol) titrated carefully with monitoring
IV amiodarone loading dose as first-line, ignoring its iodine content
Immediate synchronized cardioversion as the sole therapy
IV verapamil pushed rapidly without monitoring
Digoxin alone at a low dose
stages.explanation: Beta-blockade is the cornerstone for controlling the adrenergic manifestations and rate in thyroid storm; propranolol (which also reduces peripheral T4→T3 conversion) or esmolol (short-acting, titratable—useful when heart failure status is uncertain) are preferred, given carefully with monitoring because severe thyrotoxic heart failure can be sensitive to negative inotropy. Amiodarone is iodine-rich and can paradoxically worsen thyrotoxicosis, so it is avoided as first-line here. Cardioversion of AF is unlikely to succeed and tends to recur while the patient remains profoundly thyrotoxic—treat the storm first. Rapid IV verapamil without monitoring risks hypotension/bradycardia. Digoxin alone is inadequate for the high adrenergic drive of storm.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which adjunctive medication reduces peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion AND treats possible relative adrenal insufficiency in thyroid storm?
options: IV hydrocortisone (glucocorticoid)
Oral levothyroxine
IV furosemide
Subcutaneous insulin
Oral calcium carbonate
stages.explanation: Glucocorticoids—classically IV hydrocortisone (or dexamethasone)—are a standard adjunct in thyroid storm: they reduce peripheral conversion of T4 to the more active T3 and treat the relative adrenal insufficiency that accompanies severe thyrotoxicosis (increased cortisol turnover/demand). Combined with the beta-blocker, thionamide, iodine, antipyretics/cooling, and treatment of the precipitant, they complete the multi-pronged regimen. Levothyroxine would add thyroid hormone—the opposite of what is needed. Furosemide treats volume overload symptomatically but does not address conversion or adrenal axis. Insulin and calcium carbonate have no specific role in the conversion/adrenal aspects of storm. She requires ICU-level monitoring until stabilized.

pearls: Thyroid storm is a clinical diagnosis (fever, tachyarrhythmia, agitation/AMS, GI symptoms in a thyrotoxic patient)—don't wait for labs to treat; identify the precipitant (infection, missed meds).
Drug sequence: beta-blocker (propranolol) → thionamide (PTU/methimazole) → iodine (after the thionamide) → glucocorticoid; add cooling/acetaminophen (avoid aspirin).
Give the thionamide BEFORE iodine—iodine in an unblocked gland can transiently increase hormone synthesis and worsen the storm.
Avoid amiodarone (iodine load) for rate control; treat the storm rather than cardioverting refractory thyrotoxic atrial fibrillation.

cases.title: Severe Upper Abdominal Pain Boring to the Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe constant epigastric pain radiating to the back for the past 12 hours.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents to the ED with 12 hours of severe, constant epigastric pain that bores straight through to his back and is partially relieved by leaning forward. He has vomited several times. The pain began the morning after a weekend of heavy drinking; he estimates 10-12 beers daily and admits to binge drinking. He denies hematemesis, melena, chest pain, or fever before arrival. He has had similar but milder episodes after drinking. PMH is notable for hypertension and a remote episode of "stomach inflammation." Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily. He takes no NSAIDs and reports no recent trauma. Allergies: none. Social history: drinks alcohol daily for 20 years, smokes half a pack per day, denies illicit drugs. Family history: father with type 2 diabetes; no known pancreatic or biliary disease. He has not eaten since the pain began and feels he cannot keep fluids down. He reports no prior gallstones and no recent fatty meals or jaundice.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable, diaphoretic, and lying still with knees drawn up. Sclerae are anicteric. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmur. The abdomen is soft but markedly tender in the epigastrium with voluntary guarding; there is no rebound, no rigidity, and bowel sounds are diminished. There is no Cullen or Grey Turner sign. Murphy sign is negative. There is no palpable mass or hepatomegaly. Mucous membranes are dry and capillary refill is slightly delayed at 3 seconds. The remainder of the exam, including the chest and extremities, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single test is most useful for confirming the suspected diagnosis in this patient?
options: Serum amylase
Serum lipase
Abdominal radiograph (KUB)
Serum alkaline phosphatase
stages.explanation: Serum lipase is the preferred confirmatory test for acute pancreatitis, with higher sensitivity and specificity than amylase and a longer window of elevation; a value greater than three times the upper limit of normal in the right clinical setting establishes the diagnosis. Amylase rises and falls more quickly and is less specific because it is elevated in many other conditions. A KUB may show a sentinel loop or rule out perforation but does not confirm pancreatitis. Alkaline phosphatase reflects biliary obstruction and is part of evaluating an etiology, not confirming pancreatic inflammation. Given his alcohol binge and classic epigastric pain radiating to the back, lipase is the highest-yield initial confirmatory test.

stages.label: LABS RETURN
stages.content:
Lipase 1,420 U/L (ref 13-60)
Amylase 680 U/L (ref 30-110)
WBC 14.2 ×10³/µL
Hct 49%
BUN 28 mg/dL
Cr 1.3 mg/dL
Glucose 188 mg/dL
Calcium 8.6 mg/dL
AST 88 U/L  ALT 64 U/L  Alk phos 102 U/L
Total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL
Triglycerides 210 mg/dL
Lactate 2.4 mmol/L

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming the diagnosis, what is the most important initial intervention?
options: Immediate broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
Urgent ERCP within 24 hours
Aggressive goal-directed IV crystalloid fluid resuscitation
Keep NPO for 7 days with total parenteral nutrition
stages.explanation: Early, goal-directed IV crystalloid resuscitation (e.g., lactated Ringer's) is the cornerstone of management and reduces complications; this patient has tachycardia, hemoconcentration (Hct 49%), and an elevated BUN signaling volume depletion. Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated in acute pancreatitis without evidence of infected necrosis or cholangitis. Urgent ERCP is reserved for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis or biliary obstruction—his picture is alcoholic with normal bilirubin and near-normal alkaline phosphatase. Prolonged NPO with TPN is outdated; early enteral feeding once tolerated is preferred and TPN increases infection risk. Fluid resuscitation with close monitoring of urine output and hematocrit is the priority.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Given the laboratory results, which test best clarifies the etiology and guides further workup?
options: Right upper quadrant abdominal ultrasound
Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen now
Serum IgG4 level
Endoscopic ultrasound
stages.explanation: All patients with a first episode of acute pancreatitis should have a right upper quadrant ultrasound to evaluate for gallstones and biliary dilation, because gallstones are a common reversible cause even when alcohol is suspected. His near-normal LFTs and bilirubin make biliary obstruction less likely, but ultrasound is still standard. Early contrast CT is not needed in mild pancreatitis and is most useful after 72 hours to assess necrosis if the patient deteriorates. IgG4 is for autoimmune pancreatitis, not suggested here. Endoscopic ultrasound is a later second-line test for idiopathic or recurrent disease, not the initial step.

stages.label: DISPOSITION & COUNSELING
stages.question: The ultrasound shows no gallstones and a normal common bile duct. Pain improves with fluids and analgesia. Which intervention most reduces his risk of recurrence?
options: Lifelong pancreatic enzyme replacement
Complete alcohol cessation with referral for treatment
Permanent low-fat diet only
Scheduled prophylactic cholecystectomy
stages.explanation: This is alcohol-induced acute pancreatitis, and complete alcohol cessation—paired with a brief intervention and referral to addiction treatment—is the single most effective way to prevent recurrence and progression to chronic pancreatitis. Pancreatic enzyme replacement is for exocrine insufficiency in established chronic pancreatitis, not acute prevention. A low-fat diet alone helps in hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis but does not address the alcohol cause. Cholecystectomy is indicated for gallstone pancreatitis, which was excluded by the normal ultrasound. Smoking cessation should also be encouraged as it independently accelerates pancreatic disease.

pearls: Lipase greater than 3x the upper limit of normal with characteristic pain confirms acute pancreatitis; it is more sensitive and specific than amylase.
Early goal-directed crystalloid resuscitation is the most important intervention; trend hematocrit, BUN, and urine output rather than giving prophylactic antibiotics.
Obtain a right upper quadrant ultrasound in every first episode to exclude gallstones, even when alcohol is the presumed cause.
Complete alcohol cessation is the key to preventing recurrence and progression to chronic pancreatitis.

cases.title: Gnawing Stomach Pain That Eases After Eating
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent burning epigastric pain for two months that improves after meals.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man presents to clinic with two months of intermittent burning, gnawing epigastric pain. He notes the pain is worst when his stomach is empty, often waking him at night, and is relieved within minutes of eating or taking antacids. He has lost no weight and continues to eat well. He denies dysphagia, vomiting, hematemesis, or black stools, but admits occasional nausea. He drinks 2-3 cups of coffee daily and one or two beers on weekends. PMH: osteoarthritis of the knees, for which he takes ibuprofen 400-600 mg two or three times daily "most days" for the past year. Other medications: none. Allergies: none. He has never been tested for H. pylori. Social history: works as a contractor, smokes occasionally, no illicit drugs. Family history: a brother with peptic ulcer disease; no family history of gastric cancer. He has tried over-the-counter calcium carbonate with temporary relief. He reports no fevers, no jaundice, and no change in bowel habits. He is concerned because his father had "ulcer surgery" decades ago.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and in no distress. Conjunctivae are pink without pallor. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation; there is no rebound, guarding, or rigidity. There is no palpable mass, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no epigastric bruit. Bowel sounds are normal. There are no stigmata of chronic liver disease, no lymphadenopathy, and no abdominal distension. Rectal exam reveals brown stool that is negative for occult blood. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature in this 56-year-old most warrants consideration before deciding between empiric therapy and endoscopy?
options: Pain relieved by eating
Daily coffee intake
His age combined with new-onset dyspepsia
Occasional weekend alcohol use
stages.explanation: Age is a key triage factor: guidelines recommend prompt upper endoscopy for patients with new dyspepsia who are 60 or older, or for any age with alarm features (weight loss, GI bleeding, dysphagia, anemia, vomiting, mass). At 56 with no alarm features, he can be managed with a non-invasive test-and-treat strategy, but his age places him near the threshold and must be weighed. Pain relieved by eating suggests a duodenal ulcer but does not itself dictate endoscopy. Coffee and weekend alcohol are minor lifestyle contributors and not decisive triage factors. Recognizing alarm features and the age cutoff is the central initial decision.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He has no alarm features. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Non-invasive H. pylori testing (urea breath test or stool antigen)
Serum H. pylori IgG antibody testing
Immediate upper endoscopy with biopsy
Empiric long-term high-dose PPI without any testing
stages.explanation: For dyspepsia without alarm features in a patient under 60, the test-and-treat strategy is preferred: a non-invasive active-infection test—urea breath test or stool antigen—identifies H. pylori, which is then eradicated. These tests detect current infection and can confirm cure. Serology detects past exposure and cannot distinguish active from cleared infection, so it is no longer recommended where better tests exist. Endoscopy is reserved for alarm features or age 60 and older. Indefinite empiric PPI without testing risks masking and undertreating H. pylori and is not the standard approach. His NSAID use should also be addressed regardless of the H. pylori result.

stages.label: TEST RESULTS
stages.content:
Urea breath test: POSITIVE for H. pylori
CBC: Hgb 14.6 g/dL, normal indices
BMP: within normal limits
Note: patient has not taken a PPI or antibiotics in the past 4 weeks

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no penicillin allergy and no prior macrolide exposure, but lives in a region with rising clarithromycin resistance. Which eradication regimen is most appropriate first-line?
options: Clarithromycin triple therapy (PPI, amoxicillin, clarithromycin) for 7 days
Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI, bismuth, tetracycline, metronidazole) for 14 days
PPI monotherapy for 8 weeks
Amoxicillin alone for 14 days
stages.explanation: Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI, bismuth subsalicylate, tetracycline, and metronidazole) for 14 days is preferred first-line where clarithromycin resistance is rising or with prior macrolide exposure, and it does not rely on clarithromycin susceptibility. Clarithromycin triple therapy is acceptable only where local resistance is low and the patient is macrolide-naive, and 7 days is too short—14 days is favored. PPI monotherapy treats acid but does not eradicate the organism, so the ulcer risk persists. Amoxicillin monotherapy is inadequate and promotes resistance. He should also stop the ibuprofen given its ulcerogenic role; if an NSAID is essential, switch to the lowest dose with PPI cover.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After completing therapy, how should eradication be confirmed?
options: Repeat serum H. pylori IgG antibody in 4 weeks
No confirmation is needed if symptoms resolve
Urea breath test or stool antigen at least 4 weeks after therapy and off PPI for 1-2 weeks
Repeat urea breath test the day after finishing antibiotics
stages.explanation: Confirmation of eradication is recommended and should use a urea breath test or stool antigen performed at least 4 weeks after completing antibiotics, with the PPI held for 1-2 weeks beforehand to avoid false negatives. Serology stays positive after cure and cannot confirm eradication. Relying on symptom resolution alone is unreliable because symptoms may persist or recur despite cure, or resolve without it. Testing the day after antibiotics gives false negatives because residual suppression has not cleared. Documenting eradication is especially important given his ulcer symptoms and ongoing arthritis pain that may require future NSAID use.

cases.diagnosis: Peptic (duodenal) ulcer disease from H. pylori and NSAID use
pearls: Pain relieved by food and nocturnal awakening classically suggests a duodenal ulcer; pain worsened by food suggests a gastric ulcer.
Use test-and-treat (urea breath or stool antigen) for dyspepsia under 60 without alarm features; reserve endoscopy for alarm features or age 60+.
Bismuth quadruple therapy for 14 days is preferred first-line where clarithromycin resistance is high or with prior macrolide exposure.
Confirm eradication with a urea breath test or stool antigen 4+ weeks after therapy, off PPI for 1-2 weeks, and discontinue offending NSAIDs.

cases.title: Fatigue and Cold Intolerance Early in Pregnancy
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening fatigue, cold intolerance, and constipation at 9 weeks of pregnancy.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman at 9 weeks gestation (G2P1) presents for prenatal care reporting several weeks of fatigue, cold intolerance, dry skin, and constipation that she attributes to early pregnancy. She was diagnosed with Hashimoto thyroiditis three years ago and takes levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She has not had her dose adjusted since conceiving and admits she sometimes takes it with her prenatal vitamin and breakfast. She denies palpitations, heat intolerance, or neck pain. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, prenatal vitamin with iron, vitamin D. Allergies: none. Social history: nonsmoker, no alcohol since learning she was pregnant, works as a teacher. Family history: mother with hypothyroidism, sister with type 1 diabetes. She is concerned about the baby and asks whether her thyroid medication is still working. Her last TSH, checked before pregnancy, was 2.1 mIU/L on her current dose. She has no goiter symptoms and reports normal fetal-related symptoms otherwise.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly fatigued but is in no distress. Skin is dry. The thyroid is mildly enlarged, firm, and non-tender without discrete nodules or bruit. There is no exophthalmos or lid lag. Cardiovascular exam shows a regular rhythm with no bradycardia on exam and no murmur. Deep tendon reflexes show slightly delayed relaxation at the ankles. There is no peripheral edema. The gravid uterus is not yet palpable abdominally. The remainder of the exam, including lungs and abdomen, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate step in managing her thyroid status?
options: Reassure her that pre-pregnancy dosing is sufficient and recheck after delivery
Check a serum TSH (with free T4) now to assess adequacy of replacement
Order a thyroid uptake scan to evaluate the gland
Stop levothyroxine and recheck thyroid function in 6 weeks
stages.explanation: Pregnancy markedly increases thyroid hormone requirements beginning in the first trimester, so a woman with known hypothyroidism needs a TSH (and free T4) checked promptly once pregnancy is confirmed and her symptoms suggest under-replacement. Reassurance with deferral ignores the physiologic rise in demand and her hypothyroid symptoms. A radionuclide uptake scan is contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal radiation exposure. Stopping levothyroxine is dangerous—untreated maternal hypothyroidism risks miscarriage, preterm birth, and impaired fetal neurodevelopment. Checking TSH now lets you titrate the dose to a pregnancy-appropriate target. She should also separate levothyroxine from iron and calcium by at least 4 hours.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
TSH 6.8 mIU/L (first-trimester target <2.5)
Free T4 0.8 ng/dL (low-normal)
TPO antibodies: strongly positive
HCG: appropriate for 9 weeks gestation
CBC: Hgb 11.4 g/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a TSH of 6.8 mIU/L at 9 weeks, what is the most appropriate management of her levothyroxine?
options: Continue 75 mcg daily and recheck in the third trimester
Increase the levothyroxine dose by approximately 25-30% now
Add liothyronine (T3) to her regimen
Switch to desiccated thyroid extract
stages.explanation: In pregnancy, levothyroxine requirements rise by roughly 25-30%, and her elevated TSH confirms under-replacement, so the dose should be increased now—often operationalized as adding two extra tablets per week or raising the daily dose proportionally—then rechecked. Continuing the current dose leaves her hypothyroid during the critical window of fetal neurodevelopment. Liothyronine/T3 is not recommended in pregnancy because T3 crosses the placenta poorly and monotherapy targets are based on T4/TSH; combination therapy is avoided. Desiccated thyroid extract has an unphysiologic T4:T3 ratio and is not preferred in pregnancy. Levothyroxine monotherapy with dose escalation is the standard.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: After increasing her dose, how should her thyroid function be monitored through pregnancy?
options: Recheck TSH only once in the third trimester
Monitor free T3 levels weekly
Recheck TSH every 4 weeks through the first half of pregnancy
No further monitoring is needed if she feels better
stages.explanation: TSH should be monitored approximately every 4 weeks during the first half of pregnancy (through about 20 weeks), then at least once near 30 weeks, because requirements rise progressively and dose adjustments are common. Checking only once in the third trimester misses the early, most critical period for fetal brain development. Weekly free T3 is not useful—T3 is not the monitoring target and weekly testing is excessive. Stopping monitoring based on symptoms is unsafe because biochemical hypothyroidism can persist despite symptom improvement. Frequent TSH checks with titration to the trimester-specific target keep both mother and fetus safe.

stages.label: POSTPARTUM
stages.question: What counseling about her levothyroxine is most appropriate for the postpartum period?
options: Continue the increased pregnancy dose indefinitely
Discontinue levothyroxine entirely once she delivers
Double the dose while breastfeeding
Return to her pre-pregnancy dose after delivery and recheck TSH in about 6 weeks
stages.explanation: After delivery, thyroid hormone requirements fall back toward the pre-pregnancy baseline, so the dose is typically reduced to the pre-pregnancy amount with a TSH check about 6 weeks postpartum to confirm. Continuing the higher pregnancy dose indefinitely risks iatrogenic hyperthyroidism. Stopping levothyroxine entirely is unsafe given her underlying Hashimoto hypothyroidism, which is permanent. Doubling the dose while breastfeeding is incorrect; lactation does not increase requirements the way pregnancy does, and levothyroxine is safe during breastfeeding at appropriate doses. She is also at risk for postpartum thyroiditis given positive TPO antibodies and should be monitored accordingly.

cases.diagnosis: Inadequately replaced hypothyroidism in pregnancy (Hashimoto thyroiditis)
pearls: Levothyroxine requirements rise about 25-30% in pregnancy, beginning in the first trimester; increase the dose promptly when TSH exceeds the trimester target.
First-trimester TSH target is generally below 2.5 mIU/L; monitor TSH roughly every 4 weeks through mid-pregnancy.
Separate levothyroxine from iron- and calcium-containing prenatal vitamins by at least 4 hours to ensure absorption.
Return to the pre-pregnancy dose after delivery and watch for postpartum thyroiditis in TPO-antibody-positive women.

cases.title: Loud Snoring and Falling Asleep During the Day
cases.chiefComplaint: Daytime sleepiness and loud snoring with witnessed pauses in breathing.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man comes to clinic at his wife's urging because of loud snoring and episodes where she sees him stop breathing during sleep, followed by gasping. He describes nonrestorative sleep, morning headaches, and dozing off at his desk and recently while stopped at a red light, which alarmed him. He denies cataplexy, sleep paralysis, or hypnagogic hallucinations. PMH: hypertension (poorly controlled on two agents) and type 2 diabetes. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. He drinks alcohol most evenings to "help him sleep" and does not smoke. Social history: works as an accountant, sedentary, BMI 35 kg/m^2, neck circumference 44 cm. Family history: father with hypertension and obesity. He reports decreased energy and low mood that he attributes to poor sleep. He has gained 20 pounds over two years. He has no history of stroke or heart failure and denies chest pain.
cases.exam: The patient is obese with a crowded oropharynx (Mallampati class IV) and a large tongue; the soft palate and uvula are low-lying. The neck is thick without thyromegaly or lymphadenopathy. Nasal passages are patent. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rhythm with a loud S2 but no murmur or gallop; jugular venous pressure is normal. Lungs are clear to auscultation. There is trace lower-extremity edema. There is no focal neurologic deficit. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which screening tool best stratifies this patient's pretest probability for the suspected disorder?
options: CHADS2-VASc score
STOP-BANG questionnaire
PHQ-9 depression screen
CURB-65 score
stages.explanation: The STOP-BANG questionnaire (Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apneas, Pressure/hypertension, BMI >35, Age >50, Neck >40 cm, male Gender) is validated to stratify risk for obstructive sleep apnea, and this patient scores high across nearly every item. The CHA2DS2-VASc score estimates stroke risk in atrial fibrillation, not sleep apnea. The PHQ-9 screens for depression—useful given his low mood but not diagnostic of the sleep disorder. CURB-65 assesses pneumonia severity. A high STOP-BANG score warrants objective sleep testing. His witnessed apneas, daytime somnolence, resistant hypertension, and obesity all point strongly toward obstructive sleep apnea.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His STOP-BANG score is 6 (high risk). What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Empirically start CPAP without testing
Polysomnography or a home sleep apnea test
Overnight pulse oximetry alone
Multiple sleep latency test (MSLT)
stages.explanation: Diagnosis of obstructive sleep apnea requires objective testing: in-laboratory polysomnography is the gold standard, and a home sleep apnea test is acceptable in patients with high pretest probability and no significant comorbidities such as heart failure or severe lung disease. Starting CPAP empirically is not standard because the diagnosis and severity (apnea-hypopnea index) should be established first. Overnight pulse oximetry alone is insufficiently sensitive and specific. The MSLT evaluates for narcolepsy and idiopathic hypersomnia, not obstructive sleep apnea. A sleep study quantifies the AHI, which guides therapy and supports insurance approval for treatment.

stages.label: SLEEP STUDY RESULTS
stages.content:
Polysomnography:
Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) 38 events/hour (severe ≥30)
Mostly obstructive events
Lowest oxygen saturation (nadir) 82%
Frequent arousals; reduced REM and slow-wave sleep
No periodic limb movements

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His AHI is 38 (severe OSA). What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP)
Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (surgery)
Modafinil for daytime sleepiness
Mandibular advancement device only
stages.explanation: CPAP is the first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea; it pneumatically splints the airway, reduces the AHI, improves daytime sleepiness, and can lower blood pressure—relevant to his resistant hypertension. Upper-airway surgery is reserved for selected patients who fail or cannot tolerate CPAP. Modafinil treats residual sleepiness only after adequate CPAP therapy and does not address the apneas or cardiovascular risk. A mandibular advancement device is an alternative for mild-to-moderate disease or CPAP-intolerant patients, but it is less effective than CPAP for severe OSA. He should also pursue weight loss, limit evening alcohol, and be counseled about drowsy driving.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Beyond CPAP, which counseling point is most important to address immediately given his recent dozing at a red light?
options: Begin a high-protein diet
Counsel on drowsy-driving risk and reporting requirements
Start melatonin nightly
Recommend daytime caffeine restriction
stages.explanation: Falling asleep at a red light signals dangerous, potentially impairing daytime sleepiness, so counseling about drowsy-driving risk—and advising him to avoid driving when sleepy until treated, plus any local reporting obligations—is the most urgent safety issue. A high-protein diet is not a priority intervention. Melatonin addresses circadian sleep-phase problems, not obstructive apnea, and will not fix his hypersomnolence. Caffeine restriction may marginally help sleep quality but does not address the acute safety hazard. Emphasizing both CPAP adherence and immediate driving precautions protects the patient and the public while therapy takes effect.

pearls: STOP-BANG efficiently stratifies OSA risk; a score of 5-8 indicates high probability warranting objective sleep testing.
Confirm OSA with polysomnography or a home sleep apnea test before initiating therapy; AHI >=30 defines severe disease.
CPAP is first-line for moderate-to-severe OSA and can improve resistant hypertension and daytime function.
Always address drowsy-driving safety and modifiable factors: weight loss and avoiding evening alcohol and sedatives.

cases.title: Getting Up All Night to Urinate
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of nocturia, weak urinary stream, and incomplete bladder emptying.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man presents with progressive lower urinary tract symptoms over the past six months. He gets up three or four times nightly to urinate, has a weak and intermittent stream, hesitancy, post-void dribbling, and a sense of incomplete emptying. He denies dysuria, gross hematuria, fever, or flank pain. He has not had urinary retention. PMH: hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily; he takes an over-the-counter antihistamine for allergies and asks whether it matters. Allergies: none. Social history: retired, drinks coffee and a beer in the evening, nonsmoker. Family history: father had prostate enlargement; an uncle had prostate cancer in his 80s. He is bothered enough that his sleep and daytime function suffer. He denies erectile complaints but is worried about treatment side effects. He has had no prior PSA testing. He reports no weight loss, bone pain, or neurologic symptoms. He drinks most of his fluids in the evening.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with no suprapubic fullness or palpable bladder. Digital rectal exam reveals a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, firm, non-tender prostate without nodules, induration, or asymmetry. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Neurologic exam, including lower-extremity strength, sensation, and perineal sensation, is intact, and anal sphincter tone is normal. Genital exam is unremarkable. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial test is most appropriate to evaluate his lower urinary tract symptoms?
options: Urinalysis
CT urogram
Cystoscopy
Urodynamic studies
stages.explanation: A urinalysis is the recommended initial test in men with new lower urinary tract symptoms to exclude infection, hematuria, and glucosuria before attributing symptoms to benign prostatic hyperplasia. A CT urogram is not a first-line study and exposes him to contrast and radiation without indication. Cystoscopy is reserved for hematuria, suspected stricture, or surgical planning. Urodynamic studies are second-line for diagnostic uncertainty or before surgery. A focused history, symptom score, digital rectal exam, and urinalysis form the standard initial workup; shared-decision PSA testing can be offered. His smooth, symmetric prostate and benign exam support BPH as the leading cause.

stages.label: INITIAL RESULTS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: clear, no blood, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, no glucose
Post-void residual (bladder scan): 60 mL (normal <100 mL)
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
PSA 2.4 ng/mL (age-appropriate, no prior for comparison)
IPSS (symptom score): 19 (moderate-to-severe), quality-of-life score elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His prostate feels modestly enlarged and his main complaints are bothersome flow symptoms. Which medication provides the fastest symptomatic relief?
options: Finasteride (5-alpha-reductase inhibitor)
Tamsulosin (alpha-blocker)
Oxybutynin (anticholinergic)
Sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor)
stages.explanation: An alpha-blocker such as tamsulosin relaxes prostatic and bladder-neck smooth muscle and improves flow symptoms within days, making it the fastest-acting first-line therapy for bothersome BPH symptoms. A 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor like finasteride shrinks the gland but takes 3-6 months to work and is best for larger prostates; it can be combined with an alpha-blocker. An anticholinergic such as oxybutynin targets storage/overactive-bladder symptoms and can worsen emptying or precipitate retention in obstruction. Sildenafil helps erectile dysfunction and has modest LUTS benefit but is not first-line for his obstructive symptoms. Counsel him about orthostatic hypotension and ejaculatory changes, and consider stopping the antihistamine, which can worsen emptying.

stages.label: TREATMENT SELECTION
stages.question: If his prostate were markedly enlarged (e.g., >40 mL) and he wanted to reduce long-term progression and the risk of acute urinary retention, which strategy is best?
options: Alpha-blocker monotherapy indefinitely
Add a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor (combination therapy)
Anticholinergic monotherapy
Immediate transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP)
stages.explanation: For men with significantly enlarged prostates, adding a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor (e.g., finasteride or dutasteride) to an alpha-blocker reduces prostate volume, improves symptoms, and lowers the long-term risk of acute urinary retention and the need for surgery—benefits shown in combination-therapy trials. Alpha-blocker monotherapy relieves symptoms but does not alter disease progression or gland size. Anticholinergic monotherapy is inappropriate for predominantly obstructive symptoms and risks retention. Immediate TURP is reserved for refractory symptoms, recurrent retention, stones, recurrent UTIs, or renal impairment, not first-line for a man amenable to medical therapy. 5-ARIs also lower PSA by roughly 50%, which must be accounted for in future cancer screening.

stages.label: COMPLICATIONS & RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which scenario would most strongly indicate the need for surgical referral rather than continued medical therapy?
options: A single nocturia episode after a large evening fluid intake
Mild improvement in symptom score on tamsulosin
Recurrent urinary retention and rising post-void residual despite medication
PSA of 2.4 ng/mL that is stable over time
stages.explanation: Recurrent acute urinary retention, a large or rising post-void residual, recurrent UTIs, bladder stones, gross hematuria of prostatic origin, or renal insufficiency from obstruction are indications for surgical intervention such as TURP. Occasional nocturia after heavy evening fluids is a behavioral issue addressed with fluid timing, not surgery. Symptomatic improvement on tamsulosin indicates successful medical therapy, not a need for surgery. A stable, age-appropriate PSA is reassuring and does not mandate intervention. Recognizing complications of bladder outlet obstruction distinguishes patients who need procedural management from those who can continue medications and lifestyle measures.

cases.diagnosis: Benign prostatic hyperplasia with bothersome lower urinary tract symptoms
pearls: Start the BPH workup with history, IPSS, digital rectal exam, urinalysis, and post-void residual; offer shared-decision PSA testing.
Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) relieve flow symptoms within days; 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors shrink large glands over months and reduce retention risk.
5-alpha-reductase inhibitors lower PSA by about 50%, which must be factored into prostate cancer screening.
Refer for surgery (e.g., TURP) for recurrent retention, rising post-void residual, recurrent UTIs, stones, gross hematuria, or obstructive renal failure.

cases.title: Worsening Kidney Numbers in a Long-Standing Diabetic
cases.chiefComplaint: Routine follow-up showing a gradual decline in kidney function over the past two years.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension presents for follow-up. He feels well but his eGFR has trended down from 75 to 48 mL/min/1.73 m^2 over two years, and his most recent labs again show reduced function. He denies edema, dyspnea, hematuria, or foamy urine, though he has noticed his blood pressure creeping up. He has background diabetic retinopathy. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and over-the-counter ibuprofen for occasional back pain. Allergies: none. Social history: former smoker (quit 5 years ago), minimal alcohol, sedentary. Family history: mother with diabetes on dialysis. His home blood pressures average around 150/88 mmHg. His last HbA1c was 8.2%. He takes no SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 agonist. He asks whether he is heading toward dialysis like his mother and what he can do to slow it. He reports no recent contrast imaging and no NSAID-related symptoms otherwise.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing and not in distress. There is no periorbital or pedal edema. Cardiovascular exam shows a regular rhythm with no murmur, rub, or gallop, and jugular venous pressure is normal. Lungs are clear bilaterally. The abdomen is soft and non-tender without bruits or organomegaly. Fundoscopic exam (or recent ophthalmology note) is consistent with diabetic retinopathy. Distal lower-extremity sensation to monofilament is mildly diminished. There is no asterixis and no uremic features. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which test best confirms and stages chronic kidney disease and guides management in this diabetic patient?
options: 24-hour urine creatinine clearance
Spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) plus repeat eGFR
Renal ultrasound with Doppler
Serum cystatin C alone
stages.explanation: CKD is defined and staged by both eGFR and albuminuria, so a spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio together with a repeat (sustained, >3 months) eGFR is the best way to confirm the diagnosis and risk-stratify. Albuminuria independently predicts progression and cardiovascular risk and helps confirm diabetic kidney disease. A 24-hour collection is cumbersome and rarely needed when spot UACR suffices. Renal ultrasound is useful to assess obstruction or kidney size but is not the primary staging tool. Cystatin C can refine eGFR estimates but does not replace albuminuria assessment. Confirming chronicity (over at least 3 months) distinguishes CKD from acute kidney injury.

stages.label: LABS RETURN
stages.content:
eGFR 47 mL/min/1.73 m^2 (confirmed, stable over 3 months)
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio 410 mg/g (severely increased, A3)
Serum K 4.6 mEq/L
Bicarbonate 22 mEq/L
HbA1c 8.2%
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Urinalysis: no hematuria, no casts; renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no obstruction

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His ACE inhibitor is sub-maximal and his albuminuria is severe. What is the most appropriate next step for renal and cardiovascular protection?
options: Stop the ACE inhibitor to protect the kidneys
Up-titrate the ACE inhibitor toward the maximum tolerated dose
Add a second-line antihypertensive but keep the ACE inhibitor at its current dose
Add an NSAID for better blood pressure control
stages.explanation: In diabetic CKD with albuminuria and hypertension, an ACE inhibitor (or ARB) titrated to the maximum tolerated dose is foundational because RAAS blockade reduces albuminuria and slows progression. Stopping it removes proven renoprotection; a modest, expected creatinine rise of up to ~30% with stable potassium is acceptable. Adding another agent while leaving the ACE inhibitor sub-maximal misses the chance to optimize the most renoprotective drug first. NSAIDs are nephrotoxic, raise blood pressure, and should be discontinued in CKD—his ibuprofen use should stop. Recheck potassium and creatinine within 1-2 weeks after up-titration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His eGFR is 47 and HbA1c is 8.2% on metformin. Which agent adds the greatest kidney and cardiovascular benefit?
options: An SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g., dapagliflozin)
A sulfonylurea (e.g., glipizide)
Pioglitazone
Discontinue metformin because of CKD
stages.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors slow CKD progression and reduce cardiovascular events and heart-failure hospitalizations in diabetic kidney disease with albuminuria, and they are recommended when eGFR is above the initiation threshold (generally >=20-25 mL/min/1.73 m^2). At an eGFR of 47 he qualifies and stands to gain substantial renoprotection beyond glucose lowering. A sulfonylurea lowers glucose but offers no renal protection and risks hypoglycemia. Pioglitazone can cause fluid retention and is not renoprotective. Metformin can be continued at eGFR 47 (reduce dose below 45 and stop below 30), so discontinuation now is unnecessary. A GLP-1 agonist is an alternative or add-on for additional cardiovascular and glycemic benefit.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding most clearly warrants referral to nephrology?
options: A single home blood pressure of 150/88 mmHg
Severely increased albuminuria (UACR >300 mg/g) with declining eGFR
HbA1c of 8.2%
Hemoglobin of 12.8 g/dL
stages.explanation: Nephrology referral is indicated for severely increased albuminuria (UACR >300 mg/g), a rapidly declining or sustained eGFR below 30, resistant hypertension, recurrent or persistent electrolyte disturbances, or uncertain etiology. This patient has A3 albuminuria with a falling eGFR, meeting referral criteria to co-manage progression and plan for advanced CKD. An isolated elevated home reading calls for better blood-pressure control, not immediate referral. An HbA1c of 8.2% prompts glycemic intensification, not nephrology referral by itself. A hemoglobin of 12.8 g/dL is normal and does not indicate CKD-related anemia. Early co-management improves outcomes and supports timely planning for renal replacement if needed.

cases.diagnosis: Diabetic chronic kidney disease (stage G3a, A3)
pearls: Stage CKD by both eGFR and albuminuria (UACR), and confirm chronicity over at least 3 months to exclude acute kidney injury.
Maximize ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy for albuminuria; tolerate up to a ~30% creatinine rise with stable potassium, and stop nephrotoxic NSAIDs.
Add an SGLT2 inhibitor in diabetic CKD with albuminuria for renal and cardiovascular protection when eGFR is above the initiation threshold.
Refer to nephrology for UACR >300 mg/g, eGFR <30, rapid decline, resistant hypertension, or unclear etiology.

cases.title: Constant Worry She Cannot Turn Off
cases.chiefComplaint: Six months of excessive worry, restlessness, and trouble sleeping affecting her daily life.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman presents with about eight months of persistent, excessive worry about her job, finances, her children's safety, and her health. She finds the worry hard to control and says it is present "most days." She reports restlessness, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension in her neck and shoulders, and trouble falling asleep. She has had several visits for headaches, palpitations, and a sense of her heart racing, with normal prior workups including a recent normal thyroid panel and ECG. She denies discrete panic attacks with peak-and-fall episodes, though she sometimes feels keyed up. She denies depressed mood most of the day, anhedonia, or suicidal thoughts, and denies trauma flashbacks. She drinks one glass of wine nightly and 3 cups of coffee daily. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social history: married, works in marketing, no tobacco or drugs. Family history: mother with an anxiety disorder. She is frustrated that everything keeps coming back normal and wonders if something is being missed.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, cooperative, and mildly anxious-appearing, sitting somewhat tensely and fidgeting. Speech is normal in rate and volume; thought process is linear. Affect is anxious but reactive, and mood is described as "on edge." There is no psychomotor slowing or evidence of internal stimulation. Cardiovascular exam shows a regular rhythm with no murmur; the thyroid is normal in size without nodules or bruit, and there is no tremor or exophthalmos. Neurologic exam is non-focal. Insight and judgment are intact, and she denies suicidal or homicidal ideation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which standardized tool best screens for and quantifies the severity of her likely disorder?
options: PHQ-9
GAD-7
AUDIT-C
Mini-Mental State Examination
stages.explanation: The GAD-7 is a validated, brief questionnaire that screens for generalized anxiety disorder and quantifies severity, making it the most appropriate tool given her chronic uncontrollable worry and somatic tension. The PHQ-9 screens for depression; while comorbid mood symptoms should be assessed, it does not target anxiety severity. The AUDIT-C screens for hazardous alcohol use—worth checking given her nightly wine, but not diagnostic of anxiety. The Mini-Mental State Examination screens cognition/dementia and is irrelevant here. The GAD-7 also helps track response to treatment over time. Her normal thyroid, ECG, and exam make a primary anxiety disorder, rather than a medical mimic, most likely.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT RESULTS
stages.content:
GAD-7 score: 15 (severe anxiety)
PHQ-9 score: 6 (mild; no suicidal ideation)
TSH: normal
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
Basic labs (CBC, BMP): unremarkable
Urine toxicology: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her symptoms have persisted for 8 months. Which feature most supports generalized anxiety disorder over panic disorder?
options: Discrete sudden surges of fear peaking within minutes
Fear of having another attack in public places
Chronic, hard-to-control worry about multiple domains most days for over 6 months
Recurrent unexpected episodes with chest tightness and depersonalization
stages.explanation: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by excessive, difficult-to-control worry about multiple life domains occurring more days than not for at least 6 months, accompanied by symptoms such as restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, muscle tension, and sleep disturbance—exactly her presentation. Discrete surges of fear peaking within minutes and recurrent unexpected attacks with depersonalization describe panic attacks/panic disorder. Fear of attacks in public places suggests agoraphobia. While she occasionally feels keyed up, she lacks the discrete, crescendo panic episodes that define panic disorder, and her pervasive chronic worry meets GAD criteria.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has severe symptoms and wants the most effective evidence-based treatment. What is the best first-line approach?
options: Scheduled benzodiazepine such as alprazolam
An SSRI and/or cognitive behavioral therapy
As-needed beta-blocker only
Buspirone as monotherapy for immediate relief
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for generalized anxiety disorder is an SSRI (or SNRI) and/or cognitive behavioral therapy; combining medication and CBT is often most effective, and either alone is appropriate based on patient preference. Scheduled benzodiazepines are not first-line for chronic GAD because of dependence, tolerance, sedation, and rebound anxiety, though they are sometimes used briefly while an SSRI takes effect. An as-needed beta-blocker helps situational performance anxiety symptoms but does not treat chronic GAD. Buspirone is a reasonable second-line or augmenting agent but is not the single best first-line choice and is not fast-acting. She should also be counseled to reduce caffeine and alcohol, which can worsen anxiety and sleep.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP & COUNSELING
stages.question: She agrees to start sertraline. What counseling about expectations is most important?
options: She should feel completely better within 2-3 days
She should stop the medication as soon as she feels calmer
Full benefit may take 4-6 weeks, and anxiety can briefly worsen when starting
SSRIs are addictive and should be taken only as needed
stages.explanation: Patients should be told that SSRIs take 4-6 weeks for full anxiolytic benefit and that anxiety, jitteriness, or GI symptoms can transiently worsen in the first week or two—starting at a low dose and titrating slowly mitigates this. Expecting relief in days sets up disappointment and premature discontinuation. Stopping once calmer risks relapse; treatment is typically continued for 6-12 months after remission, and SSRIs should be tapered, not stopped abruptly, to avoid discontinuation symptoms. SSRIs are not addictive and are not used as-needed; consistent daily dosing is required. Setting accurate expectations improves adherence and outcomes, and follow-up should monitor response with the GAD-7.

pearls: GAD is excessive, hard-to-control worry about multiple domains most days for >=6 months with somatic symptoms; use the GAD-7 to screen and track severity.
Rule out medical mimics (thyroid disease, arrhythmia, substances) and distinguish GAD from panic disorder, which features discrete crescendo attacks.
First-line treatment is an SSRI/SNRI and/or CBT; avoid chronic benzodiazepines due to dependence and tolerance.
Counsel that SSRIs take 4-6 weeks to work, may transiently increase anxiety, and should be continued then tapered, not stopped abruptly.

cases.title: Morning Shakiness and a Drink to Steady the Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: Reports needing a morning drink to stop shaking and feeling he drinks too much.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man comes to clinic at his wife's request, acknowledging he drinks more than he should. He drinks 8-10 standard drinks most days and has begun having a drink in the morning to calm tremors and nausea. He has tried to cut down without success and feels guilty. He has missed work twice this month. He denies prior seizures or delirium during past periods of reduced drinking, but reports sweating and anxiety when he goes too long without alcohol. PMH: hypertension and reflux. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily; he stopped his amlodipine months ago. Allergies: none. Social history: works in sales, drinks daily for over a decade, smokes a half pack per day, denies other drugs. Family history: father with alcohol use disorder and liver disease. He last drank this morning. He wants help but is afraid of stopping suddenly because he once "felt awful" when he ran out of alcohol for a day. He denies hematemesis, abdominal swelling, or confusion.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly anxious with a fine tremor of the outstretched hands and is slightly diaphoretic. He is fully alert and oriented without confusion, hallucinations, or agitation. Sclerae are anicteric and there are no spider angiomata, palmar erythema, or gynecomastia. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with no hepatomegaly, ascites, or shifting dullness. Cardiovascular exam shows mild tachycardia with a regular rhythm. Neurologic exam shows intact orientation and no asterixis. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which validated screening instrument best characterizes the severity of his drinking?
options: CAGE questionnaire only
AUDIT (Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test)
GAD-7
MELD score
stages.explanation: The AUDIT is a 10-item validated tool that screens for and grades the spectrum of unhealthy alcohol use, including dependence severity, and is well suited to quantify his risk and guide intervention. The CAGE questionnaire is a brief 4-item screen that flags possible problem drinking but does not quantify severity or consumption as thoroughly as AUDIT. The GAD-7 screens for anxiety, not alcohol use. The MELD score estimates liver-disease mortality and is not a screening tool for use disorder. His features—tolerance, morning drinking to relieve withdrawal, failed attempts to cut down, and craving—already meet criteria for a moderate-to-severe alcohol use disorder, which the AUDIT will reflect.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT & LABS
stages.content:
AUDIT score: 24 (high-risk / likely dependence)
AST 78 U/L  ALT 45 U/L (AST:ALT ratio ~1.7)
GGT elevated
MCV 102 fL (macrocytosis)
Platelets 145 ×10³/µL
INR 1.1; total bilirubin normal
Glucose, electrolytes (incl. magnesium, potassium): within normal limits
CIWA-Ar at visit: 8 (mild)

stages.label: WITHDRAWAL RISK
stages.question: He wants to stop drinking. Given his history of feeling "awful" when alcohol runs out and his current findings, what is the most appropriate immediate concern?
options: Reassure him that abrupt cessation is completely safe at home
Assess and manage alcohol withdrawal risk, including benzodiazepines if indicated
Start naltrexone today and have him stop drinking immediately
Prescribe disulfiram and send him home to quit cold turkey
stages.explanation: Because alcohol withdrawal can progress to seizures and delirium tremens, the immediate priority is to assess withdrawal severity (e.g., CIWA-Ar) and risk factors and to manage withdrawal—benzodiazepines are the mainstay when symptoms warrant, with thiamine supplementation and a decision about the appropriate setting. Telling him abrupt cessation is completely safe is dangerous given autonomic signs and a prior bad experience. Starting naltrexone with immediate cessation ignores acute withdrawal and is inappropriate timing for maintenance therapy. Disulfiram with unsupervised cold-turkey quitting does not treat withdrawal and disulfiram is not first-line. Give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After his withdrawal is safely managed and he is no longer drinking, which medication is the best first-line option to maintain abstinence and reduce craving?
options: Naltrexone
Disulfiram as first-line for everyone
Chronic daily benzodiazepine
No medication; counseling alone is sufficient
stages.explanation: Naltrexone (oral or long-acting injectable) is a first-line medication for alcohol use disorder that reduces craving and heavy-drinking days; it is appropriate once he is past acute withdrawal and not using opioids. Acamprosate is the other first-line agent, especially with hepatic concerns. Disulfiram is second-line and best for highly motivated, supervised patients given adherence and reaction issues; it is not first-line for everyone. Chronic daily benzodiazepines are used only for the acute withdrawal period, not maintenance, due to dependence risk. Medication plus counseling/behavioral support outperforms counseling alone. Note his transaminases are mildly elevated; monitor liver function, though naltrexone is generally acceptable without severe hepatic failure.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which supplementation is most important to give early to prevent a serious neurologic complication in this patient?
options: Vitamin C
Thiamine (vitamin B1)
Vitamin D
Calcium
stages.explanation: Thiamine (vitamin B1) is essential to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, a triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia, which is precipitated when glucose is given to a thiamine-deficient patient; thiamine should be administered before or with any glucose. His macrocytosis and chronic heavy drinking signal nutritional deficiency. Vitamin C, vitamin D, and calcium address other deficiencies but do not prevent the acute, potentially irreversible neurologic emergency that thiamine deficiency causes. Magnesium and folate repletion are also appropriate adjuncts in alcohol use disorder, but thiamine is the critical priority to prevent Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome.

cases.diagnosis: Alcohol use disorder (moderate-to-severe) with withdrawal risk
pearls: The AUDIT quantifies the spectrum of unhealthy alcohol use; morning drinking, tolerance, and failed cutdown attempts indicate dependence.
Before maintenance therapy, assess withdrawal risk (CIWA-Ar); benzodiazepines are the mainstay for significant withdrawal to prevent seizures and DTs.
Naltrexone and acamprosate are first-line for maintaining abstinence; disulfiram is second-line for motivated, supervised patients.
Give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy in heavy drinkers.

cases.title: A Painful One-Sided Band of Blisters
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of burning pain followed by a one-sided rash on the left torso.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents with a painful rash on her left flank. Three days ago she developed burning, tingling pain in a band across the left side of her trunk; yesterday clustered blisters appeared in the same area. The pain is sharp and the skin is tender to light touch. She denies the rash crossing the midline, no involvement of the face or eyes, and no blurred vision. She feels generally well with no high fever. PMH: type 2 diabetes and hypertension; she recently completed a course of prednisone for a COPD exacerbation. Medications: metformin, amlodipine, an inhaled bronchodilator; she finished prednisone last week. Allergies: none. Social history: lives alone, retired, nonsmoker now (former smoker). Family history: noncontributory. She received childhood chickenpox but has not had the shingles vaccine. She is worried because a neighbor told her the pain can last for months.
