She is immunocompetent aside from recent steroid use and diabetes, and is otherwise functioning normally. She presents within 72 hours of rash onset.
cases.exam: On the left flank there is a unilateral, dermatomal band of grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, several with early crusting, not crossing the midline (T9-T10 distribution). The area is exquisitely tender, with allodynia to light touch. There is no facial, periocular, or nasal-tip involvement, and the eyes are uninvolved. There is no purulent discharge, surrounding cellulitic spread, or lymphangitis. Mucous membranes are clear. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. There is no nuchal rigidity and the neurologic exam is otherwise non-focal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the clinical picture, what is the most appropriate way to establish the diagnosis?
options: Skin biopsy with histopathology
Clinical diagnosis from the dermatomal vesicular rash
Blood cultures
Serum varicella IgG titer
stages.explanation: Herpes zoster is typically a clinical diagnosis based on the classic unilateral, dermatomal, painful vesicular eruption that does not cross the midline—exactly this presentation—so no confirmatory test is needed before treating. A skin biopsy is unnecessary for a typical case and delays therapy. Blood cultures are for suspected bacteremia, not localized zoster. A varicella IgG titer only confirms prior exposure (nearly universal) and does not diagnose active reactivation. When the presentation is atypical, disseminated, or immunocompromised, PCR of vesicle fluid (the test of choice) or direct fluorescent antibody can confirm, but here recognition and prompt treatment are key.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She presents within 72 hours of rash onset. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Topical antibiotics only
Oral antiviral therapy (e.g., valacyclovir) started now
Oral corticosteroids as monotherapy
Reassurance with no pharmacologic therapy
stages.explanation: Oral antiviral therapy—valacyclovir, famciclovir, or acyclovir—started within 72 hours of rash onset reduces the duration of the eruption and acute pain and may lower the risk of postherpetic neuralgia, so it should begin now; her age and recent steroid use further favor treatment. Topical antibiotics treat bacterial superinfection, which is absent here, and do not address the virus. Corticosteroids as monotherapy are not standard and may be added only in select cases with antivirals, not alone. Withholding treatment misses the antiviral window in an older, higher-risk patient. Treatment is also reasonable beyond 72 hours if new vesicles are still forming or in complicated disease.

stages.label: COMPLICATIONS
stages.question: She asks what could cause the pain to persist. What is the most common complication of this condition?
options: Postherpetic neuralgia
Bacterial endocarditis
Guillain-Barre syndrome
Disseminated intravascular coagulation
stages.explanation: Postherpetic neuralgia—dermatomal neuropathic pain persisting (commonly beyond 90 days) after the rash resolves—is the most common complication of herpes zoster, and older age is the strongest risk factor, making this 68-year-old patient susceptible. Bacterial endocarditis is unrelated to zoster. Guillain-Barre syndrome is a rare post-infectious neuropathy and not a typical zoster complication. Disseminated intravascular coagulation is not characteristic of localized zoster. Antiviral therapy and good pain control can reduce its likelihood and severity; treatment options for established PHN include gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants, and topical agents. Counseling about this risk helps set expectations.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: After she recovers, what is the most appropriate prevention strategy for this patient going forward?
options: Live attenuated zoster vaccine now while she has the rash
Recombinant zoster vaccine (two doses) after the acute episode resolves
Lifelong prophylactic acyclovir
No vaccine is needed since she has now had shingles
stages.explanation: The recombinant zoster vaccine (two doses) is recommended for immunocompetent adults aged 50 and older and is given after the acute episode resolves; having had an episode does not provide reliable protection against recurrence, so vaccination is still indicated. The live attenuated vaccine is no longer preferred and should never be given during active infection or to immunocompromised patients. Lifelong prophylactic acyclovir is not used for routine prevention in immunocompetent patients. Assuming no vaccine is needed after an episode is incorrect because recurrence can occur. Given her age, diabetes, and recent steroid use, the recombinant vaccine offers durable protection once she has healed.

pearls: Herpes zoster is a clinical diagnosis: a painful, unilateral, dermatomal vesicular rash that does not cross the midline; use vesicle-fluid PCR if atypical.
Start oral antivirals within 72 hours of rash onset (or later if new vesicles are forming) to shorten the course and reduce postherpetic neuralgia risk.
Postherpetic neuralgia is the most common complication, with advancing age the strongest risk factor.
Recommend the recombinant zoster vaccine (two doses) for immunocompetent adults 50+, given after the acute episode resolves.

cases.title: Shoulder Pain When Reaching Overhead
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of right shoulder pain that is worst when reaching overhead.
cases.history: A 53-year-old right-handed man presents with about six weeks of gradually worsening right shoulder pain. The pain is worst when he reaches overhead or to the side, such as putting away dishes or reaching into the back seat, and he notes a painful catch midway through raising the arm. He sleeps poorly because lying on that side aggravates the pain. He denies acute trauma, locking, or a sensation of the shoulder giving way, and denies neck pain or radiating arm numbness. He works as a house painter, frequently working overhead. PMH: well-controlled hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily; he takes acetaminophen with partial relief. Allergies: none. Social history: nonsmoker, social alcohol, recreational tennis. Family history: noncontributory. He denies fever, weight loss, or night sweats. He has had no prior shoulder injuries or surgeries. He is worried he may have a torn rotator cuff requiring surgery and asks whether he needs an MRI right away. He has full use of the hand and no neurologic symptoms.
cases.exam: Inspection of the right shoulder shows no deformity, swelling, or muscle atrophy. There is tenderness over the greater tuberosity and the subacromial region. Active range of motion reproduces pain through a painful arc between roughly 60 and 120 degrees of abduction, with full but uncomfortable elevation. Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy impingement tests are positive. Empty-can (Jobe) test elicits pain but strength is largely preserved; external rotation strength is intact. Drop-arm test is negative. There is no cervical spine tenderness, Spurling test is negative, and distal neurovascular exam of the arm is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination maneuver is most specifically used to assess for subacromial impingement in this patient?
options: Spurling test
Phalen test
Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy tests
Lachman test
stages.explanation: The Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy tests provoke subacromial impingement by compressing the supraspinatus tendon and subacromial bursa against the acromion, and both are positive here, supporting rotator cuff tendinopathy/impingement. The Spurling test evaluates cervical nerve-root compression (radiculopathy), which his normal neck exam and absent radicular symptoms make unlikely. The Phalen test is for carpal tunnel syndrome at the wrist. The Lachman test assesses anterior cruciate ligament integrity in the knee. The painful arc between 60 and 120 degrees, positive impingement signs, and preserved strength with a negative drop-arm test point to tendinopathy rather than a full-thickness tear.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His drop-arm test is negative and strength is largely preserved. Which finding would most suggest a full-thickness rotator cuff tear instead of tendinopathy?
options: A painful arc on abduction
Positive drop-arm test with significant weakness in abduction/external rotation
Tenderness over the greater tuberosity
Pain with overhead reaching
stages.explanation: A positive drop-arm test with substantial weakness in abduction or external rotation suggests a full-thickness rotator cuff tear, because the patient cannot hold or smoothly lower the arm against gravity due to loss of tendon integrity. A painful arc, greater-tuberosity tenderness, and pain with overhead reaching are all consistent with tendinopathy/impingement and can occur with or without a tear, so they do not distinguish a complete tear. His preserved strength and negative drop-arm test favor tendinopathy. Significant, persistent weakness or a positive drop-arm would prompt imaging (MRI or ultrasound) and possible surgical referral.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging is not yet indicated for his suspected tendinopathy. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Immediate MRI and orthopedic surgery referral
Activity modification, NSAIDs, and a structured physical therapy program
Long-arm immobilization in a sling for 6 weeks
Immediate subacromial corticosteroid injection as the sole treatment
stages.explanation: First-line management of rotator cuff tendinopathy/impingement without signs of a full-thickness tear is conservative: relative activity modification (avoiding aggravating overhead work), NSAIDs for pain, and a structured physical therapy program emphasizing rotator cuff and scapular strengthening, which is effective for most patients. Immediate MRI and surgery referral are unnecessary without weakness, a suspected complete tear, or failure of conservative care. Prolonged sling immobilization risks adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) and is not indicated. A corticosteroid injection alone can provide short-term relief and may be considered as an adjunct if pain limits rehabilitation, but it is not the standalone first-line treatment. Reassess in about 6 weeks.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After 8 weeks of adherent physical therapy and NSAIDs, his pain and overhead function are markedly improved. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Obtain an MRI to confirm the diagnosis anyway
Refer for surgical decompression
Start chronic daily opioids for residual pain
Continue the home exercise program and activity modification, with ergonomic adjustments at work
stages.explanation: Because he has improved with conservative therapy, the best next step is to continue the home exercise program and activity modification and to optimize workplace ergonomics—important for a painter who works overhead—to prevent recurrence. Obtaining an MRI now would not change management in a patient who is responding well. Surgical decompression is reserved for patients who fail an adequate trial of conservative care or who have a significant structural tear, neither of which applies. Chronic daily opioids are inappropriate for a musculoskeletal tendinopathy and carry dependence risk. Reinforcing rehabilitation gains and addressing the occupational overhead exposure gives the best long-term outcome.

cases.diagnosis: Rotator cuff tendinopathy with subacromial impingement
pearls: A painful arc (60-120 degrees) with positive Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy tests and preserved strength suggests rotator cuff tendinopathy/impingement.
A positive drop-arm test with significant abduction/external-rotation weakness raises concern for a full-thickness tear and prompts imaging.
First-line treatment is conservative: activity modification, NSAIDs, and structured physical therapy; avoid prolonged immobilization to prevent frozen shoulder.
Reserve MRI and surgical referral for suspected complete tears or failure of an adequate conservative trial.

cases.title: Progressive Fatigue and Tingling Feet
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of worsening tiredness and numbness in both feet.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman presents with 4 months of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea climbing stairs, and a symmetric "pins and needles" sensation in both feet that has crept up to her ankles. She also reports an unsteady gait in the dark and mild forgetfulness her daughter has noticed. Her diet is adequate but she eats little meat. PMH: hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, vitiligo, and GERD treated with omeprazole 20 mg daily for 6 years. She has had no GI surgery. Medications also include calcium and a multivitamin she takes "sometimes." Allergies: none. Social: lifelong nonsmoker, two glasses of wine weekly, retired teacher, lives with her husband. Family history: a sister with type 1 diabetes and a mother with "thyroid trouble." Review of systems is positive for a sore, smooth tongue and occasional palpitations, and negative for melena, hematochezia, heavy menses (postmenopausal), and weight loss.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale with mild scleral icterus. The tongue is smooth, red, and beefy. Cardiac exam reveals a soft systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border; lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender without hepatosplenomegaly. Neurologic exam shows diminished vibration sense and proprioception at both great toes, absent ankle reflexes, and a positive Romberg sign. Strength is 5/5 throughout. There are depigmented vitiliginous patches on the hands. No lymphadenopathy. No glossitis-related ulceration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial laboratory study is most likely to explain her combined anemia and neurologic findings?
options: Serum ferritin and iron studies
Complete blood count with peripheral smear and serum vitamin B12 level
Hemoglobin A1c
Thyroid-stimulating hormone alone
stages.explanation: A CBC with smear plus a serum B12 level directly targets the unifying diagnosis: macrocytic anemia with a peripheral neuropathy and dorsal-column signs (vibration/proprioception loss, Romberg) points to B12 deficiency. Iron studies are wrong because iron deficiency causes a microcytic anemia and does not cause subacute combined degeneration. HbA1c screens for diabetic neuropathy but would not explain macrocytosis or glossitis, and her risk profile (autoimmune) favors B12. TSH alone is reasonable to recheck given hypothyroidism but does not explain the neuropathy or beefy tongue; her levothyroxine is presumably controlling it. The smear may reveal macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils, supporting megaloblastic anemia.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL
MCV 112 fL
WBC 4.1 ×10³/µL
Platelets 132 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils
Reticulocyte count 1.2%
Vitamin B12 110 pg/mL (normal 200–900)
Folate 9 ng/mL (normal)
TSH 2.4 µIU/mL
Total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL (indirect 1.6)
LDH 480 U/L (elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: B12 deficiency is confirmed. Which test best establishes pernicious anemia as the underlying cause?
options: Anti-intrinsic factor antibody
Schilling test
Serum gastrin level
Methylmalonic acid level
stages.explanation: Anti-intrinsic factor (IF) antibodies are highly specific for pernicious anemia (autoimmune atrophic gastritis destroying parietal cells and IF), the most likely cause in this woman with vitiligo, autoimmune thyroid disease, and a family history of type 1 diabetes. The Schilling test is largely obsolete and no longer available in most centers. Serum gastrin is often elevated in pernicious anemia but is nonspecific and also rises with chronic PPI use, limiting its diagnostic value here. Methylmalonic acid is excellent for confirming true B12 deficiency at the cellular level when levels are borderline, but it identifies deficiency, not its pernicious-anemia etiology. Anti-parietal cell antibodies are sensitive but less specific than anti-IF.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Anti-intrinsic factor antibody returns positive. What is the most appropriate initial replacement strategy?
options: High-dose oral cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg daily only
A single IM dose now with no further therapy
Intramuscular cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg, initially frequent dosing then maintenance
Increase dietary meat intake and recheck in 3 months
stages.explanation: Pernicious anemia eliminates intrinsic factor, so reliable repletion uses parenteral B12: IM cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg given on a frequent loading schedule (e.g., several doses over the first weeks) followed by monthly maintenance, typically lifelong. High-dose oral B12 can work via passive absorption and is acceptable for maintenance in some patients, but initial parenteral therapy is standard, especially with neurologic involvement where rapid, reliable repletion matters. A single dose will not maintain stores in an ongoing absorption defect. Dietary change is futile because the problem is malabsorption, not intake. Monitor for hypokalemia during early brisk erythropoiesis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Three days after starting B12, she develops new muscle weakness and palpitations. Which lab abnormality and follow-up are most important to anticipate?
options: Hypercalcemia; check ionized calcium
Hypokalemia from cellular potassium uptake during rapid erythropoiesis; monitor and replace potassium
Hypernatremia; restrict free water
Hyperkalemia; give insulin and dextrose
stages.explanation: Brisk reticulocyte response after B12 (or folate) repletion drives potassium into newly forming cells, causing hypokalemia, which can manifest as weakness, cramps, and arrhythmia/palpitations. Anticipate and monitor potassium during early treatment, supplementing as needed. Hypercalcemia is unrelated to repletion. Hypernatremia is not a feature of this process. Hyperkalemia is the opposite of what occurs—potassium falls, not rises—so insulin/dextrose would be harmful. Iron deficiency may also become unmasked as erythropoiesis accelerates, so monitoring iron studies and reticulocytes confirms an appropriate response.

cases.diagnosis: Vitamin B12 deficiency due to pernicious anemia
pearls: Macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils plus dorsal-column signs (lost vibration/proprioception, positive Romberg) is subacute combined degeneration from B12 deficiency.
Anti-intrinsic factor antibody is specific for pernicious anemia; methylmalonic acid confirms true B12 deficiency when levels are borderline.
Always replete B12 before or alongside folate—giving folate alone corrects the anemia but lets neurologic damage progress.
Anticipate hypokalemia in the first days of repletion as potassium shifts into rapidly proliferating red cells.

cases.title: Hospitalized Man with New Confusion
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive confusion and lethargy noted by nursing on hospital day 3.
cases.history: A 75-year-old man admitted 3 days ago for community-acquired pneumonia is now increasingly confused and lethargic. He was started on ceftriaxone and azithromycin and is clinically improving from a respiratory standpoint—afebrile, weaning oxygen. Nursing reports he is disoriented to place and time and had a brief episode of myoclonic jerking. He takes no diuretics. PMH: 40-pack-year smoking history, COPD on tiotropium, hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily, and a 6 mm lung nodule noted on a prior CT. Medications during admission: ceftriaxone, azithromycin, amlodipine, scheduled acetaminophen, and maintenance IV fluids with D5-1/2 NS that were running. Allergies: none. Social: current smoker, no alcohol, retired machinist, lives alone. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems is limited by his confusion but the nurse notes good oral intake, no vomiting, no diarrhea, and no recent diuretic or excess free-water ingestion beyond the IV fluids.
cases.exam: The patient is somnolent but arousable, oriented only to self. Mucous membranes are moist and there is no axillary dryness. JVP is normal (not flat, not elevated). Cardiac exam is regular without murmur. Lungs show resolving right-base crackles. There is no peripheral edema and no ascites. Skin turgor is normal. Neurologic exam shows no focal deficit; reflexes are symmetric; he has intermittent myoclonus. No asterixis. Capillary refill is brisk and he is clinically euvolemic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step in evaluating his new confusion?
options: Obtain a STAT non-contrast head CT before any labs
Send a basic metabolic panel, serum osmolality, urine osmolality, and urine sodium
Empirically start hypertonic saline
Begin broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed meningitis
stages.explanation: New confusion with myoclonus in a euvolemic inpatient most efficiently begins with metabolic studies: a BMP plus paired serum and urine osmolality and urine sodium will rapidly characterize a likely electrolyte disturbance (hyponatremia) and its mechanism. Head CT is reasonable if a focal deficit, trauma, or no metabolic cause emerges, but ordering imaging before basic chemistries delays the most likely diagnosis. Empiric hypertonic saline is dangerous without knowing the sodium and volume status. Meningitis antibiotics are unwarranted in an afebrile, improving pneumonia patient with no meningismus. The clinical picture—euvolemia, possible SIADH from pneumonia/medications—directs the targeted metabolic workup.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Sodium 118 mEq/L
Potassium 4.1 mEq/L
Glucose 96 mg/dL
BUN 8 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.7 mg/dL
Serum osmolality 248 mOsm/kg (low)
Urine osmolality 540 mOsm/kg
Urine sodium 62 mEq/L
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL
AM cortisol 16 µg/dL
Uric acid 2.4 mg/dL (low)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which interpretation of these results best identifies the cause of his hyponatremia?
options: Hypovolemic hyponatremia from poor intake
Hyperglycemic pseudohyponatremia
SIADH—euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine
Hypervolemic hyponatremia from occult heart failure
stages.explanation: He has true hypotonic hyponatremia (serum osm 248) with inappropriately concentrated urine (540 mOsm/kg) and high urine sodium (62 mEq/L) while clinically euvolemic, normal TSH and cortisol, and a low uric acid—classic for SIADH. Likely drivers include pneumonia, possibly the underlying lung nodule (small-cell concern), and hypotonic IV fluids worsening it. Hypovolemia is excluded by euvolemic exam, low BUN, and high urine sodium. Pseudohyponatremia is excluded—glucose is normal and measured serum osmolality is genuinely low. Hypervolemic causes like heart failure show edema, elevated JVP, and low urine sodium, none present here. The low uric acid is a supportive SIADH clue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given Na 118 with confusion and myoclonus, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Aggressive normal saline bolus to raise sodium quickly
Cautious hypertonic (3%) saline targeting a controlled correction with frequent sodium monitoring, plus fluid restriction
Free water bolus to dilute the urine
Start a vasopressin receptor agonist
stages.explanation: Symptomatic hyponatremia (confusion, myoclonus) at 118 warrants careful 3% saline to relieve cerebral edema, with frequent sodium checks, alongside fluid restriction and stopping the hypotonic IV fluids that are aggravating SIADH. Normal saline boluses can paradoxically worsen SIADH because the kidney excretes the salt and retains the water. A free water bolus is exactly wrong—it would drop the sodium further. A vasopressin agonist would worsen water retention; a vasopressin antagonist (vaptan) is the agent that promotes free-water excretion, not an agonist. The key is controlled, monitored correction.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What correction rate ceiling minimizes the risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome over the first 24 hours?
options: No more than about 8 mEq/L in 24 hours
At least 20 mEq/L in 24 hours to relieve symptoms fast
Exactly 15 mEq/L in the first 6 hours
Correction rate does not matter once symptoms resolve
stages.explanation: To prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis), limit correction to roughly 6–8 mEq/L per 24 hours (many use ≤8, with a more conservative ≤6 in high-risk patients—elderly, chronic hyponatremia, alcohol, malnutrition). Brief targeted 3% saline can raise sodium a few mEq quickly to abort seizures, but the 24-hour total must stay capped. Twenty mEq/L and 15 mEq in 6 hours grossly exceed safe limits and risk irreversible demyelination presenting days later with dysarthria, quadriparesis, and "locked-in" features. Rate always matters; overcorrection is dangerous even after symptoms improve, and re-lowering with DDAVP/free water may be needed if limits are exceeded.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His sodium safely normalizes. Which outpatient step best addresses a potential underlying cause of his SIADH?
options: Repeat chest imaging and pursue evaluation of the lung nodule
Lifelong daily normal saline infusions
Permanent severe fluid restriction regardless of sodium
Start a thiazide diuretic
stages.explanation: In an older heavy smoker with a known lung nodule and new SIADH, malignancy—particularly small-cell lung cancer, a classic ectopic ADH source—must be excluded, so dedicated chest imaging and nodule evaluation are the right next step. Lifelong saline infusions are impractical and ineffective for SIADH. Permanent severe restriction irrespective of sodium is not individualized care; restriction is titrated to the cause and response. A thiazide causes or worsens hyponatremia and is contraindicated here. Identifying and treating the underlying trigger (infection resolution, drug review, or occult malignancy) is the durable fix.

cases.diagnosis: Euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia due to SIADH
pearls: SIADH = euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg), urine Na >30–40 mEq/L, and low uric acid, with normal thyroid/adrenal function.
Correct symptomatic hyponatremia with 3% saline but cap the rise at ~6–8 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination.
Normal saline can worsen SIADH; the kidney excretes the salt and retains free water—fluid restriction is the mainstay.
New SIADH in an older smoker mandates evaluation for small-cell lung cancer as an ectopic ADH source.

cases.title: Severe Headache with Crushing Chest Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Pounding headache and chest pressure with a very high home blood pressure reading.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man arrives by car with 2 hours of substernal chest pressure radiating to the jaw, a pounding occipital headache, and blurred vision. He checked his BP at a pharmacy and it read "too high." He admits to running out of his antihypertensives 3 weeks ago. PMH: long-standing hypertension, type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Prior medications (lapsed): lisinopril 40 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: 1 pack-per-day smoker for 35 years, occasional alcohol, works construction, recently used over-the-counter decongestants for a cold. Family history: father died of stroke at 60. Review of systems is positive for mild dyspnea and nausea and negative for focal weakness, slurred speech, seizure, abdominal/back pain, and leg swelling. He denies cocaine or stimulant use.
cases.exam: The patient is diaphoretic and uncomfortable. Fundoscopic exam reveals flame hemorrhages and arteriolar narrowing; no frank papilledema appreciated. Cardiac exam reveals an S4 gallop without murmur; lungs have bibasilar crackles. There is no carotid bruit. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with intact speech and symmetric strength. Abdomen is soft and nontender without bruit or pulsatile mass. Peripheral pulses are equal in both arms; no inter-arm BP differential. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding most clearly distinguishes a hypertensive emergency from severe asymptomatic hypertension (urgency)?
options: The absolute BP value of 220/130 mmHg
Acute target-organ damage—chest pain with cardiac strain, AKI, and retinal hemorrhages
A history of medication nonadherence
A headache alone
stages.explanation: A hypertensive emergency is defined by acute target-organ damage, not by a numeric threshold. This man shows ongoing organ injury: ischemic-type chest pain with an S4 and pulmonary crackles (cardiac), retinal flame hemorrhages (eye), and—as labs will show—acute kidney injury. The BP value alone does not define an emergency; many patients tolerate 220/130 without acute injury (that would be urgency). Nonadherence explains the rise but isn't the defining feature. Isolated headache is nonspecific and, without other organ findings, would not by itself establish emergency. The presence of evolving organ damage mandates controlled IV therapy rather than oral outpatient management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Troponin 0.09 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
Creatinine 2.3 mg/dL (baseline 1.4)
BUN 38 mg/dL
Potassium 4.6 mEq/L
ECG: LVH with lateral ST depressions
Chest X-ray: mild pulmonary vascular congestion
Urinalysis: 2+ protein, no cells
CT head (non-contrast): no hemorrhage or infarct

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How quickly and by how much should his blood pressure be lowered initially?
options: Normalize BP to <120/80 mmHg within the first hour
Lower BP only if it exceeds 240/140 mmHg
Reduce mean arterial pressure by no more than ~25% in the first hour, then toward 160/100 over the next several hours
Give a large dose of oral nifedipine to drop it rapidly
stages.explanation: In most hypertensive emergencies (without aortic dissection), reduce mean arterial pressure by no more than about 25% within the first hour, then to roughly 160/100 over the next 2–6 hours, using titratable IV agents. Aggressive normalization risks watershed cerebral, coronary, and renal hypoperfusion because chronically hypertensive patients have right-shifted autoregulation. Waiting for >240/140 ignores active organ damage already present. Short-acting oral nifedipine causes precipitous, uncontrolled drops and reflex tachycardia and is contraindicated. The goal is smooth, monitored reduction—here also treating his cardiac ischemia/strain—to avoid hypoperfusion injury.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which intravenous agent is most appropriate given his chest pain, mild pulmonary congestion, and acute kidney injury?
options: IV hydralazine bolus
Oral clonidine load
IV sodium nitroprusside as first choice given his AKI
IV nitroglycerin (or nicardipine) titrated to effect
stages.explanation: With myocardial ischemia and pulmonary congestion, IV nitroglycerin reduces preload, relieves ischemia, and is easily titrated; nicardipine is an excellent alternative offering smooth, titratable control without affecting renal dosing adversely. Hydralazine causes unpredictable drops and reflex tachycardia—poor for active ischemia. Oral clonidine is slow and not titratable for an emergency. Nitroprusside is potent but in renal impairment risks thiocyanate/cyanide accumulation, so it is not the first choice in AKI/CKD; it also can worsen coronary steal. Nitroglycerin or nicardipine best matches this cardiac-and-renal scenario, allowing controlled lowering while treating ischemia.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Thirty minutes after starting therapy his BP falls to 120/70 and he becomes confused with rising creatinine. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Evolving ischemic stroke unrelated to treatment
New aortic dissection
Cerebral and renal hypoperfusion from overly rapid BP reduction
Hypoglycemia from metformin
stages.explanation: A rapid drop from 220/130 to 120/70 overshoots the recommended ~25% first-hour reduction. In a chronically hypertensive patient with right-shifted autoregulation, this causes cerebral hypoperfusion (confusion) and worsened renal perfusion (rising creatinine)—the very hypoperfusion injury controlled lowering is meant to avoid. The fix is to ease off the infusion and allow the pressure to rise modestly. A spontaneous stroke is less likely given the clear temporal link to the BP plunge and the nonfocal exam. Dissection would typically cause tearing pain and a pulse/BP differential, absent here. Metformin does not cause hypoglycemia by itself, and glucose was normal.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive emergency with cardiac ischemia and acute kidney injury
pearls: Hypertensive emergency is defined by acute target-organ damage (brain, heart, kidney, eyes, aorta), not by a specific BP number.
Lower MAP by no more than ~25% in the first hour, then to ~160/100 over hours, using titratable IV agents to avoid hypoperfusion.
Match the drug to the organ: nitroglycerin/nicardipine for ischemia and congestion; avoid nitroprusside in renal impairment; avoid short-acting oral nifedipine entirely.
Overcorrection causes watershed cerebral, coronary, and renal ischemia—new confusion or rising creatinine after a rapid drop signals iatrogenic hypoperfusion.

cases.title: Sudden One-Sided Chest Pain in a Tall Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt sharp right-sided chest pain and shortness of breath at rest.
cases.history: A 21-year-old man presents with the sudden onset of sharp right-sided chest pain that began an hour ago while sitting watching television. The pain worsens with deep breaths and is associated with mild breathlessness. He denies trauma, fever, cough, or recent travel. He has had no similar episodes. PMH: none; no asthma or prior lung disease. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: smokes about half a pack of cigarettes daily and occasionally vapes; college student; denies cocaine, denies recent scuba diving or flights. Family history: a cousin had a "collapsed lung." He is notably tall and slender. Review of systems is negative for hemoptysis, leg swelling, calf pain, palpitations, syncope, and constitutional symptoms. He has no connective-tissue disease diagnosis but mentions he is "double-jointed."
cases.exam: The patient is a tall, thin, well-appearing man in mild distress with pleuritic pain. The trachea is midline. There is decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance to percussion over the right upper chest, with reduced tactile fremitus on that side; the left lung is clear. Cardiac exam is regular without murmur or rub; heart sounds are normal and not displaced. There is no JVD, no tracheal deviation, and no subcutaneous emphysema. Calves are nontender without swelling. He speaks in full sentences.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step in this stable patient?
options: Immediate needle decompression of the right chest
Upright chest X-ray (or bedside lung ultrasound)
CT pulmonary angiography for PE
D-dimer testing
stages.explanation: An upright chest X-ray (or bedside ultrasound) is the appropriate first test to confirm a suspected primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a hemodynamically stable patient, and it lets you measure size to guide management. Needle decompression is reserved for tension pneumothorax—signaled by hypotension, tracheal deviation, distended neck veins, and severe distress—none present here. CT pulmonary angiography and D-dimer target pulmonary embolism, which is far less likely in a tall, thin young smoker with unilateral hyperresonance and decreased breath sounds; chasing PE first would delay the correct diagnosis. The classic exam (decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance, reduced fremitus, midline trachea) already strongly suggests pneumothorax.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Upright chest X-ray: right-sided pneumothorax with a visible pleural line
Distance from chest wall to pleural line at the level of the hilum ~1.5 cm
No mediastinal shift
Lungs otherwise clear; no infiltrate or effusion
No rib fracture

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which underlying mechanism best explains a primary spontaneous pneumothorax in this patient?
options: Rupture of apical subpleural blebs
Necrotizing pneumonia eroding into the pleura
Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome)
Pulmonary embolism causing pleural air
stages.explanation: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax classically results from rupture of small apical subpleural blebs/bullae, most common in tall, thin young men who smoke—exactly this patient. Smoking markedly increases risk, and a family history can reflect connective-tissue predisposition (e.g., Marfan-spectrum). Necrotizing pneumonia would cause a secondary pneumothorax and present with fever, cough, and an infiltrate, absent here. Boerhaave syndrome causes pneumomediastinum/effusion after forceful vomiting, not this picture. Pulmonary embolism does not introduce pleural air. Recognizing the bleb mechanism explains both the demographic and the recurrence risk that informs counseling, including smoking cessation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a small (~1.5 cm) primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a stable, minimally symptomatic patient, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate large-bore chest tube thoracostomy
Supplemental oxygen with observation and a repeat chest X-ray to confirm stability/resolution
Emergent VATS pleurodesis
Discharge now with no imaging follow-up
stages.explanation: A small primary spontaneous pneumothorax (commonly defined as <2–3 cm from the chest wall) in a stable, minimally symptomatic patient can be managed with observation plus supplemental oxygen, which speeds reabsorption of pleural air, followed by a repeat chest X-ray to confirm stability or resolution before discharge with close follow-up. A chest tube is reserved for large or symptomatic pneumothoraces or clinical deterioration. VATS/pleurodesis is for recurrent or persistent disease, not a first small episode. Discharging without confirmatory follow-up imaging is unsafe because the air could enlarge. Counsel on smoking cessation and air-travel/diving precautions.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful observation, which counseling point is most important to prevent recurrence and complications?
options: He may resume scuba diving immediately
Smoking cessation, and avoid air travel and diving until cleared and resolved
Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics
He should never exercise again
stages.explanation: Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for recurrence, so cessation is the highest-yield counseling point; he should also avoid air travel until the pneumothorax has fully resolved and avoid scuba diving (potentially permanently after a spontaneous pneumothorax unless definitively treated) because pressure changes can precipitate recurrence or tension physiology. Resuming diving immediately is dangerous. Prophylactic antibiotics have no role—this is not an infection. Telling him never to exercise is excessive and unsupported; routine activity can resume after resolution. Recurrence is common, so he should seek care promptly if symptoms return, and recurrent episodes may warrant pleurodesis or bleb resection.

pearls: Classic primary spontaneous pneumothorax: tall, thin young male smoker with sudden pleuritic pain, unilateral hyperresonance, decreased breath sounds, and reduced fremitus.
Tension pneumothorax (hypotension, tracheal deviation, distended neck veins) is a clinical diagnosis warranting immediate needle decompression—do not wait for imaging.
Small, stable primary pneumothoraces can be observed with supplemental oxygen, which accelerates pleural air reabsorption; reserve chest tubes for large or symptomatic cases.
Smoking cessation is the key to reducing recurrence; avoid air travel until resolved and counsel against scuba diving.

cases.title: Expanding Rash After a Weekend Hike
cases.chiefComplaint: An enlarging circular rash on the thigh with low-grade fevers and aches.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents with a slowly enlarging red rash on his right thigh that he first noticed 6 days ago. It is mostly painless, occasionally slightly itchy, and has grown to about 8 cm with some central clearing. He has had low-grade fevers, fatigue, myalgias, and a mild headache. Ten days ago he went hiking through tall grass and wooded trails in rural Connecticut; he recalls removing a tick from that area but is unsure how long it was attached. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: works in software, no tobacco, social alcohol, lives in the Northeast, owns a dog. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for arthralgias and the constitutional symptoms above and negative for facial droop, neck stiffness, palpitations, syncope, joint swelling, chest pain, and visual changes. He has not traveled internationally and has had no other rashes.
cases.exam: On the right anterolateral thigh there is a single 8 cm erythematous, annular macular lesion with partial central clearing (target-like), warm but not tender, without vesicles, pustules, or scale, and without a palpable cord. There is no regional lymphangitic streaking; a small nontender inguinal node is palpable. Cardiac rhythm is regular with no bradycardia or irregularity, and no murmur. Neurologic exam shows intact cranial nerves with no facial palsy and no meningismus. Joints are without effusion or restricted motion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate approach to diagnosis at this visit?
options: Send two-tier Lyme serology and wait for results before treating
Diagnose early localized Lyme disease clinically based on erythema migrans and exposure, and treat now
Order a skin biopsy of the lesion
Treat empirically for cellulitis with anti-staphylococcal antibiotics
stages.explanation: Erythema migrans in a patient with an appropriate exposure (a tick bite while hiking in an endemic Northeastern area) is a clinical diagnosis of early localized Lyme disease, and treatment should begin immediately without waiting for serology. Two-tier serology is often falsely negative in the first weeks because antibodies have not yet developed, so it should not delay therapy for classic EM. A biopsy is unnecessary and nonspecific for a classic lesion. Anti-staphylococcal coverage for cellulitis is wrong: this annular, target-like, minimally tender, non-purulent lesion is EM, not bacterial cellulitis, and would not respond appropriately. Clinical recognition prevents progression to disseminated disease.

stages.label: EXAM DETAIL
stages.content:
Lesion photograph documented: 8 cm annular erythema with central clearing
No vesiculation or necrosis
No additional satellite lesions on full skin exam
Neurologic exam normal; no facial nerve palsy
Cardiac auscultation normal; resting HR 74 with regular rhythm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most distinguishes erythema migrans from a local hypersensitivity reaction to the tick bite?
options: Immediate appearance within hours of the bite
Gradual expansion over days to a large (>5 cm) annular lesion, often with central clearing
Intense pain and purulent drainage
Confinement to less than 1 cm in diameter
stages.explanation: Erythema migrans characteristically expands gradually over days to weeks, becoming a large (>5 cm) annular lesion that may show central clearing—reflecting spreading spirochetal infection. A hypersensitivity reaction appears within hours and resolves quickly, staying small. Pain and purulence suggest bacterial infection or abscess, not EM, which is typically painless or mildly pruritic. A lesion confined to <1 cm is more consistent with a simple bite reaction. Recognizing the slow centrifugal expansion and size helps clinicians treat early disease and avoid misclassifying it as an allergic bite reaction, which would be left untreated.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line antibiotic for early localized Lyme disease in this nonpregnant adult?
options: Doxycycline for a 10–14 day course
IV ceftriaxone for 28 days
Topical mupirocin
Azithromycin as the preferred first-line agent
stages.explanation: Oral doxycycline is first-line for early localized Lyme disease in nonpregnant adults, typically for 10–14 days; it also covers possible co-transmitted Anaplasma. Amoxicillin or cefuroxime are acceptable alternatives (e.g., in pregnancy or doxycycline intolerance). IV ceftriaxone is reserved for neurologic involvement (meningitis), high-grade AV block/carditis, or refractory arthritis—not uncomplicated EM. Topical mupirocin does not treat systemic spirochetal infection. Azithromycin is only a second-line alternative when first-line agents cannot be used, owing to lower efficacy. Prompt oral doxycycline reliably treats early disease and prevents dissemination to joints, heart, and nervous system.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling and follow-up point is most appropriate?
options: He needs lifelong antibiotic suppression to prevent 'chronic Lyme'
Counsel on tick-bite prevention and return precautions for facial palsy, palpitations/syncope, or joint swelling indicating dissemination
Repeat serology weekly to confirm cure
Avoid all outdoor activity permanently
stages.explanation: Appropriate counseling covers tick-bite prevention (repellents, protective clothing, prompt tick removal, checking after outdoor exposure) and return precautions for signs of disseminated disease: facial (Bell's) palsy, carditis (palpitations, lightheadedness, syncope from AV block), or migratory arthritis with joint swelling. Lifelong suppression for "chronic Lyme" is not evidence-based and is not recommended. Serial serology to confirm cure is unhelpful—antibodies persist for months to years after successful treatment and do not track cure. Permanently avoiding the outdoors is unnecessary; sensible precautions suffice. Most patients fully recover after a standard oral course.

pearls: Erythema migrans with a compatible exposure is a clinical diagnosis—treat immediately; serology is often falsely negative in the first weeks.

Oral doxycycline (10–14 days) is first-line for early localized Lyme in nonpregnant adults and also covers Anaplasma; reserve IV ceftriaxone for meningitis, high-grade carditis, or refractory arthritis.
EM expands gradually over days to a >5 cm annular lesion with central clearing and is usually painless—unlike a same-day, small, pruritic bite hypersensitivity reaction.
Counsel on tick-bite prevention and warn about disseminated features: facial palsy, AV-block carditis, and migratory arthritis.

cases.title: College Student with Weeks of Fatigue and Sore Throat
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of severe fatigue, sore throat, and tender neck swelling.
cases.history: An 18-year-old college freshman presents with about 2 weeks of profound fatigue, a sore throat, low-grade fevers, and tender swelling at the back of her neck. She has missed classes because she is "exhausted no matter how much I sleep." She reports mild left upper abdominal fullness. She lives in a dorm and has a new boyfriend; she shares drinks with roommates. PMH: none. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill. Allergies: none known. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol at parties, no illicit drugs. Family history: noncontributory. Of note, an urgent-care clinician 4 days ago prescribed amoxicillin for presumed strep throat, after which she developed a diffuse maculopapular rash. Review of systems is positive for the rash, sore throat, malaise, and decreased appetite, and negative for dysuria, dyspnea, jaundice, severe abdominal pain, and recent trauma. She has had no sick contacts with documented strep.
cases.exam: The patient is tired-appearing but nontoxic. There is tender posterior cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally and some anterior nodes; tonsils are enlarged with exudates but no peritonsillar bulge or uvular deviation. A faint diffuse maculopapular rash is present on the trunk. Abdominal exam reveals a palpable spleen tip about 2 cm below the left costal margin, mildly tender; no hepatomegaly and no rebound. There is no jaundice. The pharynx is erythematous; no oral ulcers. Lungs are clear and heart sounds are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation most strongly suggests infectious mononucleosis over streptococcal pharyngitis?
options: Anterior cervical nodes and tonsillar exudates only
Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and marked fatigue, plus a rash after amoxicillin
Sudden high fever with no lymphadenopathy
Isolated rhinorrhea and cough
stages.explanation: Posterior cervical adenopathy, splenomegaly, and prolonged profound fatigue—plus the classic maculopapular rash triggered by amoxicillin—strongly point to infectious mononucleosis (EBV) rather than strep. Anterior nodes with exudates alone overlap with strep and are nonspecific. Sudden high fever without adenopathy is atypical for mono, which features prominent lymphadenopathy. Rhinorrhea and cough suggest a viral URI and actually argue against strep but are not the mono picture either. The amoxicillin-associated rash is a near-pathognomonic clue: most patients with mono who receive aminopenicillins develop this nonallergic rash. Splenomegaly also raises management implications regarding activity.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Heterophile (Monospot) antibody: positive
WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL with 62% lymphocytes
Peripheral smear: many atypical (reactive) lymphocytes
AST 78 U/L, ALT 84 U/L (mildly elevated)
Rapid strep antigen: negative
Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Monospot is positive. What is the most likely causative organism and its typical lab footprint?
options: Group A Streptococcus; neutrophilic leukocytosis
Cytomegalovirus; always heterophile positive
Epstein-Barr virus; lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes and mild transaminitis
HIV acute retroviral syndrome; positive Monospot
stages.explanation: Epstein-Barr virus causes most heterophile-positive mononucleosis, producing a lymphocytosis with reactive (atypical) lymphocytes and frequently a mild transaminitis from hepatic involvement—exactly this patient's labs. Group A strep causes a neutrophilic picture and a negative Monospot, and her rapid strep is negative. CMV can cause a mono-like illness but is typically heterophile-negative, so a positive Monospot favors EBV. Acute HIV can mimic mono but does not cause a positive heterophile test; still, given a new partner, HIV testing is reasonable to consider. The reactive lymphocytes and positive heterophile here are classic EBV.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important activity-related counseling given her splenomegaly?
options: Resume contact sports immediately to maintain fitness
Avoid contact sports and heavy lifting for at least 3–4 weeks (longer if spleen still enlarged) to prevent splenic rupture
Strict bed rest with no activity for 6 months
No restrictions are necessary
stages.explanation: Splenomegaly in mononucleosis predisposes to splenic rupture, a rare but life-threatening complication, so patients should avoid contact/collision sports and heavy lifting for at least 3–4 weeks from symptom onset, and longer if the spleen remains enlarged—some clinicians confirm resolution before clearing high-risk athletes. Returning to contact sports immediately is dangerous. Six months of strict bed rest is excessive and unnecessary; light activity as tolerated is fine. No restrictions ignores the real rupture risk. Most rupture cases occur within the first weeks. She should seek immediate care for sudden severe left-upper-quadrant or left-shoulder (Kehr sign) pain suggesting rupture.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She asks why she developed a rash on amoxicillin. What is the best explanation and guidance?
options: She has a true penicillin allergy and must avoid all beta-lactams for life
The rash means the mono is becoming bacterial and needs IV antibiotics
The aminopenicillin rash in EBV is usually a non-IgE reaction and does not by itself establish a lifelong penicillin allergy; primarily supportive care for mono
She should take high-dose corticosteroids routinely for all mono
stages.explanation: The widespread maculopapular rash that appears when amoxicillin/ampicillin is given during EBV mononucleosis is typically a non-allergic, non-IgE-mediated reaction and does not, by itself, prove a lifelong penicillin allergy—though many patients are cautiously labeled and future allergy evaluation can clarify. Management of mono is supportive: rest, hydration, analgesics/antipyretics, and avoiding the unnecessary antibiotic (which was never indicated—rapid strep was negative). Labeling a permanent penicillin allergy outright may be inaccurate. The rash does not signal bacterial superinfection. Routine systemic corticosteroids are not recommended for uncomplicated mono and are reserved for complications like impending airway obstruction from tonsillar swelling.

pearls: Mono triad: posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and prolonged fatigue, with reactive lymphocytes and a positive heterophile (Monospot) test.
A maculopapular rash after amoxicillin/ampicillin is a classic non-allergic clue to EBV and usually does not establish a true lifelong penicillin allergy.
Counsel against contact sports and heavy lifting for at least 3–4 weeks (longer if the spleen stays enlarged) to prevent splenic rupture.
Treatment is supportive; reserve corticosteroids for complications such as airway-threatening tonsillar swelling.

cases.title: Neck Fullness and Sluggishness in a Middle-Aged Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradual neck enlargement with fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents with several months of increasing neck fullness, fatigue, cold intolerance, mild constipation, dry skin, and a 4 kg weight gain despite no diet change. She also notes heavier, longer menstrual periods. She denies neck pain, rapid enlargement, hoarseness, dysphagia, or compressive symptoms. PMH: vitiligo. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as an accountant, two children. Family history: mother has hypothyroidism; a sister has type 1 diabetes. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, and menorrhagia, and negative for palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor, exophthalmos, diarrhea, fevers, and neck tenderness. She has had no prior thyroid disease or radiation exposure to the neck.
cases.exam: The patient has mildly dry skin and slightly puffy facies. The thyroid is diffusely enlarged, firm, rubbery, and nontender, without a dominant nodule, bruit, or overlying erythema; it moves with swallowing and there is no fixed mass. There is no proptosis, lid lag, or tremor. Deep tendon reflexes show a delayed relaxation phase. Cardiac exam reveals bradycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmur. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy and no stridor. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory evaluation?
options: Serum TSH with reflex free T4
Thyroid ultrasound first, before any labs
Radioactive iodine uptake scan
Fine-needle aspiration of the gland
stages.explanation: Serum TSH (with free T4) is the initial test for suspected thyroid dysfunction; her hypothyroid symptoms, bradycardia, and delayed-relaxation reflexes point to hypothyroidism, and TSH is the most sensitive screen. Ultrasound is useful when a nodule or compressive concern exists but is not the first step for evaluating diffuse hypofunction without a dominant nodule. A radioactive iodine uptake scan is used to evaluate hyperthyroidism etiologies, not hypothyroidism. FNA is for suspicious nodules, and her gland is diffusely enlarged without a dominant nodule, so it is not indicated initially. Antibody testing will help confirm autoimmune etiology after the functional status is defined.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
TSH 14.5 µIU/mL (elevated)
Free T4 0.6 ng/dL (low-normal/low)
Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibody: strongly positive
Anti-thyroglobulin antibody: positive
Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL
LDL within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits her diffuse goiter, symptoms, and antibody profile?
options: Graves disease
Hashimoto thyroiditis (chronic autoimmune thyroiditis)
Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis
Iodine-deficiency goiter
stages.explanation: A diffuse, firm, nontender goiter with strongly positive anti-TPO (and anti-thyroglobulin) antibodies and an elevated TSH with low/low-normal free T4 is classic Hashimoto thyroiditis, the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas; her personal vitiligo and family autoimmune history fit the autoimmune cluster. Graves disease causes hyperthyroidism with a bruit, ophthalmopathy, and TSH-receptor antibodies—opposite of her hypothyroid picture. Subacute thyroiditis presents with a painful, tender gland after a viral illness, often with transient hyperthyroidism, not a painless firm goiter with positive anti-TPO. Iodine deficiency is uncommon in iodine-replete regions and would not produce high-titer anti-TPO antibodies.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With TSH 14.5 and low free T4 plus clear symptoms, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Observation alone with repeat labs in 1 year
Levothyroxine replacement titrated to normalize TSH, rechecking in about 6–8 weeks
Methimazole
Total thyroidectomy
stages.explanation: She has overt hypothyroidism (elevated TSH with low free T4) plus symptoms, so levothyroxine replacement is indicated, titrated to normalize TSH with rechecks roughly every 6–8 weeks after dose changes because of the drug's long half-life and TSH's lag. Observation alone would be appropriate only for mild subclinical disease without symptoms—not here. Methimazole treats hyperthyroidism and would be harmful in a hypothyroid patient. Thyroidectomy is reserved for compressive goiter, suspicion of malignancy, or cosmetic/airway issues, none present. Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, separated from calcium/iron, and counsel that replacement is typically lifelong.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If she had instead shown TSH 6.8 with a normal free T4 and minimal symptoms, what would be the best approach to subclinical hypothyroidism?
options: Always start full-dose levothyroxine immediately
Consider treatment selectively (e.g., TSH ≥10, symptoms, pregnancy/planning, positive anti-TPO with progression) and otherwise monitor
Order radioiodine ablation
Begin liothyronine (T3) monotherapy
stages.explanation: Subclinical hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, normal free T4) is managed by selective treatment: levothyroxine is generally favored when TSH is ≥10, when symptoms are significant, in pregnancy or those planning pregnancy, or with positive anti-TPO antibodies suggesting likely progression; otherwise periodic monitoring is reasonable, especially for mild elevations in older patients. Automatically treating everyone overtreats many who would remain stable or normalize. Radioiodine ablation is for hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism. T3 monotherapy is not standard; levothyroxine (T4) is the agent of choice. Her positive anti-TPO would lower the threshold to treat or to monitor closely for progression to overt disease.

pearls: Hashimoto thyroiditis: diffuse, firm, painless goiter with high-titer anti-TPO antibodies—the leading cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas.
Overt hypothyroidism (high TSH, low free T4) with symptoms warrants levothyroxine, rechecking TSH about 6–8 weeks after each dose change.
Treat subclinical hypothyroidism selectively: TSH ≥10, significant symptoms, pregnancy/planning, or positive anti-TPO; otherwise monitor.
Autoimmune thyroid disease clusters with other autoimmune conditions (vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, pernicious anemia).

cases.title: Irregular Periods with Acne and Excess Hair
cases.chiefComplaint: Infrequent periods, worsening acne, and unwanted facial hair.
cases.history: A 25-year-old woman presents with menstrual cycles every 45–90 days since menarche, worsening over the past 2 years, along with new coarse hair on her chin and upper lip and persistent acne. She is concerned because she and her partner are considering pregnancy and she worries about fertility. She has gained about 7 kg over 2 years. PMH: none. Medications: none; she takes no hormonal contraception. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works in retail, sedentary. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes; sister with irregular periods. Review of systems is positive for the menstrual irregularity, hirsutism, acne, and weight gain, and negative for galactorrhea, severe headaches or visual field changes, rapid virilization, deepening voice, heat/cold intolerance, palpitations, easy bruising, and purple striae. She reports no recent very rapid hair growth or clitoromegaly.
cases.exam: BMI is 31 kg/m². There is moderate facial acne and terminal hair on the chin, upper lip, and lower abdomen consistent with hirsutism. Velvety hyperpigmentation is present in the axillae and posterior neck (acanthosis nigricans). There is no galactorrhea on breast exam. There is no thyromegaly. The abdomen is soft without palpable masses. There is no clitoromegaly, no temporal balding, no moon facies, no supraclavicular fat pad, and no abdominal striae. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond confirming hyperandrogenism and ovulatory dysfunction, which initial labs best help exclude important mimics?
options: Only a total testosterone level
TSH, prolactin, and 17-hydroxyprogesterone (with total/free testosterone)
Random cortisol alone
No labs are needed; the diagnosis is purely clinical
stages.explanation: PCOS is a diagnosis of exclusion, so initial labs should rule out mimics: TSH (thyroid disease), prolactin (hyperprolactinemia causing oligomenorrhea), and 17-hydroxyprogesterone (nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia), along with total/free testosterone to document hyperandrogenism. Testosterone alone confirms androgen excess but misses the mimics that change management. Random cortisol alone is not a reliable Cushing screen and isn't routinely needed without specific signs (striae, moon facies, proximal weakness), which she lacks. Saying no labs are needed is wrong because biochemical screening excludes thyroid, prolactin, and CAH and helps satisfy Rotterdam criteria. A markedly elevated testosterone or rapid virilization would prompt evaluation for an androgen-secreting tumor.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Total testosterone: mildly elevated
Free testosterone: elevated
TSH: normal
Prolactin: normal
17-hydroxyprogesterone: normal
Fasting glucose 104 mg/dL (impaired)
Hemoglobin A1c 5.9%
Fasting lipids: low HDL, elevated triglycerides
Pelvic ultrasound: multiple small follicles in both ovaries (polycystic morphology)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Rotterdam criteria, how is the diagnosis established here?
options: Requires all of: hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, AND polycystic ovaries
Requires 2 of 3: hyperandrogenism (clinical/biochemical), oligo-/anovulation, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound, after excluding other causes
Requires only a polycystic-appearing ovary on ultrasound
Requires an elevated LH:FSH ratio as a mandatory criterion
stages.explanation: The Rotterdam criteria diagnose PCOS when at least 2 of 3 features are present—clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, oligo-/anovulation, and polycystic ovarian morphology—after excluding mimics (thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, nonclassic CAH, and, when indicated, Cushing/androgen-secreting tumor). She meets all three. Requiring all three is the stricter (older) framework, not Rotterdam. A polycystic-appearing ovary alone is insufficient and common in normal women. An elevated LH:FSH ratio is supportive but not a required diagnostic criterion. Documenting exclusion of mimics (normal TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP) is essential to confirm PCOS rather than a treatable alternative.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is not currently seeking pregnancy and wants help with irregular periods, acne, and hirsutism. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Combined oral contraceptive pills, with lifestyle/weight management
Clomiphene citrate now
Metformin as monotherapy for hirsutism
Spironolactone alone without contraception
stages.explanation: For a patient not seeking pregnancy, combined oral contraceptives are first-line: they regulate cycles, reduce androgen-driven acne and hirsutism, and protect the endometrium from unopposed estrogen, while lifestyle/weight management improves metabolic and reproductive outcomes. Clomiphene is an ovulation-induction agent reserved for when she desires pregnancy, not for symptom control now. Metformin helps insulin resistance and may aid cycles but is not the most effective monotherapy for hirsutism. Spironolactone is an effective anti-androgen but is teratogenic and should be paired with reliable contraception in a woman of reproductive potential—so it isn't appropriate alone. COCs plus lifestyle change address her current goals and her metabolic risk.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given her impaired fasting glucose, A1c 5.9%, and dyslipidemia, what is the most important ongoing management consideration?
options: No metabolic follow-up is needed in someone this young
Screen and manage cardiometabolic risk—obesity, prediabetes/type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidemia—with lifestyle intervention and periodic monitoring
Start insulin immediately
Order yearly endometrial biopsy regardless of bleeding
stages.explanation: PCOS carries elevated cardiometabolic risk, and she already shows prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose, A1c 5.9%) and dyslipidemia, so ongoing screening and management—weight/lifestyle intervention, periodic glucose/lipid monitoring, and consideration of metformin for the metabolic component—are essential to reduce progression to type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Ignoring metabolic risk because she is young misses a key long-term harm. Starting insulin immediately is not warranted for prediabetes; lifestyle (and possibly metformin) come first. Routine yearly endometrial biopsy irrespective of bleeding is not indicated—biopsy is prompted by prolonged amenorrhea or abnormal bleeding given the endometrial cancer risk from chronic anovulation, which cyclic progestin/COC therapy mitigates.

pearls: Rotterdam criteria: 2 of 3—hyperandrogenism, oligo-/anovulation, polycystic ovaries—after excluding thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, and nonclassic CAH.
Combined oral contraceptives plus lifestyle change are first-line when pregnancy is not desired; clomiphene/letrozole are for ovulation induction when fertility is the goal.
Spironolactone is an effective anti-androgen but is teratogenic and must be paired with reliable contraception.
PCOS is a cardiometabolic disorder—screen for and manage obesity, prediabetes/diabetes, and dyslipidemia, and protect the endometrium from unopposed estrogen.

cases.title: New Difficulty with Erections in a Middle-Aged Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of trouble achieving and maintaining erections.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with 6 months of progressively worsening difficulty achieving and maintaining erections, affecting both partnered and morning erections. He is embarrassed but motivated to address it. He reports mild exertional fatigue when climbing stairs but no chest pain. PMH: hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, hyperlipidemia not on therapy, and prediabetes. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide; no nitrates. Allergies: none. Social: 20-pack-year smoking history (still smokes), moderate alcohol, sedentary office job, under work stress; reports low mood at times but no anhedonia. Family history: father had a myocardial infarction at 52; mother with type 2 diabetes. Review of systems is positive for the erectile difficulty and mild exertional fatigue and negative for libido loss, testicular pain, hematuria, claudication at rest, syncope, and overt depressive symptoms. He has not tried any ED medications.
cases.exam: The patient is overweight (BMI 29 kg/m²) and appears well. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm with an S4 but no murmur; lungs are clear. There are no carotid bruits. Peripheral pulses are intact but the dorsalis pedis pulses are slightly diminished bilaterally. Genitourinary exam shows normal circumcised penis without plaques (no Peyronie), normal-sized testes without masses, and no inguinal hernia. There is no gynecomastia and no visual field defect. Neurologic exam including lower-extremity sensation is grossly intact. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond addressing the erectile complaint itself, what is the most important clinical implication of new-onset erectile dysfunction in this man?
options: It is almost always purely psychological at his age
It can be an early marker of systemic endothelial/cardiovascular disease, warranting cardiovascular risk assessment
It indicates prostate cancer until proven otherwise
It is an expected, untreatable consequence of aging
stages.explanation: Erectile dysfunction frequently signals systemic vascular endothelial dysfunction; the small penile arteries are affected early, so new ED can precede coronary events by years and should prompt cardiovascular risk assessment—especially in a smoker with hypertension, dyslipidemia, prediabetes, an S4, diminished pedal pulses, and a strong family history. Calling it purely psychological is wrong given his vascular risk profile and loss of morning erections (which suggests an organic component). ED is not a marker of prostate cancer. Framing it as untreatable aging is both inaccurate and dismissive—ED is treatable and is a modifiable-risk warning sign. Recognizing ED as a cardiovascular harbinger drives lipid, glucose, BP, and lifestyle optimization.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Fasting glucose 116 mg/dL
Hemoglobin A1c 6.2%
Total cholesterol 245 mg/dL, LDL 165 mg/dL, HDL 36 mg/dL
Morning total testosterone: low-normal
TSH: normal
Resting ECG: normal sinus rhythm with nonspecific changes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which initial management plan best addresses both his erectile dysfunction and his underlying risk?
options: Immediate penile prosthesis referral
Lifestyle modification and cardiovascular risk reduction (smoking cessation, exercise, statin, BP/glucose control) plus consideration of a PDE5 inhibitor
Testosterone replacement as first-line because his level is low-normal
Reassurance with no intervention
stages.explanation: The best plan combines aggressive risk-factor modification—smoking cessation, weight loss/exercise, initiating a statin for his high LDL and family history, and optimizing BP and glucose—with consideration of a PDE5 inhibitor (e.g., sildenafil, tadalafil) for symptomatic relief. This treats the symptom and the shared vascular pathophysiology. A penile prosthesis is a last-line option after medical therapy fails. Testosterone replacement is not first-line when testosterone is only low-normal and he has no clear hypogonadal syndrome; it would not address his vascular ED and carries its own risks. Reassurance alone ignores both treatable ED and his elevated cardiovascular risk. Reviewing contributory drugs (his thiazide) is also reasonable.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before prescribing a PDE5 inhibitor, which medication interaction is most important to screen for and counsel about?
options: Concurrent nitrate use (including as-needed nitroglycerin), which can cause severe hypotension
Concurrent acetaminophen use
Concurrent metformin use
Concurrent inhaled corticosteroid use
stages.explanation: PDE5 inhibitors and nitrates together can cause profound, life-threatening hypotension because both increase cGMP-mediated vasodilation, so concurrent nitrate use—including as-needed sublingual nitroglycerin—is an absolute contraindication, and patients must be counseled never to take nitrates within the drug's contraindicated window. Given his cardiovascular risk, he may be prescribed nitrates in the future, so this is critical to discuss. Acetaminophen, metformin, and inhaled corticosteroids have no comparable dangerous interaction with PDE5 inhibitors. Caution is also warranted with alpha-blockers (additive hypotension). Screening for nitrate use is the single most important safety step before prescribing.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He reports the exertional fatigue is new and limits him at two flights of stairs. What is the most appropriate next step regarding cardiovascular evaluation?
options: Ignore it since the resting ECG is normal
Assess functional/cardiac risk and pursue further evaluation (e.g., stress testing) for possible coronary disease before clearing vigorous sexual activity
Start a PDE5 inhibitor immediately regardless of his symptoms
Refer directly for coronary artery bypass grafting
stages.explanation: New exertional limitation in a high-risk man warrants cardiac risk stratification—including consideration of stress testing—to evaluate for coronary artery disease before clearing him for the exertion of sexual activity, since intercourse imposes a cardiovascular load. A normal resting ECG does not exclude significant coronary disease, so ignoring symptoms is unsafe. Starting a PDE5 inhibitor immediately without assessing his cardiac status risks precipitating ischemia during activity and skips needed evaluation. Referring directly for bypass surgery is premature without any ischemic evaluation or angiography. Confirming he can safely tolerate exertion is the responsible sequence; this also reinforces that his ED was an early cardiovascular flag.

cases.diagnosis: Erectile dysfunction as a marker of cardiovascular disease
pearls: New organic erectile dysfunction (including loss of morning erections) is an early marker of systemic endothelial/vascular disease and can precede coronary events—use it to trigger cardiovascular risk assessment.
First-line management combines risk-factor modification (smoking cessation, exercise, statin, BP/glucose control) with a PDE5 inhibitor.
PDE5 inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated with nitrates (including as-needed nitroglycerin) due to life-threatening hypotension; counsel every patient.
Assess exertional cardiac tolerance before clearing high-risk men for sexual activity—a normal resting ECG does not exclude coronary disease.

cases.title: Painful Ear in a Summer Swimmer
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of right ear pain, itching, and discharge worsening with touch.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents with 3 days of right ear pain, itching, and a sense of fullness, with some clear-to-cloudy drainage. The pain worsens sharply when he tugs on the ear or chews. He swims daily at the community pool this summer on a recreational team. He denies fever, vertigo, hearing loss aside from muffling, and no prior ear surgery. PMH: none; he is not diabetic. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, frequent swimmer, uses cotton swabs to clean his ears. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for ear pain, pruritus, and otorrhea and negative for fever, severe headache, facial weakness, neck stiffness, deep boring nighttime ear pain, and granulation symptoms. He has had no recent upper respiratory infection and no toothache.
cases.exam: There is marked tenderness on manipulation of the tragus and pinna of the right ear. The external auditory canal is erythematous, edematous, and narrowed, with whitish-to-cloudy debris and discharge. Visualization of the tympanic membrane is partially obscured by canal swelling, but the visible portion appears intact and mobile without bulging or perforation. There is no mastoid tenderness or postauricular swelling, no facial nerve weakness, and no granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction. The contralateral ear is normal. No cervical lymphadenopathy of concern.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding most strongly supports otitis externa rather than acute otitis media?
options: A bulging, immobile tympanic membrane
Pain on manipulation of the tragus/pinna with an erythematous, edematous external canal
Fever above 39 °C with rhinorrhea
A retracted tympanic membrane with an air-fluid level
stages.explanation: Pain on tragal/pinna manipulation with a swollen, erythematous, debris-filled external auditory canal is the hallmark of otitis externa, reflecting inflammation of the canal itself. A bulging, immobile tympanic membrane and a retracted TM with an air-fluid level indicate middle-ear pathology (acute otitis media or effusion), not canal disease; manipulating the auricle does not typically hurt in AOM. High fever with rhinorrhea suggests a systemic viral/URI process and is not specific to otitis externa, which is usually afebrile and localized. His swimming ("swimmer's ear") and cotton-swab trauma are classic risk factors that macerate and abrade the canal, predisposing to infection.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which organisms most commonly cause acute otitis externa?
options: Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus
Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae
Epstein-Barr virus
Mycobacterium tuberculosis
stages.explanation: Acute otitis externa is most commonly caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus, organisms that thrive in the warm, moist, macerated canal of a frequent swimmer. Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae are typical pathogens of acute otitis media (middle ear), not the external canal. EBV causes mononucleosis, not otitis externa. Mycobacterium tuberculosis is not a typical cause. Knowing the Pseudomonas/Staph profile directs appropriate topical antimicrobial selection. Fungal otitis externa (e.g., Aspergillus) can occur, particularly after antibiotic drops or in chronic cases, but bacterial causes dominate acute presentations.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for uncomplicated acute otitis externa?
options: Oral amoxicillin
Oral fluconazole
Systemic IV antipseudomonal antibiotics
Topical antibiotic ear drops (e.g., a fluoroquinolone, often with a corticosteroid), with canal cleaning and water precautions
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated acute otitis externa is treated with topical antibiotic ear drops—commonly a fluoroquinolone, often combined with a corticosteroid to reduce canal inflammation—along with gentle cleaning/aural toilet to remove debris and strict water precautions to keep the canal dry. Oral amoxicillin does not cover Pseudomonas and penetrates the canal poorly, so it is not first-line for localized OE. Oral fluconazole targets fungal disease, which is not the typical acute bacterial cause. Systemic IV antipseudomonal therapy is reserved for malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa—suspected in diabetics or the immunocompromised with severe deep pain and granulation tissue—which this healthy patient lacks. If the TM is perforated, choose a non-ototoxic drop.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
